title: Medtronic Valleylab Force FX – Return Electrode (Pad) Alarm / Won’t Enable Cut or Coag
description: Troubleshooting guide for Valleylab Force FX ESU return electrode alarms preventing Cut or Coag activation, focusing on external causes before internal REM circuit failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the ESU is connected to an active patient. Confirm the patient has been transitioned to another ESU if surgery is ongoing. Verify the generator is in standby mode before making adjustments. This prevents unintended activation and protects the patient.

steps.title: Verify Return Electrode Is Properly Applied
steps.instructions: Confirm the pad is fully adhered to clean, dry, hair-free skin. Check for partial lifting, wrinkles, or air pockets. Ensure correct pad placement over a large, well-vascularized muscle mass. Poor pad contact is the most common cause of REM alarms. If the pad appears compromised, replace it with a new one.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Pad Type
steps.instructions: Verify that a split (REM-compatible) return electrode is being used. Ensure it is compatible with the Valleylab Force FX REM system. Using a solid (non-split) pad or incompatible pad will trigger alarms and prevent activation.

steps.title: Inspect the Return Electrode Cable
steps.instructions: Check for bent pins, loose connectors, or damaged insulation. Ensure the connector is fully seated in the return electrode port. Wiggle the connector gently to see if the alarm clears or intermittently changes. If damage is noted, replace the cable and retest.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good Cable
steps.instructions: Disconnect the current return electrode cable. Connect a known-good cable. Attach to a new pad (not the existing one). If the alarm clears, the issue was likely cable-related.

steps.title: Check for Fluid Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the return electrode port for saline, prep solution, or debris. Ensure the connector is dry and free of contamination. Moisture can interfere with REM sensing and cause persistent alarms.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off. Wait 30–60 seconds. Restart and observe whether the alarm persists. Transient REM circuit faults may clear with a reset.

steps.title: Perform Functional Test (Without Patient)
steps.instructions: Using a test pad or simulator (if available), connect the return electrode. Verify the REM indicator shows normal status. Attempt activation into an ESU analyzer. If the alarm persists with known-good accessories and no patient involved, the issue is likely internal.

CCR.Complaint: OR staff reported return electrode alarm on Valleylab Force FX. Unit would not enable Cut or Coag during setup.
CCR.Cause: Return electrode cable found to have intermittent connection at generator plug. Alarm triggered when cable was manipulated.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced return electrode cable with known-good cable. Verified REM normal indication and successful activation into ESU analyzer. Returned unit to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab Force FX - Electrosurgical Unit – No Output or Weak Output
description: Troubleshooting guide for Valleylab Force FX ESU producing no or weak output in Cut or Coag modes, focusing on external causes before internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the ESU is not connected to an active patient. If output is in question during a case, have clinical staff transition to a backup generator immediately before troubleshooting. This prevents unintended injury or surgical delay.

steps.title: Verify Power to the Unit
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is plugged into a known-good outlet. Check for proper power-on indicators. If connected to a power strip or boom outlet, verify that the outlet is energized. Why this matters: Inconsistent power can allow the unit to power on but not deliver proper output under load.

steps.title: Confirm Output Mode and Settings
steps.instructions: Verify correct mode (Cut, Coag, Blend). Confirm wattage setting is appropriate for the intended procedure. Ensure the unit is not in a standby or default low-power setting. Incorrect settings can mimic weak output.

steps.title: Inspect the Active Electrode Pencil
steps.instructions: Check the handpiece for damage or contamination. Ensure the electrode tip is fully seated. Replace the pencil with a known-good pencil if available. Damaged or high-resistance handpieces commonly cause weak output.

steps.title: Check the Footswitch (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Verify the footswitch cable is fully seated. Test both pedals (Cut and Coag). Try activating using the hand control pencil if available. A failed footswitch or broken cable can prevent output even when the unit appears normal.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Return Electrode (Grounding Pad)
steps.instructions: Confirm proper placement and full adhesion. Check that the return electrode cable is fully inserted. Inspect for pad drying, lifting edges, or expired packaging. Try a new return electrode if unsure. Poor return contact increases impedance and may reduce or prevent output.

steps.title: Check for REM (Return Electrode Monitoring) Alarms
steps.instructions: Look for REM alarm indicators or error messages. Confirm the correct pad type (split vs. solid) is being used. REM detection may disable output if improper pad connection is detected.

steps.title: Inspect Output Receptacles
steps.instructions: Ensure the active output port is secure. Check for bent pins or visible damage. Confirm the correct output port is being used. Connection issues at the generator output port can reduce energy delivery.

steps.title: Swap Accessories Systematically
steps.instructions: Replace the active electrode. Replace the return electrode and cable. Replace the footswitch. Try another known-good ESU on the same accessories (if available). Systematic isolation prevents unnecessary generator removal.

steps.title: Check for Error Codes or Self-Test Failures
steps.instructions: Power cycle the unit. Observe startup self-test. Document any displayed error codes. Failure during internal diagnostics suggests internal generator issues.

CCR.Complaint: OR reports no cut effect during procedure despite activation.
CCR.Cause: High-resistance active electrode pencil identified; output normal after accessory swap.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced active electrode pencil. Verified proper output in Cut and Coag modes using ESU analyzer. Returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (Returned to service / Sent to vendor)

title: Medtronic Valleylab Force FX - Error Codes / Self-Test Failure at Power-Up
description: Troubleshooting guide for Valleylab Force FX ESU self-test failures and startup error codes, focusing on external power and accessory checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure the Unit Is Not Connected to a Patient
steps.instructions: If connected to an active case, immediately transfer the patient to a backup ESU before troubleshooting. Do not power-cycle repeatedly during an active surgical procedure.

steps.title: Perform a Full Power Reset
steps.instructions: Turn the unit OFF. Disconnect the power cord from the wall outlet. Wait at least 60 seconds. Reconnect directly to a known-good wall outlet (not a power strip). Power ON and observe the startup sequence. Why this matters: Temporary logic faults or voltage instability can trigger self-test failures.

steps.title: Verify the Wall Outlet and Line Power
steps.instructions: Confirm the outlet is hospital-grade and functioning. Test outlet with an outlet tester or plug in another known-good device. Avoid extension cords or daisy-chained power strips. Power irregularities commonly trigger startup errors.

steps.title: Disconnect All Accessories
steps.instructions: Remove active electrode handpiece. Disconnect return electrode (REM) cable. Remove footswitch. Remove any adapters or extension cables. Power the unit ON with nothing connected. Why this matters: Faulty accessories can cause impedance or detection errors during self-test.

steps.title: Inspect the Return Electrode (REM) Port
steps.instructions: Check for bent pins. Check for debris or fluid contamination. Ensure no foreign objects are inside the port. REM detection failures are a common cause of startup errors.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Fluid Exposure Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect for signs of fluid ingress. Look for corrosion around connectors. Note any unusual odor (burning smell). Check for excessive heat from the chassis. If fluid intrusion is suspected, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

steps.title: Document the Exact Error Code Displayed
steps.instructions: Record the exact code and message. Note whether the unit fails immediately or midway through self-test. Note any audible tones or alarm patterns. Precise documentation helps determine whether the issue is power supply–related, memory-related, or internal board-related.

steps.title: Attempt Startup on a Different Circuit (If Available)
steps.instructions: Move the unit to a separate, known-good circuit in another room if feasible. If the error clears, building power instability may be contributing.

CCR.Complaint: Unit displays error code during startup and will not enter ready mode.
CCR.Cause: Unit failed internal self-test at power-up. Error persisted after full power reset and accessory removal. No external power issues identified.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to Biomed shop for further evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual odor, sound, or heat

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 - Instrument Recognition Errors or Incomplete Seals
description: Troubleshoot Valleylab FT10 ESU when instruments are not recognized or tissue seals are incomplete, focusing on external checks first.

steps.title: Verify Power and Unit Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the ESU is properly plugged into a verified, live outlet. Confirm the unit powers on and completes its self-test. Why: Some recognition errors occur if the unit has insufficient or unstable power.

steps.title: Check Instrument Type and Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm the instrument is compatible with the FT10 system. Check that instrument connectors are clean, intact, and fully seated. Why: Partial insertion or dirty connectors can prevent recognition and reduce seal quality.

steps.title: Inspect Cable and Connector Condition
steps.instructions: Examine patient and return electrodes, cables, and handpiece connectors for cuts, kinks, or visible wear. Replace any damaged cables with known working units. Why: Damaged cables can cause incomplete current flow, leading to incomplete seals.

steps.title: Verify Instrument Settings on the ESU
steps.instructions: Confirm correct mode (e.g., cut, coagulation, vessel sealing) and power settings. Ensure instrument-specific settings match the instrument in use. Why: Incorrect modes or mismatched settings can prevent proper instrument recognition or reduce seal efficacy.

steps.title: Test with a Known Good Instrument
steps.instructions: Swap the instrument with one verified to be functioning correctly. Observe whether the ESU recognizes it and performs complete seals. Why: Confirms whether the issue is instrument-specific or unit-specific.

steps.title: Check Software/Instrument Firmware Updates
steps.instructions: Review unit logs for error codes related to instrument recognition. Verify that the ESU firmware is up to date per manufacturer guidance. Why: Older firmware may fail to recognize newer instruments correctly.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Self-Test / Calibration
steps.instructions: Follow FT10 prompts for instrument detection and calibration (without opening the unit). Why: Some recognition issues resolve after self-test recalibration.

CCR.Complaint: FT10 ESU displays instrument recognition errors; vessel seals incomplete.
CCR.Cause: Instrument connector partially seated; handpiece cable shows minor wear. Settings confirmed correct. Known good instrument tests show same error.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced worn cable and reseated connector. Unit passed self-test with known good instruments. If unresolved, unit removed from service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (functional, removed, or pending repair)

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 - Touchscreen UI Troubleshooting
description: Guide for troubleshooting touchscreen freezing, lag, or calibration issues on a Valleylab FT10 ESU

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the ESU is connected to a verified working outlet. Check the power cord and plug for damage. Why: Inconsistent power can cause UI lag or freezing.

steps.title: Perform a Full Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn the ESU off, wait 30–60 seconds, and power it back on. Why: This can clear temporary software or memory glitches causing touchscreen lag.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions on the Touchscreen
steps.instructions: Clean the screen with a soft, lint-free cloth and approved disinfectant. Ensure no protective covers or moisture interfere with touch sensitivity. Why: Dirt, liquids, or protective films can cause miscalibration or unresponsiveness.

steps.title: Inspect User Settings
steps.instructions: Check that touchscreen sensitivity settings are at recommended factory levels. Ensure no locked functions or software restrictions are active. Why: Misconfigured settings can mimic calibration issues.

steps.title: Disconnect and Reconnect Peripherals
steps.instructions: Remove instruments, footswitch, or other connected devices. Reboot the system with only essential connections. Why: External devices can interfere with UI performance.

steps.title: Observe Software Behavior
steps.instructions: Run a basic test procedure to see if UI freezing or lag occurs consistently. Note if lag is during specific menu navigation or during energy delivery. Why: Helps differentiate between software glitches and hardware failure.

steps.title: Check for Software Updates or Error Logs
steps.instructions: Verify if the ESU firmware is up to date according to Medtronic recommendations. Review any system error logs for touchscreen or calibration faults. Why: Known software bugs may cause freezing or calibration drift.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen freezes and lags during use; calibration seems off.
CCR.Cause: Cleaning and inspection performed; power cycle and peripheral check did not resolve issue; external connections verified.
CCR.Resolution: Device labeled Out of Service and sent for Medtronic service evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Device left powered off in Out of Service status

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 - Energy Delivery Interruptions or Output Faults
description: Guide for troubleshooting energy delivery interruptions or output faults on a Valleylab FT10 ESU, focusing on external checks before internal repair.

steps.title: Verify Power Supply
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit is plugged into a properly grounded outlet. Confirm the circuit is live and not overloaded. Check for tripped breakers or GFCI interruptions.

steps.title: Inspect Electrical Cords and Connections
steps.instructions: Examine the ESU power cord for frays, kinks, or damage. Check instrument cables, patient return electrode (grounding pad) cables, and accessory connections.

steps.title: Check Patient Return Electrode (Grounding Pad) Placement and Contact
steps.instructions: Ensure proper skin contact and pad adhesion. Replace the pad if adhesive is insufficient or if pad is nearing expiration.

steps.title: Verify Instrument Compatibility and Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm that the active electrode and instruments are compatible with FT10 outputs. Check that connectors are fully seated.

steps.title: Review Settings and Output Modes
steps.instructions: Confirm the selected energy mode and power settings are appropriate for the procedure. Ensure no locked modes or software prompts are preventing output.

steps.title: Perform Basic Self-Test / Calibration
steps.instructions: Run the ESU’s built-in self-test if available. Observe any fault codes or error messages.

steps.title: Observe Unit During Operation in a Safe Environment
steps.instructions: Test with a suitable load simulator (if allowed) instead of a patient. Note if interruptions recur or if fault codes are displayed.

CCR.Complaint: User reports intermittent energy delivery during cauterization, causing procedure delays.
CCR.Cause: Patient return electrode had poor contact; all cords verified, settings correct.
CCR.Resolution: Reapplied grounding pad with proper adhesion; tested ESU with load simulator—unit functioning normally.

helpfulDetails: Cables inspected and swapped if needed
Final device status: cleared or removed for repair

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 - Footswitch Communication Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting Valleylab FT10 ESU footswitch failures, including unresponsive, intermittent, or unrecognized pedals.

steps.title: Verify Footswitch Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the footswitch cable is fully seated in the ESU footswitch port. Loose or partially connected cables are a common cause of communication errors.

steps.title: Inspect Footswitch and Cable
steps.instructions: Check the footswitch for visible damage (cracks, frayed wires, bent pins). Flex the cable gently while observing if the ESU registers any input. A damaged footswitch or cable often causes intermittent or failed communication.

steps.title: Swap Footswitch (if available)
steps.instructions: If a spare, compatible footswitch is available, connect it to the ESU. If the replacement works, the original footswitch is likely faulty.

steps.title: Check ESU Settings
steps.instructions: Navigate to the ESU’s accessory or footswitch menu. Confirm that the ESU is configured to the correct footswitch type (single pedal vs dual pedal). Misconfigured settings may prevent proper recognition.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn off the ESU, wait 10–15 seconds, then power it back on. Some communication faults resolve after a reset.

steps.title: Observe Footswitch Response
steps.instructions: Press the pedal(s) and check the ESU display for acknowledgment. Listen for any relays or clicks if applicable. Document any erratic or non-responsive behavior.

CCR.Complaint: User reports that the ESU footswitch is unresponsive and energy cannot be triggered.
CCR.Cause: Footswitch cable fully seated, inspected for damage, swapped with spare — still unresponsive; settings verified.
CCR.Resolution: Unit labeled Out of Service and sent to vendor/repair bench for evaluation.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 - Internal Cooling System or Fan Faults Causing Shutdown
description: Guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot Valleylab FT10 ESU unexpected shutdowns due to suspected internal cooling system or fan faults.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the ESU is plugged into a properly grounded and live outlet. Use a known-good outlet and test power with a voltmeter if available. Why: Power fluctuations can mimic fan or cooling faults.

steps.title: Check Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit has at least 6–12 inches of clearance around vents. Verify the room temperature is within operating specifications (typically 18–30°C). Why: Overheating can trigger automatic shutdowns.

steps.title: Inspect External Vents and Filters
steps.instructions: Look for blockages such as drapes, dust, or debris at intake or exhaust vents. Remove dust with low-pressure air if needed. Why: Restricted airflow can reduce cooling efficiency and trigger shutdown.

steps.title: Observe Fan Operation During Startup
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and listen/observe the cooling fan. Confirm it spins smoothly without unusual noise, vibration, or stuttering. Why: Fan failure or obstruction can cause overheating faults.

steps.title: Check Error Codes / Alarms
steps.instructions: Review the ESU display for any cooling system warnings or error messages. Document any codes for repair tracking and service escalation. Why: The system often logs specific faults that guide repair.

steps.title: Confirm Energy Output Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure settings are within normal ranges for the procedure. Excessive high-power continuous use can trigger thermal shutdown. Why: Protects unit and patient; not a mechanical failure if settings are too high.

CCR.Complaint: Unit shuts down during procedures; fan may be faulty.
CCR.Cause: External checks show vents are clear, power is stable, and environmental conditions are normal. Fan spins but device still shuts down.
CCR.Resolution: ESU removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to vendor/biomedical bench for internal cooling system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Fan operation observed (normal or abnormal noise)
Final device status: Out of Service

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM100N – SpO₂ Sensor Not Detected or Intermittent Readings
description: Troubleshooting guide for resolving SpO₂ sensor detection failures or intermittent readings on the Nellcor PM100N pulse oximeter.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot on an active patient. Ensure the patient is moved to a functioning monitor before proceeding.

steps.title: Verify the sensor is properly connected
steps.instructions: Ensure the SpO₂ sensor is fully seated in the monitor’s sensor port. A loose or partially inserted connector is a common cause of “sensor not detected” errors.

steps.title: Inspect the sensor cable and connector
steps.instructions: Check for visible damage such as fraying, bent pins, or cracked connectors. Damaged sensors or cables can cause intermittent readings or failure to detect.

steps.title: Try a known-good sensor
steps.instructions: Swap the sensor with a confirmed working SpO₂ sensor. This helps quickly determine if the issue is with the sensor or the monitor.

steps.title: Check compatibility of the sensor
steps.instructions: Confirm that the sensor being used is compatible with the Nellcor PM100N. Using incorrect or non-approved sensors can result in detection errors.

steps.title: Inspect the sensor port on the monitor
steps.instructions: Look for debris, bent pins, or damage inside the port. A contaminated or damaged port can interrupt communication with the sensor.

steps.title: Ensure proper sensor placement on the patient (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Verify correct placement (finger, toe, etc.) with adequate perfusion. Poor placement or low perfusion can cause intermittent or inaccurate readings.

steps.title: Check for motion artifact or environmental interference
steps.instructions: Excessive patient movement, vibration, or strong ambient light (e.g., surgical lights) can disrupt signal detection and cause unstable readings.

steps.title: Confirm adequate patient perfusion
steps.instructions: Cold extremities, hypotension, or vasoconstriction can lead to weak signals. Try repositioning the sensor to a better-perfused site.

steps.title: Power cycle the device
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off, wait a few seconds, and power it back on. This can clear temporary communication faults.

steps.title: Check for error messages or alarm indicators
steps.instructions: Note any specific alarms or messages related to sensor failure, as they may guide further action or indicate internal faults.

CCR.Complaint: Unit not detecting SpO₂ sensor or showing intermittent readings during use.
CCR.Cause: Failed SpO₂ sensor cable identified during troubleshooting (issue resolved when swapped with known-good sensor).
CCR.Resolution: Replaced faulty SpO₂ sensor and verified proper operation.

helpfulDetails: Port inspected for debris or damage
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM100N – Poor Signal Quality or Frequent Artifact Alarms
description: Troubleshooting poor signal quality and frequent artifact alarms on the Nellcor PM100N pulse oximeter.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is on a functioning monitoring device before troubleshooting. Do not troubleshoot while the device is actively in use on a patient.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the SpO₂ sensor is fully seated in the monitor. Check for loose or intermittent connections. A poor connection can cause signal dropouts and artifact alarms.

steps.title: Inspect the Sensor Condition
steps.instructions: Check for damaged, worn, or contaminated sensors. Look for cracked housings, frayed cables, or dirty optical surfaces. Replace the sensor if any defects are found.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Sensor Type
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct Nellcor-compatible sensor is being used. Incompatible or non-approved sensors may produce unreliable readings.

steps.title: Check Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Ensure the sensor is applied correctly (finger, toe, or ear depending on type). Confirm proper alignment of emitter and detector. Poor placement is one of the most common causes of artifact alarms.

steps.title: Assess Patient Movement
steps.instructions: Determine if the patient is moving excessively, shivering, or restless. Motion artifact is a frequent cause of poor signal quality. Test the unit on a stationary subject or simulator if possible.

steps.title: Evaluate Perfusion at Site
steps.instructions: Check for low perfusion conditions such as cold extremities or poor circulation. Try a different site if perfusion is questionable. Low perfusion reduces signal strength and stability.

steps.title: Inspect Extension Cables (if used)
steps.instructions: Remove any extension cable and connect the sensor directly to the monitor. Swap cables if available to rule out cable failure.

steps.title: Check for Ambient Light Interference
steps.instructions: Ensure the sensor site is not exposed to bright light sources such as surgical lights or sunlight. Cover the sensor if needed to reduce interference.

steps.title: Review Alarm Settings
steps.instructions: Verify alarm thresholds and sensitivity settings are appropriate. Overly sensitive settings may trigger frequent artifact alarms.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Device
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off and back on. This can clear temporary processing or signal acquisition issues.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Sensor
steps.instructions: Use a verified working sensor to determine if the issue follows the sensor or the device. This helps isolate accessory versus device-related problems.

CCR.Complaint: Unit displaying frequent artifact alarms and unstable SpO₂ readings during use.
CCR.Cause: Worn SpO₂ sensor cable with intermittent connection causing signal dropouts.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced defective sensor with known good unit. Verified stable readings and no further alarms.

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM100N – Battery Not Charging or Reduced Runtime
description: Guide for troubleshooting battery charging issues and reduced runtime on the Nellcor PM100N pulse oximeter.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the outlet is functional using a known working device. Check if the unit powers on with AC connected. Why: A faulty outlet or no AC input will prevent battery charging.

steps.title: Inspect Power Adapter and Cord
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct Medtronic power adapter is being used. Check for frayed cables, bent connectors, or loose connections. Reseat both ends of the power cord firmly. Why: Damaged or incompatible adapters can prevent proper charging.

steps.title: Check Charging Indicator
steps.instructions: Observe the battery or charging indicator on the device. Confirm whether it shows charging status when plugged in. Why: Helps determine if the device is recognizing external power.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Device
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, disconnect AC power, wait 30 seconds, then reconnect and power on. Why: Resets minor firmware or charging logic issues.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Power Supply (if available)
steps.instructions: Swap with a confirmed working PM100N power adapter. Why: Rules out power supply failure without opening the device.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Seating (if externally accessible)
steps.instructions: If the battery compartment is accessible, ensure the battery is properly seated. Look for signs of corrosion, damage, or swelling. Why: Poor contact or battery degradation can prevent charging.

steps.title: Evaluate Runtime
steps.instructions: Fully charge the device (if possible), then run it on battery power. Compare runtime to expected performance. Why: Determines if the battery is holding a charge or has degraded.

steps.title: Check for Heat or Unusual Behavior
steps.instructions: Monitor for excessive heat near the battery area during charging. Note any unusual smells or warning messages. Why: Indicates potential battery failure or internal charging issues.

CCR.Complaint: Unit reported not charging and shutting off quickly when unplugged.
CCR.Cause: Battery would not hold charge; device powered on with AC but lost power immediately when unplugged. Verified with known good power adapter.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for battery replacement and further evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Any heat, odor, or abnormal behavior
Final device status (removed from service or returned to use)

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM100N – Display or Keypad/Button Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting display or keypad/button failures on the Nellcor PM100N pulse oximeter.

steps.title: Ensure the device is powered and charging properly
steps.instructions: Check that the battery is sufficiently charged or connected to AC power. Verify that the charger and cable are functioning. Reason: A dead or low battery can cause the display to not illuminate or buttons to be unresponsive.

steps.title: Inspect for obvious damage or contamination
steps.instructions: Check the keypad, buttons, and display for visible damage, debris, or moisture. Clean with manufacturer-approved wipes. Reason: Dirt or liquid can block button contact or interfere with touchscreen responsiveness.

steps.title: Perform a soft reset
steps.instructions: Power cycle the device using the on/off button or battery removal if needed. Reason: Temporary software glitches can cause unresponsive displays or buttons.

steps.title: Verify cable and sensor connections
steps.instructions: Disconnect and reconnect SpO₂ sensors and any accessory cables. Reason: Some devices disable the display or keypad if sensors are improperly connected or malfunctioning.

steps.title: Check for error messages or indicators
steps.instructions: Observe any LED or audible indicators that may signal device faults. Reason: Certain internal faults are flagged by lights or alarms, providing clues for further action.

steps.title: Test keypad/buttons systematically
steps.instructions: Press each button individually, noting which are unresponsive. If touchscreen, gently swipe across the entire screen. Reason: Isolating the failure helps determine if it is localized or device-wide.

CCR.Complaint: User reports display is blank and keypad unresponsive.
CCR.Cause: Device was connected to AC power, no visible damage, buttons cleaned, soft reset attempted; still unresponsive.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to repair.

helpfulDetails: Specific buttons or areas of touchscreen unresponsive
Device status LEDs or alarm behavior

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM100N – Power Supply or Intermittent Shutdown Issues
description: Guide to troubleshoot power supply problems or intermittent shutdowns on the Nellcor PM100N Pulse Oximeter.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the wall outlet is providing stable power. Use a known working outlet or test with a meter. Why: Fluctuating or low voltage can cause intermittent shutdowns.

steps.title: Check Power Cord and AC Adapter
steps.instructions: Inspect for damage, fraying, or loose connectors. Reseat both ends securely. Why: Poor connections can interrupt power supply even if the outlet is fine.

steps.title: Test Device on Battery Power Alone
steps.instructions: Fully charge the battery and run the unit without AC power. Observe for normal operation duration. Why: Confirms whether the battery can hold charge or is causing shutdowns.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts and Compartment
steps.instructions: Remove the battery and check for corrosion or debris. Reseat or clean contacts as needed. Why: Poor contact can mimic power supply failures.

steps.title: Monitor for Overheating or Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is not in direct sunlight, near heaters, or in excessive humidity. Why: Thermal protection may cause shutdowns if overheating is detected.

steps.title: Check for Software or Firmware Alerts
steps.instructions: Observe display for error codes or messages during shutdown. Document any patterns (e.g., after warm-up, during charging). Why: Some shutdowns are device-protection responses.

CCR.Complaint: Device shuts down intermittently and sometimes won’t power on.
CCR.Cause: Battery tested; AC power stable; cord and contacts checked. No external cause found.
CCR.Resolution: Device labeled Out of Service and sent for repair. Battery replacement recommended if internal test confirms degradation.

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM1000N - SpO₂ Sensor Not Detected or Intermittent Readings
description: Guide for troubleshooting SpO₂ sensor detection issues and intermittent readings on the Nellcor PM1000N.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the SpO₂ sensor is fully seated in the device connector. Check for loose, bent, or damaged pins. Why: Poor physical connection often causes detection errors.

steps.title: Inspect Sensor and Cable Condition
steps.instructions: Examine the sensor for cracks, broken clips, or frayed wires. Replace sensor if visibly damaged or worn. Why: Intermittent readings often result from cable or sensor defects.

steps.title: Swap Sensor with Known Good Unit
steps.instructions: Use a functioning SpO₂ sensor to determine if the problem is with the sensor or the monitor. Why: Confirms whether the issue is device-related or sensor-related.

steps.title: Check Patient Site
steps.instructions: Ensure the sensor is properly placed on the patient’s finger, toe, or earlobe. Verify patient is not moving excessively and that skin is clean and warm. Why: Poor perfusion, motion, or improper placement can trigger intermittent readings.

steps.title: Inspect Device Connector and Contacts
steps.instructions: Clean contacts using a manufacturer-approved cleaning method. Verify there is no debris, moisture, or corrosion. Why: Dirty contacts can prevent proper sensor detection.

steps.title: Restart Device
steps.instructions: Power down and back on after sensor inspection. Observe if sensor detection stabilizes. Why: Some firmware or communication glitches can be resolved with a reboot.

CCR.Complaint: SpO₂ sensor not detected; intermittent readings reported.
CCR.Cause: Sensor cable frayed and contacts slightly corroded; device failed to detect multiple known-good sensors.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned device contacts, replaced sensor with a known-good unit, and verified stable readings. Device functioning normally. If issue recurs, escalate to repair.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior noted (artifact or no sensor detected)
Indicator lights on sensor connector checked
Any unusual sounds or heat noted
Final device status: Stable reading with known-good sensor

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM1000N - Frequent Signal Dropouts or Artifact Alarms
description: Guide for troubleshooting frequent SpO₂ signal dropouts or artifact alarms on the Nellcor PM1000N, emphasizing external factors before internal repair.

steps.title: Verify Patient and Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Ensure the SpO₂ sensor is correctly attached to the patient’s finger, toe, or earlobe. Remove any nail polish, artificial nails, or other obstructions. Rationale: Poor sensor contact is a common cause of intermittent readings.

steps.title: Check for Patient Movement or Perfusion Issues
steps.instructions: Ask the patient to remain still during monitoring. Observe perfusion; low perfusion states (cold extremities, hypotension) can cause dropouts. Rationale: Motion and poor perfusion interfere with accurate pulse detection.

steps.title: Inspect Sensor Cable and Connections
steps.instructions: Check for frayed, pinched, or damaged cables. Confirm the sensor is fully seated in the oximeter connector. Swap with a known-good sensor if available. Rationale: Damaged cables or loose connections cause intermittent signals.

steps.title: Review Alarm Settings and Device Configuration
steps.instructions: Ensure SpO₂ alarm thresholds are appropriate for the patient population. Reset or cycle power to the device to clear temporary errors. Rationale: Misconfigured alarms may trigger unnecessarily, giving the appearance of dropouts.

steps.title: Environmental Interference Check
steps.instructions: Ensure strong ambient light, electrocautery, or infrared sources are not shining directly on the sensor. Rationale: External light or electrical interference can produce artifact alarms.

steps.title: Test on Another Patient or Simulator
steps.instructions: Apply the same sensor to a different patient or a pulse oximeter simulator. Observe if dropouts persist. Rationale: Confirms whether the issue is patient-specific or device/sensor-related.

CCR.Complaint: SpO₂ readings dropping intermittently and frequent artifact alarms reported.
CCR.Cause: Sensor placement suboptimal and cable showed minor fraying; no external interference noted.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned sensor, replaced damaged cable, verified stable readings; patient monitored successfully. Device returned to service.

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM1000N - Battery Charging Failure or Backup Battery Degradation
description: Guide to troubleshoot battery charging issues and backup battery degradation on the Nellcor PM1000N.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Always move the patient to a working device before troubleshooting a battery issue. Never rely on a device with an unverified or degraded backup battery during patient monitoring.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Check that the wall outlet is live using a verified device or outlet tester. Ensure the power cord is properly seated at both the outlet and device. Why: A faulty outlet or loose connection is the most common cause of battery charging issues.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Adapter
steps.instructions: Look for frays, cracks, or bent prongs on cords and adapter. Swap with a known working cord/adapter if available. Why: Damaged cords can prevent proper charging or trigger intermittent charging behavior.

steps.title: Check Battery Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the battery is seated correctly in the compartment. Inspect battery contacts for corrosion, dirt, or debris. Clean gently if needed. Why: Poor contact can mimic battery failure.

steps.title: Observe Charging Indicators
steps.instructions: Plug the device in and monitor the battery LED or screen indicator. Confirm the device shows charging status. Why: Confirms whether the device is attempting to charge or the battery is non-responsive.

steps.title: Test with Backup Battery
steps.instructions: If available, replace the internal battery with a known good battery. Observe device behavior and charge time. Why: Helps determine if the original battery has degraded or failed.

steps.title: Environmental Checks
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is in a temperature-controlled environment. Extreme cold or heat can reduce battery performance. Avoid charging in direct sunlight or near heat sources.

CCR.Complaint: Device does not charge and backup battery lasts only 10 minutes.
CCR.Cause: Observed that the battery fails to hold charge; AC outlet and power cord confirmed functional; battery contacts clean.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair; temporary replacement unit provided for patient monitoring.

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM1000N - Display or Control Knob/Keypad Malfunction
description: Guide for troubleshooting unresponsive or intermittent display and keypad issues on the Nellcor PM1000N pulse oximeter.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is plugged into a working AC outlet or properly seated in a charging station. Check battery charge level. A low battery may cause keypad or display malfunctions. Why: Unstable or insufficient power can mimic hardware failure.

steps.title: Inspect Display and Knobs/Keypad
steps.instructions: Look for visible damage, cracks, or stuck buttons. Ensure no debris or liquid has contaminated the keypad or display area. Why: Physical obstruction or contamination can prevent proper contact.

steps.title: Reset Device
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off and back on. If battery-operated, remove the battery, wait 30 seconds, and reinsert. Why: A simple reset can resolve temporary software or input glitches.

steps.title: Check Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure any detachable cables (power, sensor, data) are fully seated. Loose or corroded connectors can interfere with keypad or display signals.

steps.title: Test User Input
steps.instructions: Rotate the control knob or press each button sequentially. Observe whether the display responds consistently. Why: Confirms whether the failure is intermittent or constant.

steps.title: Environmental Check
steps.instructions: Ensure device is not exposed to excessive humidity, dust, or electromagnetic interference. Why: Environmental factors can cause transient display or keypad issues.

CCR.Complaint: User reports display flickers and keypad inputs are not registering.
CCR.Cause: Observed that power connections were secure but some keypad buttons intermittently failed when pressed; device may have minor internal wear or contamination.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service and labeled Out of Service; sent for manufacturer or qualified bench repair.

helpfulDetails: Battery level and charging status noted
Display behavior observed (flickering, blank, partial segments)

title: Medtronic Nellcor PM1000N - Internal Fan or Temperature-Related Fault Alarms
description: Guide for troubleshooting internal fan or temperature-related alarms on the Nellcor PM1000N Pulse Oximeter, emphasizing environmental and external checks before internal repair.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions:
Move the patient to another functioning monitor if the unit is currently in use.
Do not attempt troubleshooting on a patient-connected device to prevent inaccurate readings or alarm mismanagement.

steps.title: Check Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions:
Confirm the unit is not exposed to direct sunlight or heat sources.
Ensure ambient temperature is within the manufacturer-specified operating range.
High room temperatures or nearby heat sources can trigger thermal alarms.

steps.title: Inspect Ventilation and Placement
steps.instructions:
Check that all air vents are unobstructed and free of dust or debris.
Make sure the device is on a stable, flat surface that allows airflow.
Avoid placing the unit in confined spaces or against walls.

steps.title: Examine AC Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions:
Verify the AC power supply is stable and providing proper voltage.
Confirm the backup battery is charged and seated correctly.
Power fluctuations can cause temperature monitoring errors.

steps.title: Perform a Soft Reset
steps.instructions:
Power cycle the unit using the correct shutdown procedure.
Some transient fan or thermal faults may clear after a proper restart.

steps.title: Observe Alarm Behavior
steps.instructions:
Note if the alarm persists immediately after power-up or only after prolonged use.
Continuous alarms after environmental and power checks indicate internal issues.

CCR.Complaint: Internal fan alarm displayed; unit overheating alarm triggered intermittently during use.
CCR.Cause: Observed vents partially blocked and unit operating in high ambient temperature. Alarm persisted after environmental correction.
CCR.Resolution: Unit removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for manufacturer repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Room temperature at time of alarm
Any unusual noise from the fan or device housing

title: Medtronic Nellcor N-600x Series Pulse Oximeter - Sensor Not Recognized or Probe Errors
description: Troubleshooting sensor detection issues, probe errors, or intermittent SpO₂ signal loss caused by cables, connectors, or incompatible sensors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the sensor from the patient and transition monitoring to a functioning device if readings are unreliable.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the device and confirm the exact error message, observe intermittent vs constant behavior, waveform presence, and alarm tones.

steps.title: Inspect the Sensor and Cable for Damage
steps.instructions: Check for frayed wires, cracked housing, and bent or dirty connector pins.

steps.title: Verify Proper Sensor Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm sensor type matches device requirements and avoid incompatible brands or adapters.

steps.title: Reseat the Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and firmly reconnect the sensor ensuring a secure connection.

steps.title: Test with a Known Good Sensor
steps.instructions: Swap with a verified working sensor to determine if the issue is sensor-related.

steps.title: Check Sensor Port for Contamination or Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect for debris, bent pins, or wear and clean if needed per guidelines.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn off the device, disconnect power, wait 30 seconds, and restart.

CCR.Complaint: Device displaying “Check Sensor” and not detecting probe during use.
CCR.Cause: Failed SpO₂ sensor identified after testing with known good probe.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced faulty sensor and confirmed proper operation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (working or removed from service)

title: Medtronic Nellcor N-600x Series Pulse Oximeter - SpO2 Reading Inaccurate or Intermittent Dropout
description: Troubleshooting inaccurate SpO2 readings or intermittent signal dropout caused by sensor issues, patient conditions, interference, or connection problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If readings are unreliable, do not rely on the device for clinical decisions. Move the patient to a known working monitor. Expected: Patient monitoring continues safely Why it matters: Inaccurate SpO2 can lead to improper treatment decisions

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Observe the monitor for fluctuating or unrealistic SpO2 values, intermittent signal loss or “---” display, and low perfusion or signal quality indicators. Expected: Issue is confirmed and reproducible Why it matters: Differentiates true device issue from temporary patient condition

steps.title: Check Sensor Placement and Condition (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Inspect and reposition the sensor. Ensure proper placement, remove nail polish or debris, ensure proper fit, and check for damaged cable or worn sensor. Expected: Stable waveform and consistent readings Why it matters: Poor contact or damaged sensors are the leading cause of inaccurate readings

steps.title: Try a Known Good Sensor
steps.instructions: Replace the sensor with a verified working Nellcor-compatible sensor. Expected: Readings stabilize with new sensor Why it matters: Quickly rules out sensor failure vs monitor issue

steps.title: Assess Patient Condition
steps.instructions: Evaluate for low perfusion, movement, ambient light, or poor circulation. Warm extremity, reposition sensor, or try alternate site. Expected: Improved signal quality and reduced dropout Why it matters: Many device issues are patient-related

steps.title: Check Cable and Connector Integrity
steps.instructions: Inspect cable and port, ensure connector is seated, check for bent pins or debris, and test for intermittent signal. Expected: Stable connection without signal interruption Why it matters: Loose or damaged connectors cause intermittent dropout

steps.title: Check Monitor Settings and Filters
steps.instructions: Verify averaging time and sensitivity mode. Adjust as needed. Expected: Improved signal stability Why it matters: Incorrect settings can make readings appear unstable

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn off the device, wait 10–15 seconds, then restart. Expected: Device reinitializes and clears temporary faults Why it matters: Resolves minor processing or signal errors

CCR.Complaint: SpO2 readings fluctuating and intermittently dropping out on Nellcor N-600x.
CCR.Cause: Worn and failing SpO2 sensor causing intermittent signal loss.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced sensor with known good unit and verified stable readings.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Medtronic Nellcor N-600x Series Pulse Oximeter - Alarm Not Triggering or Misconfigured
description: Alarms not sounding, incorrect alarm limits, or delayed/missing alerts due to configuration, volume, or monitoring setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If alarms are not functioning, remove the device from active patient monitoring and transfer the patient to a working monitor. Expected: Patient is safely monitored on another device. Why it matters: Alarm failure removes a critical safety layer for patient deterioration detection.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Condition Exists
steps.instructions: Simulate or observe a condition that should trigger an alarm (e.g., low SpO₂ threshold). Confirm alarm limits are actually being exceeded and observe whether any visual or audible alert occurs. Expected: Alarm should trigger when limits are exceeded. Why it matters: Confirms whether this is a configuration issue vs no alarm condition present.

steps.title: Check Alarm Volume and Mute Status (Most Common Issue)
steps.instructions: Inspect the alarm audio settings. Ensure alarm volume is set above minimum, check if alarm is muted or temporarily silenced, and look for mute indicators on screen. Expected: Audible alarm is enabled and active. Why it matters: Muted alarms are a frequent cause of “no alarm” complaints.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Limits and Settings
steps.instructions: Review configured alarm thresholds. Check SpO₂ high/low limits, check pulse rate alarm limits, and compare against facility standards. Expected: Alarm limits are appropriately set. Why it matters: Incorrect limits can prevent alarms from triggering.

steps.title: Check Alarm Delay or Averaging Settings
steps.instructions: Evaluate whether alarm delays or averaging are affecting response. Review averaging time and check if alarm delay settings are enabled. Expected: Alarm responds in a timely manner. Why it matters: Long averaging or delay settings can make alarms appear non-functional.

steps.title: Inspect Sensor and Signal Quality
steps.instructions: Poor signal may prevent valid alarm detection. Check sensor placement and condition, verify proper patient contact, and look for low perfusion or motion artifact indicators. Expected: Stable waveform and accurate readings. Why it matters: Alarms depend on valid physiological data.

steps.title: Check for Alarm Priority Indicators (Visual Only)
steps.instructions: Determine if alarms are visual but not audible. Observe screen for flashing indicators or messages and confirm if only visual alarms are present. Expected: Both visual and audible alarms should function. Why it matters: Helps isolate speaker/audio vs configuration issue.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Reset the system to clear potential configuration glitches. Turn off the unit, disconnect from power if applicable, and restart the device. Expected: Alarm functionality returns if caused by temporary fault. Why it matters: Clears software-related issues.

CCR.Complaint: Nellcor N-600x pulse oximeter alarms not sounding during low SpO₂ events.
CCR.Cause: Alarm volume found muted and low SpO₂ limit set outside normal clinical range.
CCR.Resolution: Adjusted alarm limits to facility standard and restored alarm volume; verified proper alarm function.

helpfulDetails: Visual vs audible alarm behavior observed
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Medtronic Nellcor N-600x Series Pulse Oximeter - Power Supply or Battery Failure
description: Device not powering on, intermittent shutdowns, or failure to run on battery due to power supply, cord, or battery-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use if it cannot maintain power reliably. Transfer monitoring to a known working unit.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the device using both AC power and battery. Observe behavior including no power, AC-only operation, shutdown when unplugged, or intermittent power.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Plug the device into a known working outlet. Test the outlet with another device or try a different outlet.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Adapter
steps.instructions: Ensure the cord is fully seated, inspect for damage, and swap with a known good cord if available.

steps.title: Check Power Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: Confirm AC power light illuminates and observe charging indicators when battery is installed.

steps.title: Test Battery Operation
steps.instructions: Disconnect AC power and run the device on battery. Verify proper installation and observe runtime stability.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Check for swelling, leakage, corrosion, and replace with a known good battery if needed.

steps.title: Check Battery Contacts and Compartment
steps.instructions: Inspect contacts for cleanliness and proper alignment. Remove debris or corrosion.

steps.title: Perform a Power Reset
steps.instructions: Remove AC power and battery, wait 30–60 seconds, then reconnect AC power and restart the device.

CCR.Complaint: Device will not power on and shuts off when unplugged from AC.
CCR.Cause: Failed internal battery not holding charge and degraded power cord connection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced battery and power cord; verified proper operation on AC and battery power.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (operational or sent for repair)

title: Medtronic Nellcor N-600x Series Pulse Oximeter - Display or Keypad Malfunction
description: Troubleshooting unresponsive buttons, frozen screen, dim display, or incorrect input response caused by power, settings, or external component issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use if the display or controls are not functioning properly. Transfer monitoring to a working unit.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and confirm the malfunction. Observe blank or dim display, frozen or lagging screen, and whether buttons are not responding or responding incorrectly.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Plug into a known good outlet, verify power indicator lights, and check battery icon or status.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle (Most Common Fix)
steps.instructions: Turn off the unit, disconnect from AC power, wait 30–60 seconds, reconnect, and power back on.

steps.title: Inspect Keypad for Physical Obstruction or Damage
steps.instructions: Look for stuck or damaged buttons, check for fluid intrusion, debris, or worn overlay, and gently press each button to assess tactile response.

steps.title: Check Display Visibility and Settings
steps.instructions: Adjust brightness if available, view screen from different angles, and check for partial display or fading segments.

steps.title: Disconnect Accessories and External Connections
steps.instructions: Remove SpO₂ sensor and cables and disconnect any external modules or communication cables.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Liquid Damage or Environmental Exposure
steps.instructions: Look for discoloration, residue, or corrosion and check for unusual odor or sticky buttons.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported pulse oximeter screen blank and buttons not responding during use.
CCR.Cause: Keypad unresponsive and display remained blank after power cycle; suspected internal display or keypad failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled out of service, and sent to Biomed shop for further evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (removed from service or returned to use)

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E310 Touchscreen Error
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E310 Touchscreen Error on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10 electrosurgical unit, including touchscreen input faults, ghost touches, contamination checks, external pressure checks, and safe removal from service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if the touchscreen is responding unexpectedly, selecting items by itself, or displaying Error E310 during setup or use. Stop use of the ESU and provide another verified electrosurgical unit if the procedure is active or imminent.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Move the unit to a safe service area, power it on, and confirm the displayed condition is Error E310 Touchscreen Error. Determine whether the issue appears at startup, during touchscreen use, while idle, after cleaning, or only when certain areas of the screen are touched.

steps.title: Inspect the Touchscreen Surface
steps.instructions: Inspect the touchscreen for fluid residue, cleaning chemical buildup, blood, gel, tape residue, cracks, pressure marks, screen protector issues, or anything resting against the display. Clean the screen using the facility-approved method and allow it to dry completely.

steps.title: Check for Continuous or Ghost Touch Input
steps.instructions: With hands away from the display, observe whether menus open by themselves, buttons highlight without contact, settings change unexpectedly, or touch response occurs in the wrong area. Ghost touches or stuck input indicate a serious touchscreen reliability concern.

steps.title: Check the Chassis and Display Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the front chassis, bezel, and display area for cracked plastic, a bent frame, loose display housing, impact damage, a pinched screen, or foreign material trapped near the edge of the display. Do not perform internal disassembly during basic troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify External Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the ESU is on a stable surface, is not exposed to dripping fluids, is not being cleaned while powered on, and is not positioned where cables, accessories, drapes, or nearby equipment press against the touchscreen.

steps.title: Power Cycle After Inspection and Cleaning
steps.instructions: After the screen is cleaned, dried, and inspected, power the FT10 off, wait briefly, and power it back on. Observe startup and touchscreen behavior. If the error clears and does not return, follow facility safety check procedures before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears after removing residue, drying the screen, or removing pressure from the display, document the external cause. If Error E310 returns with a clean, dry, undamaged screen and no external pressure, remove the unit from service for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E310 Touchscreen Error. Staff reported touchscreen concern, possible unintentional screen input, and medium priority alarm condition.
CCR.Cause: Touchscreen inspected for contamination, residue, damage, and external pressure. Error persisted after cleaning, inspection, drying time, and power cycle. Likely Display PCBA or chassis-related touchscreen fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed Valleylab FT10 from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair or bench evaluation due to recurring E310 touchscreen error.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E235 REM Pad Contact Quality
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E235 REM Pad Contact Quality on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, including patient safety, REM pad placement, return electrode cable checks, accessory replacement, escalation, and CCR documentation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop monopolar energy use, remove the ESU from active patient use if the REM pad contact quality issue cannot be corrected immediately, provide another safe ESU option if needed, and do not bypass or ignore the alarm.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error E235 REM Pad Contact Quality and note whether it appears at startup, after connecting the REM pad, when monopolar mode is selected, during activation, or intermittently during patient movement.

steps.title: Confirm a REM Pad Is Required for the Selected Mode
steps.instructions: Verify whether monopolar cut or coag is being used and confirm that a compatible REM patient return electrode is required. If only bipolar is intended, check whether monopolar mode was selected accidentally.

steps.title: Check the REM Pad Connection at the ESU
steps.instructions: Confirm the return electrode cable is fully seated in the correct port. Inspect for a loose, partially inserted, damaged, contaminated, or bent connector, then disconnect and reconnect the cable firmly.

steps.title: Check the REM Pad at the Patient Site
steps.instructions: Confirm the pad is fully adhered with clean, flat skin contact. Look for lifted edges, wrinkles, trapped air, moisture, prep solution, hair, lotion, or poor placement.

steps.title: Replace the REM Pad
steps.instructions: Remove the questionable pad from service, apply a new compatible REM pad using proper skin prep and placement, reconnect the pad, and verify whether the E235 error clears.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good REM Cable or Pad Setup
steps.instructions: Swap the REM cable with a known-good cable and use a new compatible REM pad to determine whether the fault follows the accessory setup or remains with the ESU.

steps.title: Inspect for Cable or Connector Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the REM cable and connector for cracked jacket, bent pins, loose strain relief, fluid contamination, visible wear, or intermittent behavior when moved.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Workflow Causes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the alarm occurs with patient repositioning, prep solution, draping, certain pad lots, one OR room, one cable, or one accessory set.

steps.title: Confirm the Final Device Status
steps.instructions: Verify the E235 error is cleared, confirm the REM status is acceptable, confirm monopolar activation is available only after safe return electrode contact is recognized, and check that no additional errors are present.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Valleylab FT10 displaying Error E235 REM Pad Contact Quality during monopolar setup. Unit alarmed and monopolar use was not available due to return electrode contact issue.
CCR.Cause: REM patient return electrode contact issue identified. Pad connection and patient-site pad placement were checked. Fault cleared after REM pad was replaced and connection was reseated.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced REM pad, verified secure connection at ESU and patient return electrode cable, confirmed E235 cleared, and verified unit returned to normal ready status. Device returned to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E320 Check Instrument
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E320 Check Instrument on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, including Mono 1 handswitch, monopolar instrument, connector, and external input checks before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use, stop using the affected ESU output, provide an alternate ESU if electrosurgery is needed, and do not continue using an instrument that may be stuck active or falsely detected.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the Valleylab FT10, confirm Error E320 Check Instrument, determine when the error appears, and check whether it clears when the connected Mono 1 instrument is removed.

steps.title: Check for an Active or Stuck Handswitch
steps.instructions: Confirm no one is pressing the handswitch, inspect the monopolar instrument for stuck cut or coag buttons, release any physically stuck switch, and disconnect the instrument from Mono 1.

steps.title: Replace the Monopolar Instrument
steps.instructions: Remove the suspect handswitching pencil or monopolar instrument, connect a known-good compatible instrument to Mono 1, power cycle if needed, and verify whether E320 returns.

steps.title: Inspect the Mono 1 Port and Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the Mono 1 connector area for bent pins, loose connection, fluid contamination, debris, burn marks, cracked housing, or poor seating. Clean only per approved procedures and reconnect the known-good instrument firmly.

steps.title: Compare Mono 1 and Other Available Outputs
steps.instructions: If appropriate, compare behavior using another compatible monopolar output and then retest Mono 1. Determine whether the fault follows the instrument or stays with the ESU input.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Handling Causes
steps.instructions: Confirm the instrument is dry, check for cable strain, pinching, crushing, tight wrapping, fluid exposure, or recent handling that may falsely trigger the handswitch input.

steps.title: Reboot and Recheck After External Causes Are Removed
steps.instructions: Disconnect the instrument, power the FT10 off, restart it, observe whether E320 appears with no instrument connected, then connect a known-good instrument and observe again.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displaying Error E320 Check Instrument during Mono 1 handswitch instrument setup.
CCR.Cause: Initial inspection found suspected Mono 1 handswitch/instrument issue. Error cleared after suspect monopolar instrument was removed and replaced with a known-good instrument.
CCR.Resolution: Removed defective instrument from service, inspected Mono 1 connection, rebooted ESU, connected known-good instrument, and verified E320 did not return. Unit passed basic functional check and was returned to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E321 Check Foot Pedal
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E321 Check Foot Pedal on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, indicating the Mono 1 foot pedal input is detected as active and may cause an ESU alarm, activation lockout, or unintended activation concern.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if Error E321 appears during setup, before activation, or during a procedure. Stop use of the ESU, confirm no monopolar output is being activated unintentionally, and use another ESU if the procedure is active or urgent.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the unit on and confirm the exact message is Error: E321 Check Foot Pedal. Identify whether it appears at startup, when connecting the foot pedal, when selecting monopolar mode, during attempted activation, or intermittently during the case.

steps.title: Release and Inspect the Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Make sure no one is pressing the foot pedal. Remove anything near the pedal, press and release it several times, and inspect for sticking, damage, fluid, debris, tape, broken plastic, or OR contamination.

steps.title: Disconnect the Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Turn the ESU off, disconnect the Mono 1 foot pedal, power the unit back on, and observe whether Error E321 returns. If the error clears with the pedal disconnected, suspect the foot pedal, cable, or connector.

steps.title: Inspect the Foot Pedal Cable and Connector
steps.instructions: Check the cable for cuts, crushed sections, exposed conductors, strain relief damage, or fluid contamination. Inspect the connector for bent pins, corrosion, debris, fluid residue, or damage.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible Mono 1 foot pedal to the same port and check whether Error E321 returns. If the known-good pedal works normally, replace the original foot pedal and document the failed accessory.

steps.title: Check Whether the Error Follows the Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: If allowed by shop procedure, test the suspect foot pedal on another compatible FT10 or approved test setup. Do not use the suspect foot pedal clinically. If the error follows the pedal, remove it from service.

steps.title: Inspect the FT10 Foot Pedal Port
steps.instructions: With the ESU powered off, inspect the Mono 1 foot pedal port for bent contacts, recessed pins, damaged housing, contamination, fluid residue, looseness, or impact damage.

steps.title: Reboot and Recheck After Accessory Isolation
steps.instructions: After removing the suspect pedal or installing a known-good pedal, power cycle the FT10 and confirm Error E321 does not return. Verify that foot pedal input is only detected when intentionally pressed.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Accessory-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Treat the issue as accessory-related if the error clears when the pedal is removed or follows the suspect pedal. Treat it as device-related if E321 remains with no pedal connected, occurs with multiple known-good pedals, or appears at startup before a pedal is connected.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displaying Error E321 Check Foot Pedal during setup. Unit indicated Mono 1 foot pedal input was active and would not clear for normal ESU use.
CCR.Cause: Mono 1 foot pedal input issue confirmed. Foot pedal was checked for stuck activation, cable damage, connector damage, contamination, and accessory failure. Error cleared with known-good compatible foot pedal, indicating defective original foot pedal.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from patient use, inspected and disconnected suspect foot pedal, power cycled ESU, tested with known-good compatible foot pedal, and replaced defective foot pedal. Unit passed functional check and was returned to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E322 Check Instrument
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E322 Check Instrument on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, where the Mono 2 handswitch input is detected as pressed and may affect ESU readiness or safe activation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use, stop using the affected Mono 2 port, and provide another known-working ESU if electrosurgery is clinically needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error E322 Check Instrument and document whether it appears at startup, after connecting an instrument, during activation, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Check for a Pressed or Stuck Handswitch
steps.instructions: Inspect the monopolar handswitch pencil or instrument and confirm the Cut and Coag buttons are not pressed, jammed, taped down, contaminated, or trapped under a drape or cable.

steps.title: Disconnect the Mono 2 Instrument
steps.instructions: Remove the instrument from the Mono 2 port and observe whether the error clears. If it clears and returns with the same instrument, suspect the accessory or handswitch circuit.

steps.title: Replace the Instrument or Pencil
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible monopolar handswitch instrument to the Mono 2 port. Do not reuse the suspect instrument during patient care.

steps.title: Inspect the Mono 2 Port and Accessory Connection
steps.instructions: Check the Mono 2 receptacle for bent contacts, debris, fluid contamination, damaged plastic, looseness, arcing, or poor accessory fit.

steps.title: Compare Mono 1 and Mono 2 Behavior
steps.instructions: During bench testing, compare a known-good compatible instrument on Mono 1 and Mono 2 to determine whether the issue is specific to the Mono 2 input path.

steps.title: Check for External Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the ESU exterior, front panel, accessory ports, power cord, and surrounding area for fluid intrusion, impact damage, odor, heat, cracked housing, or OR contamination.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only After Accessories Are Removed
steps.instructions: Disconnect all monopolar instruments, power cycle the FT10, and observe whether Error E322 returns with no instrument connected.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Cause
steps.instructions: If the error follows one instrument, replace the instrument. If it occurs with multiple known-good instruments or with nothing connected, remove the unit from service for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E322 Check Instrument. Staff reported a Mono 2 handswitch or instrument issue and the unit would not clear reliably for use.
CCR.Cause: Mono 2 handswitch input was detected as pressed. Error cleared after disconnecting the suspect instrument, indicating a likely handswitch or accessory fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed suspect instrument from use and tested FT10 with a known-good compatible monopolar instrument. Error did not return and unit passed functional check before return to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E323 Check Foot Pedal
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E323 Check Foot Pedal on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, indicating the Mono 2 foot pedal may be pressed, stuck, connected incorrectly, damaged, or falsely detected as active.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from active patient use if Error E323 appears during setup, before activation, or during a procedure. Provide an alternate ESU if the procedure is active or urgent.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the unit and confirm the display shows Error E323 Check Foot Pedal. Note whether it occurs at startup, when the Mono 2 foot pedal is connected, when touched, or intermittently during cable movement.

steps.title: Release and Inspect the Mono 2 Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Confirm nothing is resting on the pedal, verify it returns to neutral, and check for sticking, binding, fluid contamination, tape, drape material, or physical damage.

steps.title: Check the Foot Pedal Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and inspect the foot pedal connector for bent pins, moisture, debris, damaged locking hardware, or loose fit. Reconnect it fully to the correct port.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Foot Pedal and Port
steps.instructions: Verify the foot pedal is compatible with the Valleylab FT10 and connected for the intended Mono 2 use. Confirm staff did not connect the wrong accessory or wrong port.

steps.title: Isolate the Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Power the ESU off, disconnect the suspected foot pedal, power the ESU back on, and observe whether Error E323 returns.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible foot pedal and check whether Error E323 returns. Test pedal response only in a safe, non-patient setup.

steps.title: Inspect for Cable Damage or Intermittent Faults
steps.instructions: Inspect the foot pedal cable for crushed sections, cuts, pulled strain reliefs, exposed conductors, fluid intrusion, or rolling damage. Gently move the cable while observing the alarm.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Setup Causes
steps.instructions: Look for fluid, trapped cords, equipment pressing on the pedal, incorrect adapters, extensions, or nonstandard accessories that could cause false activation detection.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is Accessory-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Compare behavior with the original pedal, no pedal connected, and a known-good compatible pedal to determine whether the fault follows the accessory or remains with the ESU.

CCR.Complaint: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E323 Check Foot Pedal. Staff reported Mono 2 foot pedal alarm during setup.
CCR.Cause: Original Mono 2 foot pedal remained detected as pressed. Error cleared when pedal was removed and did not return with known-good compatible foot pedal.
CCR.Resolution: Removed defective foot pedal from service. Installed known-good compatible foot pedal, verified normal startup and safe pedal response. Returned ESU to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E324 Check Instrument
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E324 Check Instrument on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, indicating a bipolar handswitch may be pressed, stuck, damaged, contaminated, or falsely detected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if the error appears during setup, activation, or a procedure. Stop use of the affected bipolar instrument and provide an alternate ESU or accessory path if energy delivery is needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error E324 Check Instrument. Note whether it appears at startup, when the bipolar instrument is connected, when the handswitch is touched, during activation, or intermittently.

steps.title: Release the Bipolar Handswitch
steps.instructions: Make sure the bipolar handswitch is not being held down. Inspect the handle or activation area for a stuck, jammed, wet, contaminated, or damaged switch.

steps.title: Inspect the Bipolar Instrument
steps.instructions: Check the bipolar instrument for cracked housing, bent connector pins, fluid contamination, damaged insulation, sticky switch movement, or signs of physical damage.

steps.title: Replace the Bipolar Instrument
steps.instructions: Disconnect the suspect instrument and connect a known-good compatible bipolar instrument. Power cycle the ESU if needed and observe whether Error E324 returns.

steps.title: Check the Bipolar Instrument Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the instrument is fully seated and the connector is clean, dry, undamaged, and not loose, forced, or misaligned.

steps.title: Confirm the Issue Is Not Setup or User Technique
steps.instructions: Ask whether the instrument was resting under tension, being squeezed by a clamp, or placed where the switch could be pressed. Confirm the correct accessory type is being used.

steps.title: Recheck the FT10 With No Instrument Connected
