steps.instructions: Disconnect the bipolar instrument, power cycle the FT10, and observe whether E324 appears with no bipolar instrument connected.

steps.title: Isolate Accessory-Related vs Device-Related Failure
steps.instructions: If E324 occurs only with one instrument, remove that instrument from service. If it occurs with multiple known-good instruments or with no instrument connected, remove the FT10 from service for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E324 Check Instrument during bipolar setup or activation and would not operate normally with the connected bipolar instrument.
CCR.Cause: Bipolar handswitch signal was detected as pressed. Error cleared after replacing the suspect bipolar instrument with a known-good compatible instrument, or persisted with known-good accessories indicating possible internal detection/control fault.
CCR.Resolution: Released and inspected bipolar handswitch, checked instrument condition and connector, tested with known-good bipolar instrument, verified whether Error E324 cleared, and removed failed accessory or ESU from service as appropriate.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E325 Check Foot Pedal
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E325 Check Foot Pedal on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, indicating a bipolar foot pedal input that may be active, stuck, connected incorrectly, damaged, or falsely detected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if the error appears during setup, activation, or a procedure. Stop use of the affected ESU setup and provide a backup ESU if the case is active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the Valleylab FT10 and confirm the displayed message is Error: E325 Check Foot Pedal. Note whether it appears at startup, when the foot pedal is connected, when pressed, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Confirm the Bipolar Foot Pedal Is Not Being Pressed
steps.instructions: Check whether the bipolar foot pedal is physically pressed, blocked, or mechanically sticking. Press and release the pedal several times and verify that it returns fully to the released position.

steps.title: Disconnect the Foot Pedal and Recheck the Error
steps.instructions: Power the ESU off, disconnect the bipolar foot pedal, inspect the connector, then power the FT10 back on with the foot pedal disconnected. If the error clears, suspect the foot pedal or cable.

steps.title: Inspect the Foot Pedal and Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the foot pedal cable, connector, housing, strain relief, and pedal mechanism for cuts, pinching, fluid intrusion, cracks, contamination, or damage. Replace the foot pedal if damage is found.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible bipolar foot pedal, power cycle the FT10, and confirm whether Error E325 returns. Test activation only in a safe test setup, not on a patient.

steps.title: Check the Foot Pedal Connection at the ESU
steps.instructions: Inspect the FT10 foot pedal port for bent or recessed pins, cracked housing, debris, fluid residue, loose connection, or impact damage.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is Accessory-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Compare results with the foot pedal disconnected and with a known-good foot pedal. If the error remains with multiple known-good accessories or with no pedal connected, suspect an internal ESU input fault.

steps.title: Escalate Only After External Checks Are Complete
steps.instructions: If E325 persists after external checks and known-good accessory testing, remove the FT10 from service, label it Out of Service, and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error: E325 Check Foot Pedal during setup/use. Bipolar foot pedal input appeared active or faulted.
CCR.Cause: Bipolar foot pedal circuit was detected as pressed. Foot pedal was checked for stuck activation, cable damage, connector issues, and tested with a known-good compatible foot pedal.
CCR.Resolution: Released and inspected foot pedal, checked cable and connector condition, power cycled unit, and tested with known-good foot pedal. Faulty foot pedal was replaced and ESU passed functional verification. Unit was removed from service for repair evaluation if error persisted.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E326 Check Instrument
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E326 Check Instrument on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit when the LigaSure handswitch is detected as pressed, stuck, or sending an unintended activation input.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if Error E326 appears during setup, activation, or a procedure. Stop using the affected LigaSure instrument and move the procedure to another safe ESU, LigaSure device, or approved method if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the Valleylab FT10 and confirm the displayed message is Error: E326 Check Instrument. Ask staff when the error occurred and inspect whether the LigaSure instrument appears stuck, damaged, wet, contaminated, or difficult to actuate.

steps.title: Release and Inspect the LigaSure Handswitch
steps.instructions: Confirm the handswitch button is not being held down. Release the handswitch completely and inspect the instrument handle for a stuck button, cracked housing, fluid contamination, debris, or mechanical binding.

steps.title: Check for User Setup or Handling Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm the instrument was not connected while the handswitch was pressed. Check that the cable is not pulled tight, pinched, wrapped around equipment, or positioned in a way that presses the handswitch.

steps.title: Replace the LigaSure Instrument
steps.instructions: Disconnect the suspected LigaSure instrument and connect a known-good compatible LigaSure instrument. Power cycle the FT10 if needed and observe whether E326 returns.

steps.title: Inspect the Instrument Connection Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the LigaSure port for obvious damage, bent contacts, contamination, fluid residue, or debris. Do not insert tools into the connector. Clean only according to facility-approved external cleaning procedures.

steps.title: Compare With Other ESU Functions
steps.instructions: Determine whether the problem follows the instrument or stays with the generator. Test with known-good accessories according to facility policy and check whether other ESU functions report separate errors.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Behavior
steps.instructions: With no patient connected, gently reposition the instrument cable and handle while observing whether E326 returns. Do not activate energy output unless following approved test procedures with proper test equipment or load.

CCR.Complaint: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E326 Check Instrument while staff were using a LigaSure instrument.
CCR.Cause: LigaSure handswitch input was detected as active. Original instrument was inspected and replaced. Error cleared with a known-good LigaSure instrument, indicating a faulty or stuck LigaSure instrument.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from patient use, released and inspected LigaSure handswitch, checked instrument connection, power cycled generator, and tested with a known-good LigaSure instrument. FT10 returned to service after error cleared with replacement instrument, or was labeled Out of Service and sent for bench evaluation if the error persisted.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E327 Check Foot Pedal
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E327 Check Foot Pedal on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, indicating a LigaSure foot pedal may be pressed, stuck, damaged, connected incorrectly, or causing unintended activation input.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if the error appears during a case, activation is unreliable, or there is concern for unintended energy activation. Use a known-working backup ESU or approved setup before continuing troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error E327 Check Foot Pedal. Note whether it appears at startup, when the LigaSure foot pedal is connected, when the cable is moved, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Check for a Pressed or Stuck LigaSure Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Inspect the pedal for physical pressure, stuck movement, fluid, debris, tape, drape material, cable tension, or objects pressing against it. Clear any obstruction and recheck the ESU.

steps.title: Disconnect the Foot Pedal and Recheck the Error
steps.instructions: Power the FT10 down, disconnect the LigaSure foot pedal, inspect the connector, then power the unit back on with the pedal disconnected to see whether the error clears.

steps.title: Inspect the Foot Pedal Cable and Connector
steps.instructions: Check for crushed cable sections, cuts, exposed conductors, bent pins, broken connector housing, contamination, loose strain relief, or damage from equipment rolling over the cable.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good LigaSure Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible LigaSure foot pedal and check whether Error E327 returns. If the error clears, remove the failed foot pedal from service.

steps.title: Check the ESU Foot Pedal Port and Connection Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the external foot pedal port for bent or recessed pins, debris, fluid contamination, loose fit, or physical damage. Remove the ESU from service if the port appears damaged.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Accessory-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Compare behavior with the pedal disconnected and with a known-good pedal. If the error remains with no pedal or with multiple known-good pedals, suspect an ESU-side input issue.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Cable or Movement-Related Symptoms
steps.instructions: In a safe non-patient test condition, gently move the cable near the connector and strain relief. If the error changes with movement, remove the foot pedal from service.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: After correcting the issue, confirm Error E327 no longer appears, verify normal startup, confirm the foot pedal is not falsely activating, and return the FT10 to service only when safe.

CCR.Complaint: Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E327 Check Foot Pedal during setup/use. Staff reported the LigaSure foot pedal error and stated the system appeared to detect the pedal as pressed.
CCR.Cause: LigaSure foot pedal input issue confirmed. Pedal was checked for stuck activation, obstruction, cable damage, connector seating, and contamination. Known-good foot pedal was used to isolate the fault.
CCR.Resolution: Released and inspected LigaSure foot pedal, checked cable and connector, reseated connection, and tested with known-good compatible foot pedal. Failed foot pedal was removed from service after error cleared with replacement pedal. ESU passed functional check and was returned to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E328 Unintentional Input
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E328 Unintentional Input on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10, indicating a possible stuck, contaminated, pressured, damaged, or falsely detected front panel button input.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if Error E328 appears during setup, standby, or active procedure use. Stop using the ESU if the front panel input appears unreliable and provide another known-working ESU if the procedure is active or urgent.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the unit on, observe the display, and confirm the message is Error: E328 Unintentional Input. Note when the error appears, including startup, after touching the front panel, during setup, intermittently, after cleaning, or when pressure is applied to the display or housing.

steps.title: Inspect the Front Panel for a Stuck or Pressed Button
steps.instructions: Inspect the front panel buttons and surrounding bezel. Confirm no button is physically pressed, stuck, tilted, cracked, or trapped under the overlay. Gently release any depressed button without prying into the panel or disassembling the housing.

steps.title: Check for Fluid, Cleaner Residue, or Contamination
steps.instructions: Look for cleaning solution, gel, dried residue, tape, lint, drape material, or debris around the front panel. Clean the exterior according to approved facility cleaning practices and allow the unit to fully dry before retesting.

steps.title: Remove External Pressure From the Display or Chassis
steps.instructions: Confirm nothing is leaning against the front panel. Check that the unit is sitting flat and that the front housing is not warped, cracked, pinched, or compressed by mounting hardware. Move the ESU to a stable surface and retest.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Retest
steps.instructions: Turn the Valleylab FT10 off, disconnect it from AC power briefly if appropriate, reconnect power, and restart the unit. Observe whether Error E328 returns.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Line Cord
steps.instructions: Plug the FT10 into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Inspect the power cord for damage, looseness, or strain. Avoid power strips, loose outlets, or questionable OR boom receptacles during testing.

steps.title: Check for Repeatability With No Accessories Connected
steps.instructions: Disconnect handpieces, foot pedals, patient return electrode cables, and other accessories. Power the unit on with only AC power connected and observe whether E328 returns.

steps.title: Reconnect Accessories One at a Time
steps.instructions: If the error cleared with accessories removed, reconnect accessories one at a time and observe the display after each connection. Replace any damaged or questionable accessory.

steps.title: Evaluate for Device-Level Failure
steps.instructions: If the error returns after external checks, document when it occurs and remove the device from service. Persistent E328 may indicate a front panel input path, display assembly, chassis, or controller-related fault requiring bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Valleylab FT10 reported Error E328 Unintentional Input during startup/setup. Staff reported a front panel input error and removed the unit from OR use.
CCR.Cause: Front panel buttons were inspected for stuck input, contamination, pressure, and chassis damage. Unit was power cycled and tested on a known-good outlet. Error returned with accessories removed, indicating likely internal display/front panel, chassis, or controller input fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair. Device was not returned to clinical use due to recurring E328 condition.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E329 Check Foot Pedal
description: Troubleshooting guide for Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Error E329 Check Foot Pedal, indicating invalid foot pedal input that may affect safe ESU activation control.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if Error E329 appears during setup, activation, or a procedure. Stop use of the ESU if activation control is unreliable and provide another ESU if electrosurgery is clinically needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the Valleylab FT10 and confirm the message is Error E329 Check Foot Pedal Invalid Foot Pedal Input. Ask when the error occurred and whether it clears when the foot pedal is released.

steps.title: Release the Foot Pedal and Check for Stuck Activation
steps.instructions: Make sure no one is pressing the foot pedal. Inspect for a stuck switch, debris, cords, drapes, or equipment holding the pedal down. Press and release each pedal function to confirm normal movement.

steps.title: Check the Foot Pedal Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and inspect the foot pedal connector for bent pins, contamination, fluid residue, damage, or looseness. Reconnect the pedal securely and check the cable routing.

steps.title: Inspect the Foot Pedal and Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the pedal cable, strain relief, insulation, housing, and switches for damage, fluid intrusion, sticky movement, cracks, or impact signs.

steps.title: Swap with a Known-Good Compatible Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Replace the suspect foot pedal with a known-good compatible Valleylab FT10 foot pedal. Power cycle the ESU if needed and confirm whether Error E329 returns during safe test setup.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Port-Specific Behavior
steps.instructions: Determine whether the error occurs with one pedal, one connector, one mode, or only when the pedal is pressed. Confirm the correct pedal type is being used.

steps.title: Perform a Basic ESU Condition Check
steps.instructions: Inspect the FT10 foot pedal port area and confirm the unit powers normally with no additional errors, odor, overheating, unusual sounds, or visible damage.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Resolved
steps.instructions: If the error clears after releasing, reconnecting, or replacing the pedal, document the finding and complete facility functional checks. If the error returns with a known-good foot pedal, remove the ESU from service and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displaying Error E329 Check Foot Pedal Invalid Foot Pedal Input. Staff reported foot pedal activation issue during setup or use.
CCR.Cause: Foot pedal input fault confirmed. Foot pedal and cable inspected. Error cleared after replacing suspect foot pedal with known-good compatible foot pedal.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced faulty foot pedal. Verified error no longer appears and foot pedal activation response is normal in test setup. Unit passed functional check and was returned to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E330 Prolonged Activation
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E330 Prolonged Activation on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10 electrosurgical unit, including patient safety steps, accessory checks, activation input isolation, and escalation guidance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ESU from active patient use if Error E330 appears during a procedure, output is interrupted, or activation behavior is questionable. Stop using the affected activation device and provide another ESU if the procedure must continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error E330 Prolonged Activation. Ask staff whether it occurred during monopolar, bipolar, LigaSure, foot pedal, or hand-controlled instrument use, and document whether output was interrupted.

steps.title: Dismiss the Error and Check for Immediate Return
steps.instructions: Allow the internal check to complete, dismiss the message if prompted, and observe whether the error clears and stays cleared before reconnecting the device for clinical use.

steps.title: Check for a Stuck or Held Activation Device
steps.instructions: Inspect hand-controlled instruments and foot pedals for stuck switches, pressed pedals, fluid contamination, physical damage, or objects holding an activation control down.

steps.title: Disconnect Accessories and Recheck the Error
steps.instructions: Power the FT10 off, disconnect activation accessories, power the unit back on, and observe whether E330 returns at startup or idle.

steps.title: Test One Accessory at a Time
steps.instructions: Reconnect accessories individually and observe whether the error returns. If a specific accessory causes the error, remove that accessory from service and replace it.

steps.title: Check Foot Pedal Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the foot pedal body, cable, plug, and strain relief for damage, fluid intrusion, sticky movement, or crushed cable sections. Test with a known-good compatible foot pedal if available.

steps.title: Check Instrument and Handswitch Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the instrument connector, cable, insulation, and activation buttons. Replace the instrument with a known-good compatible accessory if the hand switch or instrument is suspected.

steps.title: Check the ESU Front Panel and Connectors
steps.instructions: Inspect the generator ports, controls, and front panel for contamination, bent pins, fluid residue, debris, or stuck buttons. Do not perform internal disassembly during field troubleshooting.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Operational Check
steps.instructions: After the error clears, follow facility ESU checkout practices and confirm normal power-up, idle behavior, activation, deactivation, tones, display indications, and output readiness before returning the unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E330 Prolonged Activation during use and required acknowledgement before continued operation.
CCR.Cause: Prolonged activation condition verified. Foot pedal, hand-controlled instrument, cables, and connectors inspected. Error returned with known-good accessories, indicating likely internal activation detection issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed FT10 from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair. Documented E330 behavior, accessory checks, known-good accessory test, and final device status.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E331 Invalid Instrument
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E331 Invalid Instrument on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10 electrosurgical unit when the generator does not recognize or accept the connected surgical instrument.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if Error E331 appears during setup or a procedure. Stop use of the affected ESU output and switch to a known-working ESU, instrument, or backup setup if the procedure is active.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error E331 Invalid Instrument. Note whether the message appears at startup, when the instrument is connected, during activation, with one instrument, or on one specific port or mode.

steps.title: Identify the Connected Instrument
steps.instructions: Confirm the instrument type matches the intended FT10 function. Verify compatibility, labeling, packaging condition, and expiration status if applicable.

steps.title: Replace the Instrument
steps.instructions: Disconnect the suspect instrument, inspect the connector, and connect a new known-compatible instrument. Power cycle the FT10 if needed and check whether Error E331 clears.

steps.title: Check the Instrument Connection Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the FT10 connection point for physical damage, debris, fluid intrusion, loose fit, or abnormal connector resistance. Confirm the instrument seats fully and securely.

steps.title: Confirm the Error Is Not Accessory-Specific
steps.instructions: Test with a second known-good compatible instrument. If only one instrument causes E331, suspect the instrument. If multiple known-good instruments cause E331 on the same FT10, suspect a device-side fault.

steps.title: Check for Contamination or Fluid Exposure
steps.instructions: Inspect the instrument connector and FT10 front panel for prep solution, blood, saline, moisture, debris, or residue. Clean external surfaces only according to facility-approved procedures and allow the area to dry before retesting.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Retest After External Checks
steps.instructions: Disconnect the instrument, power the unit off, wait briefly, power the unit back on, reconnect a known-good compatible instrument, and observe whether Error E331 returns.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is Device-Related
steps.instructions: If Error E331 appears with multiple known-good compatible instruments after external checks, remove accessories, restart the generator, reconnect only a known-good instrument, and treat the issue as a likely device-side recognition fault if the error repeats.

CCR.Complaint: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E331 Invalid Instrument during instrument connection/setup. Staff reported the generator would not accept the connected instrument.
CCR.Cause: Instrument compatibility, connector condition, and external setup were checked. Error cleared after replacing the connected instrument, indicating a defective or invalid accessory.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced instrument with known-compatible accessory. Error E331 cleared. Valleylab FT10 passed functional check and was returned to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E350 Internal Check
description: Troubleshooting guide for Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Error E350 Internal Check, a return electrode overtemperature condition that may interrupt ESU activation until the system cools and safe operation is verified.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ESU from active patient use if Error E350 appears and activation is interrupted. Use another ESU if surgical energy is urgently needed. Do not repeatedly activate the unit while the error is present.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Error: E350 Internal Check and determine whether the condition includes Return Electrode Overtemp. Ask when the error appeared and whether it occurred during activation, after extended use, at startup, or only with certain accessories.

steps.title: Allow Cooling Time
steps.instructions: Stop ESU activation, allow the system and return electrode pathway time to cool, and only attempt reactivation when it is safe to do so.

steps.title: Check the Return Electrode Pad and Patient Contact
steps.instructions: Confirm the pad is properly applied with full skin contact. Look for lifting edges, wrinkles, dry gel, poor adhesion, contamination, hair, scar tissue, bony areas, or poor tissue contact. Replace the pad if condition is questionable.

steps.title: Check the Return Electrode Cable and Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the cable is fully seated. Inspect for cuts, crushed sections, bent pins, loose connectors, contamination, or fluid intrusion. Reseat the cable and test with a known-good setup if available.

steps.title: Check Accessories and Instruments
steps.instructions: Identify which instrument was used when the error occurred. Inspect the accessory for damage, contamination, sticking activation controls, or unusual heating. Try a known-good compatible accessory if safe and appropriate.

steps.title: Review Use Pattern and Settings
steps.instructions: Ask whether the error followed long or repeated activations. Review the active mode, power setting, instrument, and clinical workflow. Confirm the settings are appropriate and allow additional cooling time if needed.

steps.title: Inspect the ESU Externally
steps.instructions: Check for blocked vents, dust buildup, fluid contamination, physical damage, unusual odor, abnormal heat, fan concerns, restricted ventilation, or improper AC power.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: Only power cycle if the unit is no longer needed for active patient care. Restart the unit, allow startup checks to complete, reconnect a known-good return electrode and accessory setup, and confirm whether Error E350 returns.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E350 Internal Check during activation. Staff reported return electrode overtemperature message and interrupted ESU activation.
CCR.Cause: Return electrode overtemperature condition confirmed. Return electrode pad placement, cable connection, accessory condition, ventilation, and use pattern were checked. Error returned after cooling and known-good accessory setup.
CCR.Resolution: Allowed cooling time, inspected return electrode pad and cable, checked accessory setup, verified external condition and ventilation, and reactivated safely. Error returned, so unit was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E355 Invalid Activation
description: Troubleshooting guide for Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Error E355 Invalid Activation, commonly caused by unsupported foot pedal activation when activation must occur from the instrument only.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if activation is not behaving as expected. Stop using the affected activation method and use another verified ESU if the procedure must continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the FT10, confirm Error E355 Invalid Activation, and determine whether the error occurred during foot pedal use, instrument activation, or repeated activation attempts.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Activation Method
steps.instructions: Check whether the connected instrument supports foot pedal activation or requires activation from the instrument hand controls.

steps.title: Dismiss the Error and Reactivate Correctly
steps.instructions: Dismiss the message and reactivate using the supported activation method. Do not use the foot pedal if the connected setup requires instrument-only activation.

steps.title: Inspect the Foot Pedal Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect and inspect the foot pedal connector and cable for bent pins, contamination, fluid, damage, or poor seating.

steps.title: Try Operation Without the Foot Pedal
steps.instructions: Remove the foot pedal, connect the intended instrument, dismiss the error, and activate from the instrument only.

steps.title: Swap the Foot Pedal if Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: Use a known-good compatible foot pedal only if the instrument and mode support foot pedal activation.

steps.title: Check for Unintended Foot Pedal Activation
steps.instructions: Inspect the pedal for stuck motion, debris, fluid contamination, damaged housing, or pressure from nearby equipment.

steps.title: Check Instrument and Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect and reconnect the instrument, confirm the correct port, and test with a known-good compatible instrument if available.

steps.title: Isolate Accessory vs Device Issue
steps.instructions: Test with the foot pedal removed, a known-good compatible foot pedal, a known-good compatible instrument, and the correct activation method to determine whether the issue follows an accessory or remains with the generator.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displaying Error E355 Invalid Activation during attempted foot pedal activation.
CCR.Cause: Foot pedal activation was not supported for the connected instrument/setup. Error cleared when activation was performed from the instrument controls.
CCR.Resolution: Dismissed error, verified correct activation method, inspected foot pedal and instrument connections, confirmed normal operation using instrument activation only, and returned ESU to service.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E356 System Error
description: Troubleshooting guide for Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Error E356 System Error, indicating invalid RF calibration that may affect ESU readiness and controlled energy delivery.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if Error E356 appears during setup, startup, or attempted activation. Use another verified ESU if electrosurgery is needed and do not attempt clinical output while the error is unresolved.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power the FT10 on and confirm the displayed message is Error: E356 System Error Invalid RF Calibration. Note whether it appears during startup, after self-test, during activation, after connecting an accessory, or intermittently.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Startup Conditions
steps.instructions: Plug the unit directly into a known-good hospital-grade AC outlet, avoid power strips or extension cords, confirm the power cord is fully seated, and power cycle the unit after confirming proper power.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and External Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord, plug, strain relief, case, display, front panel, and output receptacles for damage, contamination, fluid intrusion, heat, smoke, or burnt odor.

steps.title: Remove Accessories and Recheck
steps.instructions: Disconnect all patient cables, foot pedals, handpieces, bipolar instruments, LigaSure devices, REM cables, and adapters. Power the unit back on with only AC power connected and observe whether Error E356 returns.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Connector Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect all ports and recently used accessories for moisture, debris, damaged pins, residue, cracked connectors, or loose-fitting plugs. Do not use damaged or contaminated accessories.

steps.title: Confirm the Error Is Not Related to User Configuration
steps.instructions: Confirm the user was not attempting to use an unsupported instrument or incompatible accessory. Verify that the error appears before selecting clinical output settings and ask whether recent service, calibration, software update, or repair occurred.

steps.title: Attempt a Controlled Restart
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, allow shutdown to complete, reconnect only the power cord, and turn the unit back on. Do not repeatedly power cycle the ESU to force it into service.

steps.title: Determine Whether RF Calibration Is Required
steps.instructions: If Error E356 remains present after external checks, treat it as an RF calibration or service-level issue. Route the unit for bench evaluation and RF energy calibration using approved service equipment and facility procedure.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displaying Error E356 System Error Invalid RF Calibration. Unit was not considered safe for electrosurgical output.
CCR.Cause: Error was confirmed during startup. Power source, power cord, external accessories, foot pedal, instruments, and visible connectors were checked. Error persisted with all accessories removed, indicating likely RF calibration or internal generator service condition.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for bench evaluation and RF energy calibration. Unit not returned to clinical use pending successful calibration, testing, and service verification.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E415 Feature Not Enabled
description: Troubleshooting guide for Error E415 Feature Not Enabled on the Medtronic Valleylab FT10 when the Bipolar Resection feature is unavailable and cannot be used during ESU setup.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if the needed Bipolar Resection function is unavailable. Stop troubleshooting on the active patient setup and use another properly configured ESU if the feature is clinically required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Power on the Valleylab FT10 and confirm that the displayed message is Error: E415 Feature Not Enabled. Note when the error appears and whether other ESU modes function normally.

steps.title: Translate the Error Into the Real-World Problem
steps.instructions: Treat E415 as a feature availability or configuration issue first. Explain that the requested Bipolar Resection feature is not enabled on this device and should not be bypassed.

steps.title: Check That the Correct ESU Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm whether this specific FT10 is expected to support Bipolar Resection by checking inventory records, service history, configuration notes, or comparison with another known-enabled FT10.

steps.title: Check for User or Workflow Selection Error
steps.instructions: Confirm the procedure, requested mode, connected accessory, and workflow. Determine whether staff selected a feature unavailable on this ESU configuration.

steps.title: Check External Accessories and Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect bipolar accessories, foot pedals, handpieces, and cables. Confirm they are appropriate and fully seated, and compare the setup on another known-enabled FT10 if available.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: Power the FT10 off, remove it from clinical use, wait briefly, then power it back on and attempt to access Bipolar Resection again. If the feature is not enabled, E415 will reappear.

steps.title: Confirm Whether Feature Enablement Is Required
steps.instructions: If Bipolar Resection is required on this unit, contact Medtronic/Covidien or the appropriate vendor support channel with the model, serial number, configuration details, and exact error code.

CCR.Complaint: Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E415 Feature Not Enabled when staff attempted to use the Bipolar Resection feature.
CCR.Cause: Bipolar Resection feature was not enabled on this FT10 configuration. External accessories, workflow selection, and setup were checked, and the error remained associated with feature availability.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from use for Bipolar Resection workflow, advised department to use a properly configured ESU, and escalated for Medtronic/Covidien feature enablement verification.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E416 Unknown Instrument
description: Troubleshooting guide for Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Error E416 Unknown Instrument when the ESU does not recognize the connected instrument, preventing normal setup or activation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from patient use if the instrument is not recognized or the unit cannot confirm the connected accessory. Use another ESU or compatible instrument if the procedure is active or urgent. Do not continue activation attempts with an unrecognized instrument.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the displayed message is Error E416 Unknown Instrument. Power on the FT10, connect the reported instrument, and note whether the error appears immediately, during setup, or when activation is attempted.

steps.title: Check Instrument Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm the connected instrument is intended for use with the Valleylab FT10 and the selected output function. Verify the instrument type, connector style, compatibility, and correct port.

steps.title: Reseat the Instrument Connection
steps.instructions: Disconnect the instrument, inspect the connector for bent pins, damaged contacts, contamination, fluid, or debris, then reconnect the instrument firmly and confirm it seats fully.

steps.title: Replace the Instrument
steps.instructions: Remove the original instrument and connect a new or known-good compatible instrument of the same type. If the replacement instrument works, remove the failed instrument from use.

steps.title: Inspect the Original Instrument
steps.instructions: Check the instrument cable, connector, jaws, handle, and activation controls for visible damage, contamination, crushed cable sections, fluid exposure, insulation damage, or broken connector housing.

steps.title: Check Whether the Error Follows the Instrument or the ESU
steps.instructions: Test with a known-good compatible instrument. If the error follows the instrument, the instrument is the likely cause. If multiple known-good instruments show E416 on the same FT10, suspect an ESU-side recognition or connector issue.

steps.title: Inspect the FT10 Instrument Port Externally
steps.instructions: Inspect the affected instrument port for visible damage, looseness, debris, fluid residue, cracked plastic, or contamination. Do not open the ESU during basic clinical response.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Recheck
steps.instructions: Disconnect the instrument, power the FT10 off, wait briefly, power it back on, reconnect a known-compatible instrument, and observe whether E416 returns.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If replacing or reseating the instrument clears Error E416, document the failed instrument, confirm the FT10 recognizes the replacement, verify no active error remains, and return the ESU to service if it passes facility requirements.

CCR.Complaint: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E416 Unknown Instrument when staff connected an instrument. Instrument was not recognized and ESU setup could not continue normally.
CCR.Cause: Connected instrument was not recognized by the FT10. Error cleared after replacing the instrument with a known-compatible accessory, indicating a likely failed, damaged, incompatible, or unreadable instrument.
CCR.Resolution: Removed suspect instrument from use. Connected known-compatible replacement instrument. FT10 recognized instrument normally with no E416 error present. Unit returned to service after normal function was verified.

title: Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Electrosurgical Unit (ESU) - Error: E420 Usage Limit
description: Troubleshooting guide for Medtronic Valleylab FT10 Error E420 Usage Limit when an inserted device has already been used and is not accepted by the generator.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Valleylab FT10 from active patient use if the procedure cannot continue safely with the current instrument. Stop activation attempts, notify the surgical team, provide a replacement instrument or alternate ESU if needed, and do not continue attempting to use an instrument that the generator has rejected.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that the displayed error is Error E420 Usage Limit. Check whether it appears at instrument connection, during startup, when attempting activation, or after swapping instruments. Identify the specific inserted device involved.

steps.title: Translate the Error Into the Real-World Problem
steps.instructions: Recognize that the FT10 is rejecting the connected device due to usage-limit status. Treat the issue as an instrument acceptance problem unless it persists with multiple known-good devices.

steps.title: Check the Connected Instrument or Device
steps.instructions: Remove the instrument, verify compatibility, check packaging and labeling, confirm expiration status, and determine whether the device appears opened, reused, or previously used on another case.

steps.title: Replace With a New, Compatible Device
steps.instructions: Connect a new, unopened, compatible Medtronic-supported device. Make sure it is fully seated in the correct FT10 port and observe whether Error E420 clears.

steps.title: Check for Connection or Seating Issues
steps.instructions: If the error remains, disconnect and reconnect the device. Inspect the connector and FT10 port for bent pins, contamination, fluid, damage, debris, obstruction, or incomplete seating.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Device
steps.instructions: Connect another known-good device that has not been used and is appropriate for the FT10. Confirm whether the FT10 accepts it without Error E420.

steps.title: Check for Pattern Across Multiple Devices
steps.instructions: Determine whether the error follows one device or occurs with several new compatible devices. Remove a single rejected device from use, or remove the FT10 from service if multiple known-good devices cause E420.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medtronic Valleylab FT10 displayed Error E420 Usage Limit when instrument was connected. Generator would not accept inserted device for use.
CCR.Cause: Inserted device had already been used or was not accepted as valid for continued use. Error cleared after replacing with a new compatible device.
CCR.Resolution: Removed rejected device from use. Connected new compatible instrument, confirmed FT10 recognized device without Error E420, and returned ESU to service.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Error: CO2 ERROR
description: Troubleshooting guide for CO2 ERROR on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p when a failure prevents operation of the CO2 monitoring function.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the monitor is connected to a patient and CO2 monitoring is needed, transfer the patient to another working monitor or use an alternate approved monitoring method. Do not rely on a monitor with an active CO2 ERROR for ventilation monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed message is CO2 ERROR. Note whether the error appears at startup, after connecting the sampling line, during active monitoring, or intermittently during use. Check whether the CO2 waveform, EtCO2 value, respiration rate, or CO2-related alarms are missing or unreliable.

steps.title: Check the Patient and Clinical Condition First
steps.instructions: If the monitor is alarming during patient use, verify the patient’s respiratory status before assuming the monitor is wrong. Confirm whether the patient is breathing, ventilated, or connected properly to the breathing circuit or cannula.

steps.title: Inspect the CO2 Sampling Line or FilterLine
steps.instructions: Verify the sampling line is compatible, fully seated, not kinked, not crushed, not blocked, not wet, not contaminated, and properly connected to the patient or airway circuit. Replace the sampling line with a known-good compatible line if available.

steps.title: Check for Moisture, Secretions, or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the sampling line connection area and patient connection for moisture, secretions, or contamination. Replace contaminated accessories. Do not blow into the device or force fluid through the sampling path.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Remove the device from patient use first. Turn the monitor off, wait briefly, then restart it. Allow the unit to complete startup and observe whether CO2 ERROR returns.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Verify the monitor is connected to a known-good AC outlet or has adequate battery charge. Confirm the power cord, power supply, and battery indicators appear normal.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the monitor for cracked housing, damaged CO2 sampling port, bent or contaminated connector area, signs of fluid intrusion, evidence of being dropped, heat, odor, or unusual noise.

steps.title: Attempt Operation with Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: Use a known-good compatible sampling line and test the monitor in a controlled setup away from the patient if available. Confirm whether the CO2 waveform or values return.

steps.title: Stop if the Error Clears
steps.instructions: If CO2 ERROR clears after replacing the sampling line, correcting the connection, stabilizing power, or restarting the device, perform a basic functional check. Confirm CO2 waveform, EtCO2 value, alarm behavior, and that the error does not return during observation.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displaying CO2 ERROR. CO2 function unavailable and no reliable EtCO2 waveform or CO2 value available.
CCR.Cause: Verified CO2 ERROR during startup/use. Checked sampling line connection, known-good compatible sampling line, power source, and visible external condition. Error persisted, indicating likely internal CO2 function failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed monitor from service and labeled Out of Service. Device requires repair or bench evaluation by authorized service personnel before return to patient use.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Error: SpO2 ERROR
description: Troubleshooting guide for SpO2 ERROR on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p when a failure prevents the SpO2 function from operating.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the Capnostream 20p from patient use if SpO2 monitoring is required for active patient care. Transfer SpO2 monitoring to another approved monitor before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed message is SpO2 ERROR. Note whether the error appears during startup, after connecting an SpO2 sensor, during patient monitoring, or intermittently.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Sensor and Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the SpO2 sensor, extension cable, and connector for damage, bent pins, contamination, fluid intrusion, cuts, crushed cable sections, or loose fit. Confirm the accessories are compatible.

steps.title: Swap With a Known-Good SpO2 Sensor
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor and cable. If the error clears, remove the failed accessory from service and verify SpO2 operation.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Connector Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the SpO2 input port for broken plastic, bent contacts, debris, residue, looseness, or signs of impact damage.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Remove the device from patient use, power the monitor off, disconnect AC power, wait briefly, reconnect power, and restart the unit.

steps.title: Check Power and General Device Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm stable AC power and inspect the monitor for abnormal heat, odor, physical damage, cracked housing, liquid exposure, or other visible signs of failure.

steps.title: Confirm Whether Other Functions Still Operate
steps.instructions: Check whether CO2 monitoring, display operation, controls, and alarms appear normal. Determine whether the fault is isolated to SpO2 or part of a broader device failure.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Accessory-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the error clears with known-good accessories, treat it as accessory-related. If the error remains with known-good accessories and after restart, remove the monitor from service for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed SpO2 ERROR and would not provide SpO2 readings during use.
CCR.Cause: Checked SpO2 sensor, cable, connector, AC power, startup behavior, and general device condition. Error remained with known-good SpO2 accessories, indicating likely internal SpO2 function failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed monitor from patient use, labeled Out of Service, and sent for authorized repair/bench evaluation due to persistent SpO2 ERROR.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: No breath xxx seconds
description: Troubleshooting guide for the No breath xxx seconds alarm on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p when no valid breath has been detected for the displayed number of seconds.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat this as a patient safety alarm before assuming equipment failure. Immediately notify clinical staff and confirm the patient is being assessed. Do not silence, ignore, or dismiss the alarm until the patient’s breathing status has been confirmed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor displays No breath xxx seconds. Note how long the timer has been running and observe whether the alarm occurs continuously, intermittently, during transport, after accessory changes, or after patient movement.

steps.title: Confirm Clinical Response Before Equipment Checks
steps.instructions: Verify that clinical staff have assessed whether the patient is breathing normally. If the alarm is related to apnea, airway obstruction, respiratory depression, or ventilation failure, stop equipment troubleshooting and support the clinical response per facility policy.

steps.title: Check the CO₂ Sampling Line and Airway Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the CO₂ sampling line, cannula, airway adapter, or circuit connection for disconnection, loose fittings, kinks, blockage, moisture, secretions, incorrect placement, or a sampling line pulled away from the patient.

steps.title: Check the Patient Interface Placement
steps.instructions: For nasal or oral cannulas, confirm proper placement at the nares and mouth area. For intubated or ventilated patients, confirm the airway adapter is correctly installed and not bypassed, cracked, loose, or disconnected.

steps.title: Verify the Capnogram Waveform and EtCO₂ Reading
steps.instructions: Look for a consistent CO₂ waveform with each breath and confirm whether a reasonable EtCO₂ value is displayed. A missing or flat waveform may indicate no sampled breath, poor accessory placement, blockage, or device-side sampling issues.

steps.title: Replace the Sampling Line or CO₂ Accessory
steps.instructions: If placement appears correct but no valid waveform is present, replace the sampling line, cannula, or airway adapter with a known-good compatible accessory. Confirm breath detection, waveform, EtCO₂ readings, and alarm behavior return to normal.

steps.title: Check the Monitor-Side Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the sampling line is fully seated at the monitor connection. Inspect the inlet area for visible contamination, broken connector parts, moisture, or signs of damage without attempting internal disassembly.

steps.title: Check Alarm Settings and Patient Configuration
steps.instructions: Confirm the no-breath or apnea alarm setting is appropriate for the patient type and clinical area. Verify adult, pediatric, or neonatal configuration as applicable and confirm alarm settings are not being used to hide a real patient safety concern.

steps.title: Isolate Accessory or Patient Setup from Device Failure
steps.instructions: After the patient is safe, test the monitor with a known-good sampling line and appropriate department-approved setup. If the monitor still fails to detect valid breaths, remove it from service for repair evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed No breath xxx seconds alarm and staff reported no valid breath was being detected by the monitor.
CCR.Cause: Patient safety alarm verified. Clinical staff assessed patient. CO₂ sampling line and patient interface were checked for disconnection, blockage, moisture, poor placement, or accessory failure.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced sampling line and confirmed stable CO₂ waveform, EtCO₂ reading, and cleared alarm. Monitor returned to service after normal operation was verified.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: EtCO2 HIGH ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for an EtCO2 HIGH ALARM on a Medtronic Capnostream 20p capnography monitor when measured EtCO2 is above the upper alarm limit and may indicate patient compromise, alarm limit issues, accessory problems, setup concerns, or unreliable CO2 measurement.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the EtCO2 HIGH ALARM as a patient safety alarm first. Confirm the patient is being assessed immediately by clinical staff before troubleshooting the monitor.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor displays EtCO2 HIGH ALARM. Observe the EtCO2 value, waveform, upper alarm limit, and whether the alarm is continuous, intermittent, related to movement, transport, accessory change, or alarm limit adjustment.

steps.title: Confirm Clinical Response Before Equipment Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Make sure the responsible clinical team has evaluated the patient and ventilation setup. Do not remove monitoring until the patient is safe and alternate monitoring is available if needed.

steps.title: Check the Alarm Limit Setting
steps.instructions: Review the upper EtCO2 alarm limit and confirm it is appropriate for the patient population and care area. Authorized clinical staff should correct inappropriate limits per facility policy.

steps.title: Inspect the CO2 Sampling Line or Airway Adapter
steps.instructions: Check the sampling line, FilterLine, airway adapter, or cannula for kinks, blockage, moisture, contamination, loose connections, incorrect placement, or damage. Replace questionable disposable accessories.

steps.title: Check the Capnogram Waveform
steps.instructions: Observe whether the waveform is clinically reasonable, elevated, distorted, erratic, or inconsistent with the patient presentation. Compare waveform behavior with the displayed EtCO2 value.

steps.title: Check for Rebreathing or External Setup Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect only safe external setup items such as tubing routing, sampling line placement, airway adapter position, exhaust or scavenging setup if applicable, and obvious connection errors. Escalate clinical ventilation concerns to the responsible team.

steps.title: Compare With Another Monitor or Measurement Source if Needed
steps.instructions: If the patient is stable and staff suspect a monitor issue, compare the EtCO2 reading with another known-good capnography monitor, module, or approved measurement source using known-good accessories.

steps.title: Check Power, Connections, and Physical Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm stable power, secure external connections, and no visible damage, contamination, liquid intrusion, cracked ports, damaged connectors, impact damage, or overheating.

steps.title: Remove From Service if Equipment Fault Is Suspected
steps.instructions: If the alarm persists with a stable patient, correct alarm settings, known-good accessories, and comparison testing suggests unreliable readings, remove the device from service, label it Out of Service, and send it for bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p reported with EtCO2 HIGH ALARM. User stated EtCO2 value was above the upper alarm limit during patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Alarm confirmed. Clinical evaluation required first due to elevated EtCO2 reading. External checks included alarm limit review, sampling line inspection, FilterLine replacement, waveform observation, power check, and physical inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Verified alarm behavior and confirmed clinical staff response. Replaced questionable sampling accessory and checked external connections. If alarm cleared with correct setup and clinical staff approved, device returned to service. If alarm persisted with known-good accessories or questionable EtCO2 readings, device removed from service for bench evaluation.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: EtCO2 LOW ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for EtCO2 LOW ALARM conditions on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p, including patient safety checks, alarm limit review, sampling line inspection, accessory replacement, and escalation when the monitor cannot be trusted.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately assess the patient or confirm clinical staff are actively assessing the patient. Treat EtCO2 LOW ALARM as a patient safety alarm first. Move the patient to backup monitoring before removing the device from use if the monitor, sampling line, or EtCO2 value is suspected to be unreliable.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is displaying EtCO2 LOW ALARM. Observe the EtCO2 value, capnogram waveform, respiration rate, and alarm timing. Determine whether the alarm is continuous, intermittent, related to movement, during transport, or after a sampling line or accessory change.

steps.title: Confirm Whether the Reading Matches the Patient Condition
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff whether the low EtCO2 value matches the patient's airway status, ventilation status, perfusion, and treatment plan. Do not treat the alarm as an equipment issue until the patient has been clinically evaluated.

steps.title: Check the Lower EtCO2 Alarm Limit
steps.instructions: Review the configured lower EtCO2 alarm limit and compare it with the patient condition, clinical expectation, and care area protocol. Have clinical staff adjust alarm limits according to policy if needed.

steps.title: Inspect the CO2 Sampling Line or Airway Adapter
steps.instructions: Check the Microstream CO2 sampling line, cannula, or airway adapter for disconnection, loose fit, kinks, crushing, contamination, moisture, or secretions. Replace damaged, blocked, wet, contaminated, or questionable accessories.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Patient Interface Placement
steps.instructions: For nasal or oral sampling, confirm the cannula is positioned correctly. For intubated or ventilated patients, confirm the airway adapter is installed correctly and the breathing circuit is connected.

steps.title: Check for Leaks or Disconnections in the Breathing Circuit
steps.instructions: Inspect the breathing circuit, airway adapter, ET tube or trach connection, filter, and sample line connection points. Ask respiratory therapy or clinical staff to verify circuit integrity if needed.

steps.title: Evaluate the CO2 Waveform
steps.instructions: Review the capnogram waveform along with the numeric EtCO2 value. A weak, flat, irregular, or absent waveform may indicate poor sampling, apnea, shallow breathing, obstruction, disconnection, or a true clinical condition.

steps.title: Swap Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: If clinical staff suspect a false alarm and the patient is stable, replace the CO2 sampling line, cannula, or airway adapter with known-good compatible accessories. Recheck the EtCO2 value, waveform, respiration rate, and alarm status.

steps.title: Check the Monitor Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the CO2 sampling line is fully seated at the monitor connection. Inspect the inlet area for visible damage, contamination, cracked fittings, or loose connection points. Do not disassemble the monitor during clinical troubleshooting.

steps.title: Test the Monitor Away From the Patient
steps.instructions: After alternate monitoring is established, remove the monitor from patient use if the device is suspected to be unreliable. Use a known-good compatible CO2 sampling line and approved facility test method to verify monitor response.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed EtCO2 LOW ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff reported EtCO2 value below the lower alarm limit with concern for low or inaccurate CO2 reading.
CCR.Cause: Patient was assessed by clinical staff. Lower EtCO2 alarm limit, CO2 sampling line, patient interface placement, airway/circuit connections, and monitor inlet connection were checked. Issue was caused by a displaced/kinked CO2 sample line, which prevented accurate exhaled CO2 sampling.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced/repositioned CO2 sampling line and confirmed stable EtCO2 numeric value, visible waveform, and no recurring EtCO2 LOW ALARM. Device returned to service.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: RR HIGH ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for RR HIGH ALARM on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p when respiration rate is above the upper alarm limit due to patient condition, alarm settings, sampling issues, artifact, or possible device fault.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the RR HIGH ALARM as a clinical condition until proven otherwise. Notify clinical staff immediately, confirm the patient is being assessed, and do not troubleshoot the monitor on an active patient unless clinical staff confirm it is safe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is displaying RR HIGH ALARM. Observe whether the alarm occurs continuously, intermittently, during movement, during coughing or talking, or only during certain breathing patterns.

steps.title: Compare the Displayed Respiratory Rate to the Patient
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff visually assess the patient's breathing rate and pattern. Compare the displayed RR value to observed respirations and identify whether the reading matches the patient.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Limits Are Appropriate
steps.instructions: Review the upper RR alarm limit with clinical staff. Confirm the limit is appropriate for the patient type, care area, and clinical condition. Only authorized clinical staff should adjust alarm limits.

steps.title: Check the CO2 Sampling Line or Airway Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the sampling line, cannula, airway adapter, FilterLine, and tubing setup for loose connections, kinks, moisture, blockage, incorrect placement, or patient dislodgement.

steps.title: Inspect the Capnogram Waveform
steps.instructions: Look for a stable CO2 waveform. Check whether the monitor is detecting breaths consistently or counting noise, movement, talking, coughing, shallow breathing, or irregular waveform patterns as respirations.

steps.title: Check for Patient Movement or Artifact
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the alarm occurs during movement, agitation, coughing, talking, suctioning, repositioning, or transport. Correct setup or positioning issues when possible.

steps.title: Replace the Disposable Sampling Accessory if False Readings Are Suspected
steps.instructions: If the waveform is poor or the displayed RR does not match the patient, replace the sampling line, cannula, airway adapter, or compatible disposable accessory and verify whether the reading stabilizes.

steps.title: Check the Monitor Setup and Patient Type
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor configuration matches the patient population and intended use. Verify that neonatal, pediatric, or adult settings are appropriate where applicable.

steps.title: Test the Monitor Away From the Patient if a Device Issue Is Suspected
steps.instructions: If repeated false RR HIGH ALARM events are reported across multiple patients or accessories, remove the device from patient use and perform a bench check with known-good accessories.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p reported RR HIGH ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff stated respiration rate was above the upper alarm limit.
CCR.Cause: Verified RR HIGH ALARM. Patient assessment required first. Checked upper RR alarm limit, sampling line position, waveform quality, accessory condition, and movement artifact. Alarm appeared related to patient respiratory rate or alarm limit condition rather than confirmed device failure.
CCR.Resolution: Clinical staff notified for patient assessment. Sampling line and connections checked. Alarm limit reviewed by clinical staff. Monitor function verified with stable capnogram waveform. Device returned to service.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: RR LOW ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for RR LOW ALARM conditions on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p when respiration rate is below the lower alarm limit, not detected correctly, or may indicate patient respiratory depression.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the RR LOW ALARM as a possible patient emergency first. Check the patient immediately or confirm clinical staff are already responding. Move the patient to backup monitoring before evaluating the device if monitoring must continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor displays RR LOW ALARM. Observe whether the alarm occurs continuously, intermittently, after patient movement, or after changing the sampling line. Check for low displayed respiration rate, weak waveform, EtCO2 changes, or no breath detection.

steps.title: Confirm Clinical Response Before Equipment Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Ask nursing or respiratory staff whether the patient’s respiratory rate is actually low. Compare the reading with direct patient observation or another clinical assessment method. If the respiration rate is truly low, the monitor may be functioning correctly.

steps.title: Check the Alarm Limit Setting
steps.instructions: Review the lower respiration rate alarm limit and confirm it is appropriate for the patient population and care area. Have clinical staff adjust alarm limits only according to facility policy.

steps.title: Inspect the Sampling Line or Cannula
steps.instructions: Check the CO2 sampling line, nasal cannula, airway adapter, or FilterLine for disconnection, loose connection, kinks, moisture, occlusion, cracked tubing, incorrect accessory, or displacement from the patient.

steps.title: Check Patient Interface Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the cannula or airway sampling device is positioned correctly. For nasal/oral cannulas, verify the prongs and oral scoop are placed properly so exhaled breath reaches the sampling line consistently.

steps.title: Check the CO2 Waveform
steps.instructions: Observe the capnogram waveform. A reliable waveform should match patient breathing. Weak, intermittent, flatline, or irregular waveforms may indicate sampling line position, patient breathing pattern, obstruction, or accessory problems.

steps.title: Replace the Sampling Accessory
steps.instructions: Install a known-good compatible sampling line, cannula, or airway adapter. Allow the monitor time to detect breaths and update the respiration rate. If the alarm clears, the original accessory was likely the cause.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Use-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Review whether the alarm occurs during patient movement, oxygen delivery changes, nebulizer treatment, suctioning, poor cannula fit, mouth breathing, or very shallow breathing.

steps.title: Compare With Another Monitor or Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: If the patient is stable and clinical staff agree, compare the Capnostream reading with another monitoring method or known-good capnography setup. Determine whether the issue follows the patient/accessory setup or remains with the monitor.

steps.title: Remove From Service if the Alarm Appears False and Cannot Be Corrected
steps.instructions: If clinical staff confirm the patient is breathing normally, alarm limits are appropriate, and known-good accessories do not correct the false alarm, remove the monitor from use, label it Out of Service, and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed RR LOW ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff reported respiration rate was below the lower alarm limit or alarm appeared despite observed breathing.
CCR.Cause: Verified RR LOW ALARM. Clinical staff assessed patient condition. Checked lower RR alarm limit, sampling line, cannula placement, CO2 waveform, and accessory condition. Alarm was either consistent with patient respiratory status or suspected false after external causes were ruled out.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected sampling line/cannula placement, replaced CO2 sampling accessory, verified stable waveform and respiration rate reading, and returned monitor to service. If alarm persisted with known-good accessories, removed device from service and sent for bench evaluation.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: SpO2 HIGH ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Medtronic Capnostream 20p SpO2 HIGH ALARM when oxygen saturation is above the configured upper alarm limit or the reading may be falsely elevated due to sensor, site, cable, configuration, or monitor issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Check the patient immediately and notify clinical staff. Do not assume the alarm is equipment-related until the patient has been assessed. If continuous monitoring is required, move the patient to another working monitor before removing sensors, swapping cables, or removing the Capnostream 20p from service.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm that SpO2 HIGH ALARM is displayed on the monitor. Observe whether the alarm occurs continuously, intermittently, only with patient movement, or only on one sensor site. Check whether the SpO2 value is stable, unstable, or inconsistent with the patient condition.

steps.title: Confirm the Displayed SpO2 Value and Alarm Limit
steps.instructions: Compare the displayed SpO2 value to the configured upper alarm limit. Ask clinical staff whether the upper alarm limit is appropriate for the patient and care area. Do not change alarm limits without clinical approval.

steps.title: Check for Signs That This Is a Real Patient Condition
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff assess the patient, oxygen delivery, respiratory status, and therapy settings. Confirm whether the displayed SpO2 value matches the patient condition. Escalate any patient concern through clinical staff immediately.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Check that the sensor is correctly positioned on the approved monitoring site. Confirm the sensor is fully seated and not loose, twisted, misaligned, or partially detached. Reposition the sensor only when it is clinically appropriate and staff are aware.

steps.title: Check the Sensor Site
steps.instructions: Inspect for motion, poor perfusion, cold extremities, nail polish, artificial nails, moisture, dirt, or excess ambient light. Use a different approved site if clinical staff determine the current site is not reliable. Confirm the sensor site is clean, stable, and appropriate for SpO2 monitoring.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Cable and Sensor Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm that the sensor is securely connected to the SpO2 cable. Confirm that the cable is firmly connected to the monitor. Look for bent pins, loose connectors, cuts, crushed cable sections, contamination, or fluid exposure.

steps.title: Swap the SpO2 Sensor
steps.instructions: Replace the sensor with a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor if available. Retest on the same patient only if clinically appropriate. Use an approved simulator if available and patient testing is not appropriate. If this resolves the issue, remove the faulty sensor from use and return the monitor to service after confirming normal operation.

steps.title: Swap the SpO2 Cable if Applicable
steps.instructions: If replacing the sensor does not resolve the issue, try a known-good compatible SpO2 patient cable or interface cable. Inspect the original cable for physical damage, connector wear, or intermittent behavior. If this resolves the issue, replace the defective cable and document the finding.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Monitoring Source
steps.instructions: If available and clinically appropriate, compare the Capnostream 20p SpO2 reading with another monitor or pulse oximeter. If available, use an approved SpO2 simulator to compare expected values with displayed values.

steps.title: Check Monitor Configuration and Alarm Settings
steps.instructions: Review alarm limits, patient type or mode, alarm profiles, and unit-specific settings. Confirm settings with clinical staff before making any changes. Verify that alarm limits match the intended clinical use.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor for Physical or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Check for fluid intrusion, cracked housing, damaged connectors, contamination, signs of impact, overheating, unusual sounds, or abnormal odor. Look for additional SpO2-related error messages or system faults. Confirm the monitor is being used in an appropriate environment and is not exposed to obvious interference or damage.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check
steps.instructions: Use an approved SpO2 simulator if available. Verify that the monitor reads appropriately. Verify that the monitor alarms when simulated values exceed the configured upper limit. Confirm that readings and alarms return to expected behavior after testing.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p reported SpO2 HIGH ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff reported SpO2 reading above the configured upper alarm limit.
CCR.Cause: Alarm was verified. Patient assessment was required first. Sensor placement, sensor site, SpO2 cable connection, alarm limits, and accessory condition were checked. Alarm was traced to incorrect upper alarm limit setting or accessory-related reading instability.
CCR.Resolution: Verified alarm behavior with clinical staff, inspected SpO2 sensor and cable, confirmed alarm settings, tested with known-good accessories or simulator as available, and returned device to service after normal function was confirmed. Device was removed from service for repair if false alarm persisted.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: SpO2 LOW ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Medtronic Capnostream 20p SpO2 LOW ALARM when oxygen saturation is below the configured lower alarm limit or falsely reading low due to sensor, cable, signal quality, settings, or monitor connection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately confirm the patient has been assessed by clinical staff. Treat the low SpO2 alarm as a possible patient emergency first. Move the patient to a backup monitor or alternate validated SpO2 source before troubleshooting if continuous monitoring is required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed alarm is SpO2 LOW ALARM. Observe whether it occurs immediately after sensor placement, during movement, intermittently, continuously, or only with one sensor or cable. Check SpO2 value, pulse rate, and pleth waveform quality.

steps.title: Confirm the Alarm Limit Setting
steps.instructions: Review the lower SpO2 alarm limit and confirm it is appropriate for the care area and facility policy. Do not change alarm limits unless directed by authorized clinical staff.

steps.title: Check Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Inspect SpO2 sensor placement, alignment, contact, site selection, and ambient light exposure. Poor sensor placement can cause unstable or falsely low SpO2 readings.

steps.title: Check Patient and Environmental Signal Factors
steps.instructions: With clinical staff involved, consider motion, low perfusion, cold extremities, nail polish, artificial nails, edema, or sensor site compression. Confirm whether another monitoring source agrees with the low reading.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor and Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the sensor and extension cable for damaged insulation, bent pins, loose connectors, contamination, crushed cable sections, or intermittent connection when moved. Disconnect and reconnect firmly.

steps.title: Swap the SpO2 Sensor or Cable
steps.instructions: Replace the SpO2 sensor and extension cable with known-good compatible accessories. If the alarm clears and readings stabilize, document the defective accessory and return the monitor to service after verification.

steps.title: Compare Against Another Monitoring Source
steps.instructions: Compare the Capnostream 20p SpO2 reading with another validated monitor or spot-check device when available. A large difference may indicate sensor placement, accessory, signal quality, configuration, or device-related issues.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor Connector
steps.instructions: Inspect the SpO2 input connector for loose fit, damaged pins, contamination, cracked housing, corrosion, or signs of liquid intrusion. Remove the monitor from service if connector damage is suspected.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Operational Check
