steps.instructions: Using approved test equipment or a known-good setup, verify stable SpO2 value, pulse rate, waveform, and expected alarm behavior. Return the monitor to service only if readings and alarms are reliable.

CCR.Complaint: Medtronic Capnostream 20p displayed SpO2 LOW ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff reported SpO2 reading was below the lower alarm limit.
CCR.Cause: Checked alarm limit, sensor placement, patient signal factors, SpO2 sensor, extension cable, and monitor connector. Alarm appeared related to poor SpO2 sensor/cable connection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced defective SpO2 sensor/cable, verified stable SpO2 reading, pulse rate, and waveform with known-good accessory, confirmed alarm cleared, and returned monitor to service.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: PULSE RATE HIGH ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Capnostream 20p PULSE RATE HIGH ALARM when the pulse rate is above the upper alarm limit or may be falsely elevated due to settings, sensor placement, motion artifact, cable issues, poor signal quality, or monitor fault.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately confirm the patient condition with clinical staff. Treat the alarm as a patient safety alarm first. If monitoring must continue, move the patient to a backup monitor before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the Capnostream 20p displays PULSE RATE HIGH ALARM. Determine whether the alarm is continuous, intermittent, movement-related, sensor-related, or associated with poor SpO2 signal quality.

steps.title: Confirm the Pulse Rate Against Another Source
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff compare the displayed pulse rate with another valid source such as manual pulse assessment, bedside monitor, ECG monitor, or another pulse oximeter. If the high pulse rate is clinically confirmed, stop equipment troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Alarm Limit Settings
steps.instructions: Verify the upper pulse rate alarm limit is appropriate for the patient and care area. Confirm whether the limit was changed, set too low, or carried over from a previous patient setup.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Check that the SpO2 sensor is correctly placed and secure. Look for loose placement, wrong sensor size, poor contact, slipping, excessive pressure, or poor perfusion at the sensor site.

steps.title: Check for Motion Artifact
steps.instructions: Observe whether the pulse rate spikes during movement, tremors, shivering, transport, or staff handling. Secure the sensor and cable if appropriate and confirm whether readings stabilize.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Cable and Connections
steps.instructions: Check the SpO2 cable connection at the monitor and sensor. Look for loose connections, bent pins, cable damage, intermittent readings, or contamination in the connector.

steps.title: Swap the SpO2 Sensor or Cable
steps.instructions: Use a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor and cable. If the alarm clears and readings stabilize, replace the faulty accessory and complete a functional check.

steps.title: Check Signal Quality and Waveform
steps.instructions: Review the SpO2 waveform, pulse indicator, or signal quality indicator if available. Look for weak signal, erratic waveform, dropouts, or sudden pulse rate jumps.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Verify SpO2 and pulse rate display properly, alarm limits are appropriate, alarm indicators function, and readings remain stable with known-good accessories.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed PULSE RATE HIGH ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff reported pulse rate reading above the upper alarm limit.
CCR.Cause: Patient assessment was required first. Equipment checks found possible false high pulse rate alarm related to SpO2 sensor placement, motion artifact, cable connection, signal quality, or alarm limit configuration.
CCR.Resolution: Confirmed reported alarm, verified alarm settings, inspected SpO2 sensor placement, checked cable connection, and tested with known-good SpO2 accessory. Device was returned to service after readings stabilized or removed from service for repair evaluation if the false alarm persisted.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: PULSE RATE LOW ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Capnostream 20p PULSE RATE LOW ALARM, including patient safety response, alarm limit review, SpO2 sensor checks, accessory inspection, and escalation if the alarm persists.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Respond to the alarm as a possible real patient condition first. Notify clinical staff immediately. If the patient is unstable or the alarm appears clinically valid, stop troubleshooting and allow clinical staff to manage the patient. Move the patient to another reliable monitor before equipment troubleshooting if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed alarm is PULSE RATE LOW ALARM. Observe whether the alarm occurs at startup, during monitoring, intermittently, during movement, only with one sensor, only with one cable, or only on one patient. Compare the displayed pulse rate with another approved source when clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Limits and Configuration
steps.instructions: Check the pulse rate lower alarm limit setting. Verify that the alarm limit is appropriate for the patient population and care area. Do not change alarm limits unless authorized by clinical staff and facility policy.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Sensor Placement
steps.instructions: Inspect the SpO2 sensor site. Confirm the sensor is properly positioned, fully seated, and aligned. Check for loose placement, poor contact, ambient light exposure, nail polish, artificial nails, cold extremities, edema, or excessive motion.

steps.title: Check Patient Motion and Perfusion Conditions
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the alarm occurs during movement, shivering, transport, repositioning, or poor peripheral circulation. Check whether the sensor is on a cold, poorly perfused, or compressed extremity. Staff may relocate the sensor if clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor and Cable
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient troubleshooting if equipment inspection is needed. Inspect the SpO2 sensor, adapter cable, and connector for loose connection, bent pins, cracked housing, frayed cable, contamination, fluid intrusion, intermittent readings, or incompatible accessories. Replace with known-good compatible accessories if available.

steps.title: Check the Monitor Connection Port
steps.instructions: Inspect the SpO2 input port for damaged pins, looseness, contamination, broken plastic, or poor connector fit. Remove the monitor from service if port damage is found.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: After the patient is safe, test the monitor with a known-good SpO2 sensor and cable. Use an approved simulator if available or a controlled known-good setup according to department policy. Verify stable pulse rate readings without recurring alarms.

steps.title: Check for Pattern or Repeat Failure
steps.instructions: Review whether the alarm has been reported repeatedly on the same monitor, with multiple patients, or across different sensors. Repeated false alarms after accessory replacement suggest a device-side issue and require removal from service.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p reported PULSE RATE LOW ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff reported displayed pulse rate was below the lower alarm limit.
CCR.Cause: Patient safety was prioritized. Alarm limit configuration was checked. SpO2 sensor placement, patient motion, perfusion conditions, sensor cable, connector, and monitor input port were inspected. Alarm appeared related to poor SpO2 sensor contact and intermittent cable movement.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned SpO2 sensor and tested monitor with known-good compatible SpO2 cable. Pulse rate reading remained stable and PULSE RATE LOW ALARM did not return during verification. Device returned to service.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: FiCO2 RATE HIGH ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for a FiCO2 RATE HIGH ALARM on a Medtronic Capnostream 20p capnography monitor, including patient safety checks, sampling line inspection, accessory review, alarm setting verification, and escalation steps.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Check the patient immediately and involve clinical staff if the alarm is active, repeated, or associated with respiratory distress, abnormal EtCO2, abnormal SpO2, abnormal pulse rate, or poor waveform quality. Move the patient to backup monitoring if needed before troubleshooting the device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is FiCO2 RATE HIGH ALARM. Observe when the alarm occurs and check whether FiCO2 readings, EtCO2 values, respiratory rate, SpO2, pulse rate, or waveform quality are abnormal.

steps.title: Confirm the Patient Is Not Actively Rebreathing CO2
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff evaluate the airway, breathing circuit, ventilation status, and patient condition. Do not assume the monitor is faulty until patient and ventilation causes have been addressed or ruled out.

steps.title: Check the Sampling Line or FilterLine Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the CO2 sampling line for loose connections, kinks, blockage, moisture, secretions, contamination, damaged connectors, or incorrect accessory type. Replace with a known-good compatible sampling line if there is any doubt.

steps.title: Inspect the Patient Interface or Airway Adapter
steps.instructions: Check the cannula, airway adapter, mask connection, or sampling point. Confirm it is correctly placed, not blocked, not contaminated, not connected backward, and not positioned where exhaled gas can be repeatedly sampled as inspired gas.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Setup-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Look for oxygen masks trapping exhaled gas, cannula displacement, covered airway areas, tubing under bedding, poor ventilation around the sampling point, or improper oxygen delivery setup.

steps.title: Review Alarm Limits and Configuration
steps.instructions: Verify the FiCO2 upper alarm limit is appropriate for the care area and patient population. Do not silence, widen, or disable alarm limits just to stop the alarm unless authorized clinical staff confirms the setting is inappropriate and facility policy allows adjustment.

steps.title: Compare With Other Patient Parameters
steps.instructions: Review EtCO2, respiration rate, SpO2, pulse rate, and waveform quality. Use the full patient monitoring picture to determine whether the alarm is likely clinical, setup-related, accessory-related, or equipment-related.

steps.title: Test With Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: If clinically safe, replace the sampling line, cannula, airway adapter, or related accessory with known-good compatible parts. Verify whether the alarm clears and CO2 readings stabilize.

steps.title: Remove the Monitor From Service if the Alarm Appears False and Persists
steps.instructions: If the FiCO2 RATE HIGH ALARM continues with known-good accessories, correct setup, appropriate alarm limits, and no clinical explanation, remove the monitor from service, label it Out of Service, and send it for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed FiCO2 RATE HIGH ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff reported inspired CO2 reading above the upper alarm limit.
CCR.Cause: Patient safety confirmed with clinical staff. Sampling line, airway interface, alarm limit, waveform quality, and accessory condition checked. Alarm appeared related to sampling setup/accessory condition.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced CO2 sampling accessory and verified stable readings and waveform. Alarm cleared during testing. Monitor returned to service. If alarm had persisted, unit would have been removed from service for bench evaluation.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: IPI LOW ALARM
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Medtronic Capnostream 20p IPI LOW ALARM, including patient safety response, parameter review, accessory checks, and escalation guidance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat IPI LOW ALARM as a patient condition until proven otherwise. Have clinical staff assess the patient before equipment troubleshooting. Use backup monitoring if readings are unreliable.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is IPI LOW ALARM and document when it occurs, whether it is continuous or intermittent, and whether it appears during movement, sedation, transport, or accessory repositioning.

steps.title: Review Other Monitored Parameters
steps.instructions: Check EtCO2, respiration rate, SpO2, pulse rate, waveform quality, and trend data to determine whether the low IPI alarm is supported by other abnormal values.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Limits and Patient Setup
steps.instructions: Verify the IPI lower alarm limit and patient configuration are appropriate for the patient population and clinical area. Do not change limits unless directed by clinical staff and facility policy.

steps.title: Check the CO2 Sampling Setup
steps.instructions: Inspect the cannula, sampling line, airway adapter, or FilterLine for kinks, loose connections, blockage, moisture, disconnection, or incorrect placement.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Sensor and Patient Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect SpO2 sensor placement, sensor size, cable connection, low perfusion, patient movement, nail polish, cold extremity, and sensor damage. Reposition or replace the sensor if needed.

steps.title: Check Cables, Accessories, and Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm CO2 and SpO2 accessories are fully seated and not damaged. Swap suspect accessories with known-good parts when available.

steps.title: Assess for Motion or Environmental Causes
steps.instructions: Look for patient movement, transport vibration, cable tension, cannula displacement, or poor sensor contact. Secure cables and sampling lines and confirm readings stabilize.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p reported IPI LOW ALARM during patient monitoring. Staff reported low IPI indication and requested evaluation of monitor and accessories.
CCR.Cause: Patient status alarm required clinical evaluation. CO2 sampling line and SpO2 sensor were checked for placement, connection, damage, and signal quality. Alarm condition was supported by monitored parameters / caused by unstable accessory signal / could not be duplicated after accessory correction.
CCR.Resolution: Patient care team evaluated patient. Verified alarm behavior, reviewed EtCO2, respiration rate, SpO2, pulse rate, and IPI trend. Checked sampling line, SpO2 sensor, cables, and alarm settings. Corrected accessory issue and returned monitor to service, or removed monitor from service for bench evaluation if alarm persisted.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: PULSE NOT FOUND
description: Troubleshooting guide for the PULSE NOT FOUND alarm on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p when the monitor cannot detect a pulse from the SpO2/pulse monitoring function.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the PULSE NOT FOUND alarm as a possible patient condition until clinical staff confirms otherwise. Have clinical staff immediately assess the patient and move the patient to backup monitoring if pulse detection is unreliable.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Confirm that the Capnostream 20p is displaying PULSE NOT FOUND and identify whether the alarm is continuous, intermittent, related to movement, related to sensor placement, or specific to one patient or accessory.

steps.title: Reposition the SpO2/Pulse Sensor
steps.instructions: Remove and reapply the sensor using proper placement practice. Confirm correct alignment, good contact, and that the sensor is not too loose, too tight, folded, or slipping.

steps.title: Check the Patient Site and Signal Quality
steps.instructions: Check for poor perfusion, cold extremities, edema, tremors, excessive movement, nail polish, artificial nails, or other site-related causes. Try another appropriate sensor site if allowed.

steps.title: Inspect the Sensor and Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the sensor and extension cable are fully connected. Inspect for looseness, bent pins, damage, contamination, strain, pinching, or intermittent connection.

steps.title: Swap the Sensor or Accessory
steps.instructions: Test with a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor and cable. If the alarm clears with the replacement accessory, remove the failed accessory from use.

steps.title: Compare With Another Monitor if Needed
steps.instructions: If the patient is stable but the alarm continues, compare with another approved monitor or pulse oximeter using known-good accessories to determine whether the issue follows the patient/accessory or remains with the Capnostream unit.

steps.title: Confirm Stable Operation Before Return to Use
steps.instructions: Verify that the alarm clears, pulse detection is stable, and the monitor operates normally with known-good compatible accessories before returning it to service.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed PULSE NOT FOUND alarm and staff reported no detectable pulse reading from the SpO2/pulse sensor.
CCR.Cause: Patient safety confirmed by clinical staff. Sensor placement, patient site, cable connection, and accessory condition were checked. Alarm cleared after SpO2 sensor was repositioned/replaced, or device-side fault suspected if alarm persisted with known-good accessories.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned SpO2 sensor, inspected cable connection, and tested with known-good compatible accessory. Verified stable pulse detection and returned monitor to service, or removed device from service for repair if alarm persisted.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: SpO2 SENSOR NOT ON PATIENT
description: Troubleshooting guide for the SpO2 SENSOR NOT ON PATIENT alarm on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p capnography monitor when the sensor is off the patient or the monitor cannot detect proper SpO2 sensor application.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether SpO2 monitoring is clinically required. Do not leave the patient unmonitored if oxygen saturation monitoring is needed. Transfer monitoring to another device before troubleshooting if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor displays SpO2 SENSOR NOT ON PATIENT. Observe whether the alarm occurs immediately, intermittently, during motion, after repositioning, or only with a specific sensor or cable.

steps.title: Check Sensor Placement on the Patient
steps.instructions: Confirm the SpO2 sensor is fully seated on the approved site and that the emitter and detector are aligned across the tissue. Check for loose, twisted, or partially detached sensor placement.

steps.title: Check Patient-Site Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect for poor perfusion, cold extremities, excessive motion, nail polish, artificial nails, dirt, moisture, lotion, thick tissue, or poor alignment. Move the sensor to a better site if clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor
steps.instructions: Check the sensor for damage, contamination, worn adhesive, cracked plastic, bent contacts, damaged wiring, or poor condition. Replace with a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Cable and Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the sensor is fully connected to the patient cable or monitor input. Inspect for looseness, bent pins, contamination, damage, or poor seating. Disconnect and reconnect firmly, then test with a known-good cable if available.

steps.title: Check for Motion or Intermittent Contact
steps.instructions: Gently move the sensor cable and observe whether the alarm appears or clears. Secure the cable to reduce pulling on the sensor.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Settings and Clinical Context
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are not mistaking this sensor detection alarm for a high or low SpO2 limit alarm. Review whether the patient should remain on SpO2 monitoring and whether the sensor type is appropriate.

steps.title: Test the Monitor With a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: Test with a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor, known-good patient cable, and suitable test subject or approved SpO2 simulator if available.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If sensor placement, sensor replacement, cable correction, or site change clears the alarm, document the correction and return the monitor to service.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed SpO2 SENSOR NOT ON PATIENT alarm. Staff reported the monitor was not reading SpO2 while the sensor was applied.
CCR.Cause: SpO2 sensor was not properly seated on the patient, causing the monitor to detect the sensor as off patient.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned SpO2 sensor, verified proper placement and cable connection. SpO2 reading returned and alarm cleared. Device remained in service.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: FILTERLINE BLOCKAGE
description: Troubleshooting guide for the FILTERLINE BLOCKAGE alarm on a Medtronic Capnostream 20p capnography monitor, including FilterLine routing, patient connection checks, accessory replacement, and escalation steps.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being properly assessed and monitored. If CO2 monitoring is needed for active patient care and the Capnostream 20p is not providing reliable readings, move the patient to a backup monitor or alternate monitoring method first.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows FILTERLINE BLOCKAGE. Ask staff when the alarm appeared and whether it is constant or intermittent.

steps.title: Check the FilterLine Routing
steps.instructions: Inspect the full FilterLine from the monitor to the patient connection. Look for kinks, tight bends, pinched tubing, crushed sections, or tubing trapped under equipment.

steps.title: Disconnect and Reconnect the FilterLine
steps.instructions: Disconnect the FilterLine from the monitor connection and reconnect it securely. Confirm the connection is fully seated and not loose, partially inserted, cross-threaded, or damaged.

steps.title: Inspect the Airway Adapter or Patient Connection
steps.instructions: Check the airway adapter, nasal cannula, or sampling connection for moisture, mucus, secretions, medication residue, contamination, or physical obstruction.

steps.title: Replace the FilterLine
steps.instructions: Install a new compatible FilterLine if the alarm remains. Do not reuse a line that is wet, contaminated, kinked, crushed, clogged, or damaged.

steps.title: Check for Use-Related or Environmental Causes
steps.instructions: Confirm the FilterLine is not routed where it can be pinched during bed movement, patient movement, transport, or repositioning.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good FilterLine
steps.instructions: If available, test the monitor with a new or known-good compatible FilterLine and an appropriate test setup according to facility practice.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed FILTERLINE BLOCKAGE alarm during use and EtCO2 monitoring was interrupted.
CCR.Cause: FilterLine was kinked at the patient connection, restricting CO2 sampling.
CCR.Resolution: Inspected FilterLine routing, reconnected the FilterLine, checked the airway adapter, replaced the FilterLine, and verified normal CO2 sampling with no recurring FILTERLINE BLOCKAGE alarm.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - Alarm: BATTERY LOW
description: Troubleshooting guide for the BATTERY LOW alarm on a Medtronic Capnostream 20p capnography monitor when battery charge is low and the monitor may shut down soon.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Capnostream 20p is actively monitoring a patient, connect the monitor to AC power immediately if safe and available. If AC power is not available or the BATTERY LOW alarm cannot be corrected quickly, move the patient to another working monitor before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is BATTERY LOW. Ask when the alarm appeared and check whether it occurred during battery-only operation, transport, after unplugging the power cord, after a power outage, or while connected to AC power.

steps.title: Connect the Monitor to AC Power
steps.instructions: Plug the monitor into a known working hospital-grade AC outlet. Confirm the power cord is fully seated at the wall outlet and monitor. Check for AC power or charging indication and allow the battery to begin recovering.

steps.title: Check the AC Outlet
steps.instructions: If the alarm remains after connecting AC power, try a different known working outlet. Check for a dead, switched, loose, damaged, or tripped outlet according to facility practice.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord for cuts, bent prongs, damaged insulation, loose fit, overheating, or incorrect cord type. Replace or test with a known good compatible cord if allowed by facility policy.

steps.title: Check for Charging or AC Power Indication
steps.instructions: Verify that the monitor recognizes AC power and that the battery level begins increasing after remaining connected. If the monitor is plugged in but does not charge, remove it from service for further evaluation.

steps.title: Allow Time for Battery Recovery
steps.instructions: If the battery was deeply discharged, leave the monitor connected to AC power and observe whether the battery level improves and the BATTERY LOW alarm clears. Do not return it for transport use until charging is verified.

steps.title: Check for Repeated or Premature Battery Low Alarms
steps.instructions: Ask whether the alarm occurs shortly after removing the monitor from AC power or during normal transport use after charging. A battery that drops quickly may be weak, aged, or unable to hold charge.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical or Environmental Issues
steps.instructions: Check for liquid damage, cracked housing, damaged power inlet, loose connectors, excessive heat, burning smell, or other unsafe conditions. Remove the monitor from service if damage is found.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If AC power clears the alarm and the monitor charges normally, document the low battery or unplugged AC condition. Verify normal monitoring display, alarm behavior, power indication, and charging status before returning the device to service.

CCR.Complaint: Capnostream 20p displayed BATTERY LOW alarm. Staff reported battery charge was low and the monitor may shut down soon.
CCR.Cause: Monitor was operating on battery power with low remaining charge. AC power was not connected. Power cord and outlet were inspected and no damage was found.
CCR.Resolution: Connected monitor to known working AC outlet, verified secure power cord connection, confirmed AC/charging indication, and observed BATTERY LOW alarm cleared. Device returned to service after normal operation was verified.

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - CO2 ERROR / CO₂ FUNCTION FAILURE
description: Troubleshooting CO2 ERROR or loss of CO₂ monitoring before removing the Capnostream 20p from service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Capnostream 20p is actively monitoring a patient and CO₂ monitoring is needed, move the patient to another working monitor or use an approved alternate monitoring method before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient remains safely monitored. Why it matters: A CO₂ function failure may prevent reliable EtCO₂, respiration rate, waveform, and apnea monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error
steps.instructions: Confirm that the displayed message is CO2 ERROR or that the CO₂ function is unavailable. Note whether the issue appears during startup, after connecting a sampling line, during monitoring, or intermittently. Expected outcome: The exact failure condition is confirmed. Why it matters: This separates a true CO₂ function failure from a simple sampling line, setup, or accessory problem.

steps.title: Check Whether CO₂ Values and Waveform Are Missing
steps.instructions: Look for a missing capnogram waveform, dashed EtCO₂ value, missing respiration rate, or disabled CO₂ alarms. Expected outcome: The scope of the CO₂ failure is understood. Why it matters: A complete loss of CO₂ data points more strongly toward a device or module issue.

steps.title: Confirm the Monitor Is Powered Correctly
steps.instructions: Connect the Capnostream 20p to a known-good AC outlet. Verify the power cord is fully seated and that the monitor powers on normally without low battery or power-related alarms. Expected outcome: The monitor runs from stable AC power. Why it matters: Low power, battery issues, or unstable AC connection can cause abnormal startup or functional errors.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off, wait briefly, then restart it while connected to AC power. Observe whether the CO2 ERROR returns immediately during startup. Expected outcome: The monitor starts normally and the CO₂ function becomes available. If resolved: Document the reset and return the device to service only after functional checks pass. Why it matters: Some errors may clear after a controlled restart, but recurring errors need further evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect the CO₂ Sampling Line Connection
steps.instructions: Remove and reconnect the sampling line. Make sure it is fully seated, not cross-threaded, loose, damaged, kinked, wet, contaminated, or blocked. Expected outcome: The line connects securely and allows proper sampling. Why it matters: A bad or obstructed sampling line can mimic CO₂ monitoring failure or prevent valid breath detection.

steps.title: Replace the Sampling Line With a Known-Good Compatible Line
steps.instructions: Use a new or verified compatible Microstream sampling line appropriate for the patient setup. Expected outcome: CO₂ sampling resumes, waveform appears, and EtCO₂/respiration rate display normally. If resolved: The original sampling line was likely defective, blocked, expired, contaminated, or incompatible. Stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check for Moisture, Contamination, or Visible Damage at the CO₂ Inlet
steps.instructions: Inspect the sampling port area externally. Do not insert tools into the port unless following approved site procedure. Look for liquid exposure, residue, cracks, loose fit, or physical damage. Expected outcome: The inlet appears clean, dry, and physically intact. Why it matters: Moisture or contamination can affect the sampling system and may require bench service.

steps.title: Allow Warm-Up or Initialization to Complete
steps.instructions: After startup, give the monitor time to complete its normal initialization. Watch whether the error clears or remains active. Expected outcome: CO₂ function becomes available after normal startup. Why it matters: Calling a failure too early can lead to unnecessary repair evaluation.

steps.title: Test With a Simple Functional Check
steps.instructions: If your site procedure allows it, connect a known-good sampling line and verify that the monitor detects CO₂ using an approved test source or simulator. Do not use patient breath testing unless permitted by your facility policy. Expected outcome: A stable waveform and EtCO₂ value appear. If resolved: The monitor passes basic CO₂ function testing and may be returned to service after documentation. Why it matters: A functional check confirms whether the CO₂ system is actually operating.

steps.title: Check Alarm and Parameter Behavior After CO₂ Returns
steps.instructions: Confirm that EtCO₂, respiration rate, waveform, and apnea/no-breath alarm behavior are available and not disabled by setup or configuration. Expected outcome: CO₂ monitoring and related alarms operate as expected. Why it matters: Restoring a waveform is not enough if the related monitoring and alarms are still unavailable.

steps.title: Repeat the Failure Check After Movement
steps.instructions: Gently move the monitor, power cord, and sampling line connection while observing the screen. Do not stress the connectors. Expected outcome: The CO2 ERROR does not return. Why it matters: Intermittent connector or internal sampling system issues may only appear during movement or transport.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Using the Monitor if CO₂ Failure Repeats
steps.instructions: If CO2 ERROR returns after power cycling, accessory replacement, and basic external checks, remove the unit from service. Expected outcome: The device is not used for patient monitoring until evaluated. Why it matters: A recurring CO₂ function failure can compromise respiratory monitoring and alarm reliability.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported CO2 ERROR on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p and stated that EtCO₂ and respiration rate were not displaying during monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Verified CO2 ERROR during startup and confirmed no CO₂ waveform using a known-good sampling line and stable AC power.
CCR.Resolution: Removed monitor from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent unit for bench evaluation due to unresolved CO₂ function failure.

helpfulDetails: Whether the unit was on AC power or battery
Outlet tested or alternate outlet used
Whether error occurred at startup or during monitoring
Sampling line replaced with known-good compatible line
CO₂ inlet inspected for damage, moisture, or contamination
EtCO₂ value present, dashed, unstable, or absent
Alarm behavior before and after troubleshooting
Power behavior during startup, battery use, and AC connection
Environmental factors such as liquid exposure, transport damage, contamination, or recent cleaning
Indicator lights, alarms, or display behavior observed during startup
Any unusual sound from sampling pump area
Any unusual heat, smell, liquid exposure, or physical damage
Final device status: returned to service, removed from service, or sent for repair

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - SpO2 ERROR / Pulse Oximetry Function Failure
description: Troubleshooting SpO2 ERROR when the Capnostream 20p cannot reliably operate the pulse oximetry function.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Capnostream 20p is actively monitoring a patient and SpO2 monitoring is required, transfer the patient to another working monitor before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient remains continuously monitored by a reliable device. Why it matters: An SpO2 function failure may remove oxygen saturation and pulse rate monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows SpO2 ERROR or that the SpO2 function is unavailable, blank, dashed out, or not producing a valid reading. Expected outcome: The reported issue is confirmed before parts are swapped or service is escalated. If the error is no longer present: Reconnect accessories and observe the device before returning it to service.

steps.title: Check Whether the Issue Is With the Monitor, Sensor, or Patient Cable
steps.instructions: Disconnect the SpO2 sensor and patient cable from the monitor. Inspect the connector and port for bent pins, debris, fluid, cracking, or looseness. Expected outcome: The connector and port are clean, dry, undamaged, and properly seated. If damage or contamination is found: Remove the device or accessory from service as appropriate.

steps.title: Reseat the SpO2 Cable and Sensor
steps.instructions: Reconnect the SpO2 cable firmly to the Capnostream 20p and attach a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the sensor and begins displaying SpO2 and pulse rate. If this resolves the issue: Document the loose connection or sensor replacement and stop.

steps.title: Swap With Known-Good Accessories
steps.instructions: Test the monitor using a known-good SpO2 patient cable and known-good compatible sensor. Expected outcome: If the original cable or sensor was faulty, the error clears with known-good accessories. If the error clears: Remove the failed accessory from service and replace it.

steps.title: Check for Accessory Mismatch
steps.instructions: Confirm the sensor and cable are compatible with the Capnostream 20p configuration being used. Do not assume all pulse oximetry sensors are interchangeable. Expected outcome: Only correct, approved SpO2 accessories are used. Why it matters: Incorrect sensors or cables can cause detection errors, unreliable readings, or SpO2 function failure.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor Safely
steps.instructions: With the device off the patient, power the monitor down, disconnect AC power briefly, reconnect AC power, and restart the unit. Expected outcome: The monitor completes startup without the SpO2 error returning. If this resolves the issue: Continue observation and document the reset.

steps.title: Check Whether the Error Appears During Startup or Only After Connecting Accessories
steps.instructions: Start the monitor with no SpO2 accessories connected, then connect the cable and sensor one at a time. Expected outcome: The timing of the error helps separate accessory-related issues from internal SpO2 hardware failure. If the error appears only with one cable or sensor: Replace that accessory. If the error appears with no accessories connected: Suspect an internal monitor fault.

steps.title: Inspect for Fluid Intrusion, Impact Damage, or Contamination
steps.instructions: Look for signs of dropped equipment, cracked housing, damaged SpO2 port, sticky residue, cleaning fluid entry, corrosion, unusual heat, odor, or loose connectors. Expected outcome: No external damage or unsafe condition is present. If damage, heat, smell, or fluid intrusion is found: Remove the monitor from service immediately.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check Using a Simulator If Available
steps.instructions: Connect a compatible SpO2 simulator or approved test setup with a known-good cable. Expected outcome: The monitor displays stable SpO2 and pulse rate values without error. If the simulator test passes: The original issue was likely accessory, setup, or patient-related. If the simulator test fails: Remove the monitor from service for bench evaluation.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported a SpO2 ERROR on the Medtronic Capnostream 20p and stated the monitor would not display SpO2 or pulse rate.
CCR.Cause: Verified SpO2 ERROR during testing; known-good SpO2 sensor and patient cable did not clear the fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed Capnostream 20p from service, labeled unit Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation/repair due to suspected internal SpO2 function failure.

helpfulDetails: Whether the monitor was in active patient use
Whether SpO2 and pulse rate were blank, dashed, unstable, or unavailable
Whether the error appeared at startup or only after connecting accessories
Any damaged pins, loose ports, cracked housing, fluid residue, odor, or heat

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - No Breath Alarm or Apnea Alarm During Monitoring
description: Troubleshooting no breath or apnea alarms caused by patient condition, sampling line issues, setup problems, or unreliable breath detection.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat a No Breath or apnea alarm as a patient safety alarm first, not an equipment problem. Confirm clinical staff are assessing the patient immediately. Expected outcome: The patient is checked for apnea, airway obstruction, respiratory depression, disconnection, or ventilation failure. Why it matters: A no breath alarm may indicate the patient is not breathing or that ventilation is not being detected.

steps.title: Do Not Troubleshoot on an Unstable Active Patient
steps.instructions: If the patient requires continued monitoring, move monitoring to another approved capnography monitor or alternate monitoring method before troubleshooting the Capnostream 20p. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely while the suspect device is evaluated. If this resolves the immediate safety concern, continue troubleshooting only when safe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is displaying a No Breath, apnea, or related breath detection alarm. Note the alarm wording, alarm delay time, waveform behavior, EtCO2 value, and respiration rate display. Expected outcome: The exact alarm and monitor behavior are confirmed. Why it matters: This separates a true apnea/no breath alarm from a staff report, nuisance alarm, or unrelated CO2 alarm.

steps.title: Check for a Valid CO2 Waveform
steps.instructions: Look for a normal capnogram waveform. Check whether the waveform is absent, flat, very small, intermittent, noisy, or delayed. Expected outcome: A clear waveform should appear with each detected breath. If the waveform returns and breath detection normalizes, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Sampling Line or Cannula Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the Microstream sampling line is fully connected to the monitor and properly connected to the patient or airway circuit. Expected outcome: The sampling line is secure at both ends. Why it matters: A loose or disconnected line can prevent the monitor from detecting exhaled CO2.

steps.title: Check for Kinks, Occlusion, Moisture, or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the full length of the sampling line. Look for bends, pinching under bed rails, fluid, secretions, crushed tubing, or a blocked filter area. Expected outcome: The sampling path is open and dry. If a kink or blockage is corrected and the alarm clears, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Replace the Sampling Line
steps.instructions: Install a new compatible sampling line or cannula. Do not reuse a visibly contaminated, wet, damaged, or suspect line. Expected outcome: CO2 waveform and respiration rate return if the original line was defective or blocked. Why it matters: Sampling lines are common external failure points and should be ruled out before suspecting the monitor.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Patient Setup
steps.instructions: Verify the monitor setup matches the clinical use case, such as adult, pediatric, or neonatal monitoring if applicable. Confirm the correct sampling accessory is being used for nasal/oral, intubated, or non-intubated monitoring. Expected outcome: The monitor and accessory setup match the patient and monitoring method. Why it matters: Incorrect setup can cause poor breath detection or nuisance apnea alarms.

steps.title: Check Patient Placement and Cannula Position
steps.instructions: Confirm the cannula is positioned correctly at the nares and mouth area if monitoring a non-intubated patient. Check whether the patient is mouth breathing, talking, moving, receiving oxygen, or has the cannula displaced. Expected outcome: The sampling line captures exhaled breath consistently. If repositioning restores waveform and RR detection, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Check Ventilator or Airway Circuit Connection if Intubated
steps.instructions: For intubated or ventilated patients, confirm the sampling adapter is connected correctly in the breathing circuit and is not loose, cracked, blocked, or filled with condensation. Expected outcome: Exhaled CO2 reaches the sampling line with each breath. Why it matters: Circuit connection issues can mimic apnea even when the patient or ventilator is breathing.

steps.title: Review Alarm Settings and Apnea Delay
steps.instructions: Confirm the apnea/no breath alarm delay and respiratory alarm settings are appropriate for the patient population and clinical area. Do not change alarm settings without clinical approval. Expected outcome: Alarm limits and delay settings match the clinical monitoring need. Why it matters: An overly short apnea delay may create nuisance alarms, while an overly long delay can delay response.

steps.title: Check for Low EtCO2 or Shallow Breathing
steps.instructions: Observe whether the patient has very low EtCO2, shallow respirations, weak waveform amplitude, or irregular breathing. Confirm clinical staff are aware. Expected outcome: The alarm is understood as either a clinical condition or a detection issue. Why it matters: The monitor may alarm because it cannot detect valid breaths, even if slight airflow is present.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the patient is no longer connected or monitoring has been transferred, power cycle the Capnostream 20p and allow it to restart normally. Expected outcome: The monitor boots without CO2 function errors and resumes normal sampling behavior. Do not power cycle during active patient monitoring unless clinical staff confirm it is safe.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: Use a known-good sampling line and a safe functional check method approved by your department. Confirm the monitor detects CO2 waveform and respiration rate consistently. Expected outcome: The Capnostream 20p detects breaths reliably with known-good accessories. If the monitor works with known-good accessories, the original line, setup, or patient connection was likely the cause.

steps.title: Check for Related Device Errors
steps.instructions: Look for CO2 ERROR, pump-related messages, blocked line messages, startup errors, unusual noise from the internal pump, or failure to draw sample flow. Expected outcome: No internal CO2 system fault is present. Why it matters: Persistent no breath alarms with good external setup may indicate an internal sampling or CO2 module issue.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Capnostream 20p was alarming No Breath during patient monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Found the CO2 sampling line kinked under the bed rail, causing intermittent waveform loss and unreliable respiration detection.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned and replaced the sampling line, verified stable CO2 waveform and respiration rate detection, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: EtCO2 value and respiration rate shown
CO2 waveform present, absent, flat, weak, or intermittent
Whether issue followed a specific accessory
Any CO2 ERROR or blocked line message
Pump noise, unusual sounds, heat, or smell
Whether patient was moved to another monitor

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - FILTERLINE / SAMPLING LINE BLOCKED, DISCONNECTED, OR NOT DETECTED
description: Troubleshooting FilterLine, sampling line, blockage, disconnection, or detection issues affecting CO₂ monitoring on the Capnostream 20p.

steps.title: Ensure patient safety first.
steps.instructions: If the Capnostream 20p is actively monitoring a patient and CO₂ monitoring is required, move the patient to another working monitor before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient ventilation monitoring continues without interruption. Why it matters: A sampling line problem can prevent reliable EtCO₂, respiration rate, apnea, and no-breath alarm monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the reported issue.
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact message or symptom being reported. Look for conditions such as: FilterLine not detected; Sampling line disconnected; Sampling line blocked; No CO₂ waveform; Missing EtCO₂ value; No breath detection; Intermittent CO₂ readings. Expected outcome: The reported issue is confirmed before replacing parts or removing the unit from service.

steps.title: Inspect the FilterLine connection at the monitor.
steps.instructions: Check that the FilterLine is fully seated in the Capnostream 20p CO₂ port. Remove and reconnect it firmly. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the FilterLine and begins sampling normally. If this resolves the issue, document the loose connection and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the full sampling line path.
steps.instructions: Check the line from the monitor to the patient for kinks, pinching, stretching, crushing, fluid contamination, or disconnection. Expected outcome: The sampling path is open and physically intact. Why it matters: A blocked or restricted sampling line can prevent the monitor from pulling a proper sample.

steps.title: Check for patient-end disconnection or incorrect placement.
steps.instructions: Verify the cannula, airway adapter, or sampling connection is properly attached and positioned for the patient setup being used. Expected outcome: The patient connection is correct and able to deliver a valid breath sample to the monitor.

steps.title: Replace the FilterLine with a known-good compatible line.
steps.instructions: Install a new or known-good Medtronic-compatible FilterLine. Avoid reusing a line that appears clogged, wet, contaminated, or damaged. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the replacement FilterLine and CO₂ monitoring resumes. If this resolves the issue, the original FilterLine was likely blocked, damaged, expired, contaminated, or defective.

steps.title: Check for moisture or contamination.
steps.instructions: Inspect the removed FilterLine and connector area for moisture, secretions, condensation, medication residue, blood, or visible contamination. Expected outcome: No contamination is present at the monitor port or sampling line connector. Why it matters: Moisture or debris can cause blockage alarms, detection problems, or unreliable CO₂ readings.

steps.title: Confirm the correct accessory type is being used.
steps.instructions: Verify staff are using the correct FilterLine type for the clinical application, such as nasal/oral sampling, intubated airway sampling, or pediatric/neonatal use where applicable. Expected outcome: The accessory matches the intended patient setup and monitoring mode. Why it matters: An incorrect sampling accessory can cause poor breath detection or unreliable readings.

steps.title: Restart the monitor if the issue appears after accessory replacement.
steps.instructions: If the FilterLine has been replaced and the connection is correct, power the monitor off and back on when safe to do so. Expected outcome: The monitor initializes normally and recognizes the sampling line. If this resolves the issue, document that the issue cleared after replacing the line and restarting the monitor.

steps.title: Test with a known-good setup off patient.
steps.instructions: Use a known-good FilterLine and a safe test setup to confirm the monitor can detect sampling activity. Do not perform testing on an active patient unless clinically safe and approved. Expected outcome: The monitor detects the line and displays appropriate CO₂ response or sampling behavior.

steps.title: Inspect the CO₂ inlet area externally.
steps.instructions: Check the monitor’s FilterLine port for visible damage, broken plastic, loose fit, debris, or signs of fluid exposure. Do not insert tools deeply into the port or perform internal disassembly during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The port appears intact and holds the FilterLine securely.

steps.title: Remove from service if detection or blockage errors continue with known-good lines.
steps.instructions: If multiple compatible FilterLines produce the same blocked, disconnected, or not detected condition, suspect an internal sampling system, port, pump, sensor, or pneumatic issue. Expected outcome: The device is removed from clinical use and sent for bench evaluation or repair.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Capnostream 20p displayed a sampling line blocked/not detected message and would not display a reliable CO₂ waveform.
CCR.Cause: Found the installed FilterLine kinked near the bed rail and not allowing proper sampling.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the FilterLine with a compatible known-good line, verified the monitor detected the line, confirmed CO₂ waveform returned, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue was blocked, disconnected, or not detected
Sampling line checked for kinks or moisture
EtCO₂ value present, missing, or unstable

title: Medtronic Capnostream 20p Capnography Monitor - BATTERY LOW, CHARGING FAILURE, OR UNIT SHUTS DOWN ON BATTERY
description: Troubleshooting battery low alarms, charging problems, weak battery runtime, or shutdown during battery operation.

steps.title: Ensure patient safety first.
steps.instructions: If the Capnostream 20p is actively monitoring a patient, do not troubleshoot battery or charging issues while relying on the same device for patient monitoring. Move the patient to another working monitor or connect the unit to AC power immediately if monitoring must continue. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues without interruption. Why it matters: A weak or failing battery can cause loss of EtCO2, SpO2, respiration rate, pulse rate, and alarm monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the reported issue.
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue is: BATTERY LOW alarm; Unit not charging; Battery icon not increasing; Unit shuts off when unplugged; Unit only works on AC power; Unit powers off during transport; Battery appears fully charged but drains quickly. Expected outcome: The exact battery-related complaint is confirmed. Why it matters: A low battery alarm, failed charging circuit, bad battery, or loose AC connection may look similar at first.

steps.title: Connect the monitor to a known good AC outlet.
steps.instructions: Plug the Capnostream 20p into a tested wall outlet. Avoid using a questionable power strip or extension cord during troubleshooting. Confirm that the unit powers on and shows signs of AC power or charging. Expected outcome: The monitor powers on normally and recognizes AC power. If this resolves the issue: The original outlet, power strip, or power source was likely the problem. Stop troubleshooting and document the finding.

steps.title: Inspect the power cord and power connection.
steps.instructions: Check the AC power cord for: Loose connection at the monitor; Damaged insulation; Bent or loose plug blades; Strain damage near the connector; Intermittent power when the cord is moved. Reseat the cord firmly at both ends. Expected outcome: AC power remains stable without flickering, rebooting, or charging interruptions. If this resolves the issue: Replace or secure the faulty cord as appropriate and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Swap with a known good power cord if available.
steps.instructions: Use a compatible hospital-approved power cord that is known to work on another device. Expected outcome: The monitor charges and runs normally on AC power. If this resolves the issue: The original power cord was defective. Remove the bad cord from service.

steps.title: Check the battery status after AC power is connected.
steps.instructions: Leave the monitor connected to AC power and observe whether the battery indicator shows charging activity. Look for: Battery icon changing; Charging indicator; Battery level increasing over time; Battery remaining at zero or very low; Battery alarm continuing while on AC power. Expected outcome: The battery begins charging and the low battery alarm clears after adequate charging time. Why it matters: A unit that runs on AC but never charges may have a failed battery, charging issue, or internal power problem.

steps.title: Allow adequate charging time before judging runtime.
steps.instructions: If the unit was stored unplugged or heavily discharged, allow it to charge before testing battery operation. Do not immediately fail the battery based only on a low charge condition after storage. Expected outcome: Battery level improves after charging. If this resolves the issue: Return the unit to service only after confirming acceptable battery operation.

steps.title: Test battery operation safely.
steps.instructions: Once charged, remove AC power while the monitor is not connected to a patient. Observe whether the unit: Continues running; Immediately shuts down; Reboots; Shows BATTERY LOW; Drops battery percentage quickly; Runs for only a short time. Expected outcome: The unit should remain powered on under battery operation. If the unit immediately shuts down or runtime is very short: Suspect a weak, failed, disconnected, or end-of-life battery.

steps.title: Check for signs of battery or power-related damage.
steps.instructions: Inspect the monitor externally for: Battery compartment damage, if accessible; Swelling or deformation; Unusual heat; Burning smell; Liquid intrusion; Cracked housing near the power input; Loose power inlet. Expected outcome: No physical damage, heat, odor, or power inlet looseness is found. If damage, heat, odor, or swelling is found: Remove the monitor from service immediately and escalate for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Review recent history.
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the monitor was: Stored unplugged for a long time; Used during transport; Dropped; Exposed to fluid; Recently had the battery replaced; Recently failed during a patient move. Expected outcome: Use history helps separate normal discharge from battery failure or damage. Why it matters: Battery complaints often follow storage, transport use, or physical damage.

steps.title: Perform a basic functional check on AC power.
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor boots normally, display works, alarms are present, and no additional system errors appear while connected to AC. Expected outcome: The monitor functions normally on AC power. If the unit also fails on AC power: The issue is not limited to the battery and should be escalated for repair.

steps.title: Do not continue using the monitor for battery-dependent transport if battery performance is unreliable.
steps.instructions: Even if the monitor works on AC power, do not return it for transport use if it shuts down, has poor runtime, or does not charge reliably. Expected outcome: The device is only used when its power source is reliable. Why it matters: A monitor that fails during transport can create a patient safety risk.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Medtronic Capnostream 20p displayed BATTERY LOW and shut down when unplugged from AC power.
CCR.Cause: Verified the monitor operated on AC power, but battery level did not recover after charging and the unit powered off during battery test.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the Capnostream 20p from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for battery replacement or bench repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Battery charge level before and after charging
Whether unit shut down immediately when unplugged
Any rebooting, flickering, or intermittent power
Unusual heat, odor, swelling, or physical damage
Whether unit works normally on AC power

title: Medtronic Capnostream 35 Capnography Monitor - FilterLine disconnected, blocked, or not recognized
description: Troubleshooting FilterLine disconnection, blockage, kink, leak, or recognition issues affecting CO₂ sampling on the Capnostream 35.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
If the Capnostream 35 is actively monitoring a patient, confirm that clinical staff are aware CO₂ monitoring may be interrupted or unreliable.

If EtCO₂ monitoring is clinically required, move the patient to another working monitor or use another approved monitoring method before troubleshooting.

Expected outcome: Patient ventilation monitoring continues safely.

Why it matters: A disconnected or blocked FilterLine can prevent accurate EtCO₂, respiration rate, apnea, and no-breath monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Message or Behavior
steps.instructions:
Confirm the exact message or symptom reported by staff. Look for messages such as FilterLine disconnected, FilterLine blocked, no FilterLine detected, no CO₂ waveform, absent EtCO₂ value, or no respiration rate.

Observe whether the issue occurs immediately after connecting the FilterLine, during patient movement, during transport, or after the device has been running for a while.

Expected outcome: The reported issue is confirmed and the failure pattern is understood.

If the issue cannot be duplicated: Document the check and return the unit only if it passes functional verification.

steps.title: Inspect the FilterLine Connection at the Monitor
steps.instructions:
Remove and reconnect the FilterLine at the Capnostream 35. Make sure it is fully seated and not cross-threaded, loose, or partially inserted.

Check for debris, damage, moisture, or cracked plastic at the connection point.

Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes the FilterLine and CO₂ sampling resumes.

If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the correction.

steps.title: Inspect the Entire FilterLine Tubing Path
steps.instructions:
Trace the FilterLine from the monitor to the patient end. Look for kinks, crushing, tight bends, pinched tubing under bed rails, trapped tubing in stretcher side rails, or tubing pulled tight during repositioning.

Expected outcome: The tubing path is clear and open.

Why it matters: A physical restriction can trigger blockage alarms or prevent accurate sampling.

steps.title: Replace the FilterLine With a Known-Good Compatible Line
steps.instructions:
Install a new, compatible Medtronic FilterLine appropriate for the patient setup.

Do not reuse a visibly contaminated, wet, kinked, cracked, or damaged line.

Expected outcome: The monitor detects the new FilterLine and displays a stable CO₂ waveform when connected to a breathing patient or approved simulator.

If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the defective or blocked disposable line.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Contamination
steps.instructions:
Inspect the removed FilterLine for visible fluid, condensation, secretions, blood, medication residue, or contamination.

If moisture or contamination is present, discard the line and verify function with a new one.

Expected outcome: The replacement line allows normal CO₂ sampling.

Why it matters: Fluid or contamination can block the sampling path and may damage the monitor if ignored.

steps.title: Check the Patient-End Connection
steps.instructions:
Verify that the cannula, airway adapter, or sampling connection is properly placed and connected.

For intubated patients, confirm the airway adapter is correctly installed in the breathing circuit and not blocked by secretions or condensation.

For nasal/oral cannulas, confirm the cannula is positioned correctly and not folded, pulled away, or blocked.

Expected outcome: The patient-end sampling point is open and correctly positioned.

If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the setup correction.

steps.title: Check for Incorrect Accessory or Incompatible Line
steps.instructions:
Confirm the installed line is the correct type for the Capnostream 35 and the patient application.

Look for off-brand, damaged, expired, modified, or incorrect sampling accessories that may not be recognized or may restrict flow.

Expected outcome: A compatible FilterLine is installed and recognized by the monitor.

Why it matters: Capnography monitors depend on the correct sampling accessory to maintain proper flow and measurement accuracy.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor Only When Safe
steps.instructions:
If the FilterLine is known-good and properly connected but still not recognized, remove the unit from patient use if needed and restart the monitor.

After restart, reconnect the FilterLine and observe whether the same message returns.

Expected outcome: The monitor initializes normally and recognizes the FilterLine.

If the error clears: Perform a functional check before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Perform a Basic CO₂ Functional Check
steps.instructions:
Using an approved CO₂ simulator or known-good test setup, verify that the Capnostream 35 detects the FilterLine, pulls sample flow, displays a CO₂ waveform, and reports EtCO₂ and respiration rate appropriately.

Expected outcome: CO₂ monitoring operates normally with a known-good line and test source.

If the monitor fails with a known-good FilterLine and test setup: Treat the issue as likely internal.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Capnostream 35 displayed a FilterLine blocked/disconnected message and was not showing a CO₂ waveform.
CCR.Cause: Found the installed FilterLine kinked under the stretcher rail and unable to sample properly.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the FilterLine, corrected tubing routing, verified CO₂ waveform and EtCO₂ reading with functional check, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether CO₂ waveform was missing or intermittent
Whether EtCO₂ or respiration rate was blank, dashed, or unstable
Whether the line was kinked, blocked, wet, or contaminated
Whether a known-good FilterLine was tested
Power behavior during restart or power cycle
Environmental factors such as transport, bed rail position, stretcher rail position, or patient movement
Indicator lights, screen messages, or alarm behavior observed

title: Medtronic Capnostream 35 Capnography Monitor - SpO2 Sensor Disconnected or Not Detecting Patient
description: Troubleshooting SpO2 sensor disconnection, poor sensor detection, missing pulse oximetry values, or sensor-off-patient conditions on the Medtronic Capnostream 35.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Capnostream 35 is actively monitoring a patient and SpO2 monitoring is clinically needed, make sure clinical staff are aware that pulse oximetry may not be reliable. Move the patient to another working monitor if SpO2 monitoring cannot be restored quickly. Expected outcome: The patient remains safely monitored while troubleshooting is performed. Why it matters: A disconnected or undetected SpO2 sensor can cause loss of oxygen saturation and pulse rate monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact condition shown on the monitor. Look for messages such as sensor disconnected, sensor off patient, no SpO2 value, dashed SpO2 reading, missing pulse rate, or poor signal quality. Note when the issue occurs: Immediately after sensor connection; Only during patient movement; With one specific sensor; With one specific cable; During startup; Intermittently during monitoring. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed and separated from a normal patient movement or placement problem.

steps.title: Check Sensor Placement on the Patient
steps.instructions: Confirm that the SpO2 sensor is properly placed on the patient according to sensor type. Check for: Sensor not fully seated on the finger, toe, ear, or approved site; Emitter and detector not aligned; Sensor loose or partially off; Excessive patient movement; Poor perfusion at the sensor site; Nail polish, artificial nails, moisture, blood, or debris interfering with detection. Reposition the sensor or ask clinical staff to move it to a better site if needed. Expected outcome: SpO2 and pulse rate values return with a stable waveform or pulse indicator. If this resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the SpO2 Sensor
steps.instructions: Remove the sensor from service if it is visibly damaged, contaminated, stretched, cracked, kinked, or has exposed wiring. If using a disposable sensor, confirm it is not expired, reused beyond intended use, or damaged from handling. Expected outcome: A damaged or inappropriate sensor is identified and replaced. If replacing the sensor restores SpO2 monitoring, document the sensor issue and stop.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm the sensor and cable being used are compatible with the Capnostream 35 and the installed SpO2 technology/configuration. Do not assume all SpO2 sensors are interchangeable. Medtronic Capnostream monitors may use specific SpO2 sensor/cable families depending on configuration. Expected outcome: An incompatible sensor or cable is ruled out before suspecting monitor failure. If the wrong sensor type was used, replace it with the correct compatible sensor and stop.

steps.title: Check the SpO2 Extension Cable or Interface Cable
steps.instructions: Inspect the SpO2 cable between the patient sensor and the monitor. Look for: Bent or damaged pins; Loose connector fit; Cracked connector shell; Pinched cable jacket; Intermittent connection when the cable is gently moved; Evidence of fluid intrusion or contamination. Disconnect and reconnect the cable firmly. Expected outcome: The connection is secure and free of visible damage.

steps.title: Swap With a Known-Good Sensor and Cable
steps.instructions: Use a known-good compatible SpO2 sensor and cable from a working setup. Test the monitor on a simulator or approved test method if available. If testing on a person is permitted by site policy, only do so safely and briefly. Expected outcome: If SpO2 works with known-good accessories, the original sensor or cable is the likely cause. If SpO2 still does not work, the issue may be with the monitor or SpO2 input circuit.

steps.title: Inspect the Monitor SpO2 Connector
steps.instructions: Check the Capnostream 35 SpO2 input connector for obvious external damage. Look for: Bent pins; Loose connector; Cracked housing; Debris inside the connector; Signs of liquid contamination; Connector movement when the cable is inserted. Do not open the monitor or attempt board-level repair during floor troubleshooting. Expected outcome: External connector damage is identified or ruled out.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor if Safe
steps.instructions: If the monitor is not connected to an active patient, power the unit off, wait briefly, and power it back on. Reconnect the known-good SpO2 sensor and cable after startup. Expected outcome: Temporary detection or software communication issues clear after restart. If this resolves the issue, verify stable SpO2 operation before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Check for Related Monitor Errors
steps.instructions: Look for any additional system messages, SpO2 error messages, startup faults, frozen display behavior, or other monitoring functions failing at the same time. Expected outcome: You determine whether this is isolated to the SpO2 sensor path or part of a wider monitor fault.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check
steps.instructions: With a known-good compatible sensor and cable, verify that the Capnostream 35 detects the sensor, displays SpO2, displays pulse rate, and responds normally to sensor removal. Confirm that the alarm behavior is appropriate when the sensor is disconnected or removed from the patient. Expected outcome: SpO2 monitoring and alarm detection operate normally before the device is returned to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Medtronic Capnostream 35 displayed an SpO2 sensor disconnected condition and would not detect the patient sensor.
CCR.Cause: Found damaged SpO2 extension cable causing intermittent sensor detection when connected to the monitor.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the SpO2 extension cable, tested with a known-good compatible sensor, verified stable SpO2 and pulse rate readings, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether SpO2 value was blank, dashed, intermittent, or inaccurate
Whether issue followed the sensor/cable or stayed with the monitor
Any related SpO2 ERROR or system fault

title: Medtronic Capnostream 35 Capnography Monitor - No Breath / Apnea Alarm Or Unreliable Respiration Detection
description: Troubleshooting no breath, apnea, or unreliable respiration detection caused by patient condition, sampling line issues, setup problems, or monitor faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat a no breath or apnea alarm as a patient safety alarm first, not an equipment problem. Confirm clinical staff are assessing the patient immediately. Do not silence or dismiss the alarm until the patient’s breathing status is verified. Expected outcome: The patient is assessed for apnea, airway obstruction, respiratory depression, ventilator disconnection, or other clinical concerns. Why it matters: A no breath or apnea alarm may indicate the patient is not breathing or that ventilation is not being detected.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Active Troubleshooting if Patient Monitoring Is Still Needed
steps.instructions: If the patient still requires EtCO2 or respiration monitoring, move monitoring to another working device or approved backup method before continuing equipment troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues safely without relying on a questionable monitor. If this resolves the immediate risk, stop clinical use of the suspect device and continue evaluation off-patient.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm or Detection Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm or behavior reported by staff. Look for messages such as no breath, apnea, missing respiration rate, intermittent respiration rate, weak capnogram, or absent CO2 waveform. Note when the problem occurs: Immediately after connecting the patient; After patient movement; During transport; Only with a specific sampling line; Only on battery power; Intermittently during monitoring; After switching patient type or care area. Expected outcome: The reported issue is confirmed and the failure pattern is understood. Why it matters: Intermittent breath detection problems are often caused by setup, sampling, moisture, obstruction, or patient interface issues.

steps.title: Check the CO2 Waveform, Not Just the Numeric Respiration Rate
steps.instructions: Observe whether the capnogram waveform is present, stable, weak, irregular, or completely absent. Expected outcome: A consistent waveform should appear with valid breathing, and the respiration rate should update appropriately. Why it matters: If the waveform is poor or missing, the monitor may not be receiving a usable breath signal.

steps.title: Inspect the Sampling Line or Cannula Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm the CO2 sampling line is fully seated in the monitor and properly connected to the patient interface. Check for: Loose connection; Kinked tubing; Crushed tubing; Disconnected cannula; Sampling line pulled loose during movement; Wrong or incompatible accessory; Moisture, secretions, or blockage. Expected outcome: The sampling path is secure, open, and appropriate for the patient setup. If reconnecting or correcting the sampling line restores waveform and respiration detection, stop.

steps.title: Replace the Sampling Line or Cannula With a Known-Good Compatible Accessory
steps.instructions: Use a new or known-good Medtronic-compatible CO2 sampling line or cannula. Expected outcome: A blocked, contaminated, damaged, or incorrect sampling line is ruled out. If respiration detection becomes stable after replacing the line, document the accessory issue and return the monitor only if all testing passes.

steps.title: Check for Patient Interface Placement Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm the cannula, airway adapter, or sampling connection is positioned correctly for the patient type and breathing route. For nasal/oral cannulas, verify the cannula is not displaced from the nose or mouth. For intubated or ventilated patients, verify the airway adapter and circuit connection are secure. Expected outcome: The sample source is correctly positioned to capture exhaled CO2. Why it matters: The monitor cannot detect breaths reliably if the sample line is not receiving exhaled gas.

steps.title: Check for Low or Absent EtCO2 Readings
steps.instructions: Observe whether EtCO2 values are missing, very low, dashed out, or inconsistent. Expected outcome: If EtCO2 is absent or unstable, the no breath/apnea alarm may be caused by lack of valid CO2 detection rather than a standalone respiration rate problem. Why it matters: Respiration rate on a capnography monitor depends on breath detection from the CO2 waveform.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Settings and Patient Type
steps.instructions: Check whether the apnea/no breath delay, respiration rate limits, and patient type are appropriate for the patient being monitored. Do not change clinical alarm settings without clinical approval. Expected outcome: Alarm behavior matches the intended clinical setup. Why it matters: Incorrect patient type or alarm delay settings can cause alarms to occur sooner, later, or differently than staff expect.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Contamination at the Sample Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the sampling inlet area and sampling line connection for visible moisture, residue, or contamination. Do not force fluid into or out of the device. Expected outcome: No moisture or contamination is present at the external sample connection. Why it matters: Moisture or contamination can interfere with sample flow and cause unreliable CO2 and respiration readings.

steps.title: Restart the Monitor Only After Patient Safety Is Addressed
steps.instructions: If the issue is not occurring on an active patient, power cycle the monitor and allow it to complete startup normally. Reconnect a known-good sampling line and check whether the alarm or unreliable detection returns. Expected outcome: Temporary software or startup communication issues clear after restart. If the issue clears and does not return during testing, document the restart and final functional check.

steps.title: Compare Against a Known-Good Monitor if Available
steps.instructions: Using the same patient setup or a test setup when appropriate, compare behavior with another Capnostream 35 or approved capnography monitor. Expected outcome: The issue follows either the patient/accessory setup or the suspect monitor. Why it matters: This helps separate a device problem from a sampling line, setup, or clinical condition.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check Off-Patient
steps.instructions: Use site-approved procedures to verify that the monitor powers normally, recognizes the sampling line, displays CO2/respiration information as expected, and alarms appropriately. Expected outcome: The monitor should detect valid breaths and alarm appropriately when no breath condition is simulated or present per site procedure. Do not return the monitor to service unless the issue is resolved and the device passes functional verification.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Medtronic Capnostream 35 was alarming no breath/apnea and intermittently losing respiration rate during monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Found CO2 sampling line partially kinked near the patient connection, causing weak waveform and unreliable breath detection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced sampling line, verified stable CO2 waveform and respiration rate, confirmed alarm cleared, and returned monitor to service after functional check.

helpfulDetails: Whether patient was transferred to another monitor
CO2 waveform present, weak, intermittent, or absent
Respiration rate displayed or dashed out
Cannula or airway adapter placement checked
Alarm settings reviewed with clinical staff
Power behavior, including whether the issue occurred on AC power or battery power
Environmental factors such as transport, movement, humidity, moisture, or location-specific conditions
