Indicator lights, screen messages, alarms, or displayed status information

title: Medtronic Capnostream 35 Capnography Monitor - Battery, Charging, or Power-On Failure
description: Troubleshooting Capnostream 35 battery, charging, AC power, power cord, docking, or startup failure issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Capnostream 35 is connected to a patient and is not powering on, charging, or maintaining power, remove it from patient use immediately. Move the patient to another approved monitor before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues without relying on a questionable device. Why it matters: Loss of power can interrupt EtCO2, respiration rate, SpO2, pulse rate, and alarm monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact symptom reported by staff: Will not power on; Powers on only when plugged in; Battery does not charge; Battery drains quickly; Charging indicator does not appear; Unit shuts off during use or transport; Display flickers, reboots, or powers down unexpectedly. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before parts or internal failure are suspected. If the problem was caused by misunderstanding normal battery behavior, educate staff, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Check AC Power at the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug a known working device or outlet tester into the same outlet. Confirm the outlet has power and is not controlled by a switch, failed power strip, tripped breaker, or emergency power issue. Expected outcome: The outlet provides reliable AC power. If the outlet is dead or unreliable, move the monitor to a working outlet, notify facilities if needed, document the issue, and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and External Power Supply
steps.instructions: Check the AC power cord and power supply for: Loose connection; Bent or damaged prongs; Cuts, kinks, or crushed cable insulation; Missing strain relief; Signs of overheating, melting, or odor; Incorrect or non-approved power supply. Reseat the power cord firmly at the outlet, power supply, and monitor connection. Expected outcome: The monitor receives stable external power. If a damaged cord or power supply is found, remove it from use, replace it with an approved equivalent, retest the monitor, and stop if resolved.

steps.title: Confirm the Monitor Shows External Power or Charging Indication
steps.instructions: Connect the monitor to a known good outlet using a known good power cord and power supply if available. Look for charging indication, AC power indication, screen activity, startup lights, or fan/display response depending on the device behavior. Expected outcome: The monitor recognizes external power and begins charging or powering on. If the monitor works with a known good power supply, the original power accessory is the likely cause. Replace the accessory and document the correction.

steps.title: Allow Time for a Deeply Discharged Battery
steps.instructions: If the battery is fully depleted, connect the Capnostream 35 to AC power and allow it to charge before attempting repeated startups. Avoid rapid power-button cycling. Expected outcome: The monitor eventually shows charging or powers on after receiving adequate charge time. If the monitor begins charging normally, continue charging, perform an operational check, document the result, and return to service only if safe.

steps.title: Check Battery Seating if Externally Accessible
steps.instructions: If the battery is user-accessible without disassembly, verify it is properly seated and latched. Do not open the device beyond normal battery access. Expected outcome: The battery is fully seated and recognized by the monitor. If reseating the battery restores operation, perform a charging and power-on check before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Inspect the Battery Area for Damage
steps.instructions: If the battery compartment is externally accessible, check for: Cracked battery housing; Swelling; Corrosion; Liquid intrusion; Damaged contacts; Burn marks; Unusual heat or smell. Expected outcome: Battery area appears clean, dry, and physically safe. If swelling, heat, corrosion, liquid damage, or burning is found, remove the device from service immediately. Do not continue charging.

steps.title: Test Battery Operation After Charging
steps.instructions: After the monitor has charged, disconnect AC power briefly and verify whether the unit remains on battery power. Observe for sudden shutdown, battery warning, rapid percentage drop, or reboot. Expected outcome: The monitor remains powered and behaves normally on battery. If the monitor only works on AC power, the battery may be depleted, failed, not recognized, or the charging circuit may require repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Power Loss
steps.instructions: With the monitor powered on and not connected to a patient, gently move the external power connection and power cord. Do not stress the connector. Watch for flickering display, rebooting, charging status changes, or loss of AC indication. Expected outcome: Power remains stable during normal movement. If power cuts in and out, suspect a damaged cord, power supply, DC jack, connector, or internal power path. Remove from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Startup and Functional Check
steps.instructions: Once the monitor powers on, verify: Display comes up normally; Startup completes without repeated rebooting; Battery/AC status appears reasonable; No power-related alarms remain; Buttons or touchscreen respond normally; CO2 and SpO2 functions are available as expected. Expected outcome: The device starts normally and is ready for normal functional testing. If startup fails, freezes, reboots, or power alarms remain, remove the monitor from service.

steps.title: Return to Service Only if Power Is Stable
steps.instructions: The Capnostream 35 should only be returned to service if it powers on reliably, charges normally, operates from AC power, and maintains battery operation as expected. Expected outcome: The device is safe for patient monitoring use. If any power instability remains, do not return it to clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Medtronic Capnostream 35 would not power on and would not show charging when plugged in.
CCR.Cause: Found the external power supply connection loose at the monitor input; unit powered on normally after reseating and testing on a known good outlet.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated power connection, verified AC power and charging indication, powered unit on successfully, confirmed stable operation, and returned device to service.

helpfulDetails: External power supply inspected or swapped
Whether the unit powers on with AC only
Whether the unit stays on when unplugged
Any rebooting, flickering, or shutdown behavior
Any unusual heat, smell, swelling, corrosion, or liquid damage
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Medtronic Capnostream 35 Capnography Monitor - Data Export, EMR, Wireless, or Vital Sync Connectivity Issue
description: Troubleshooting Capnostream 35 data export, EMR communication, wireless connection, or Vital Sync connectivity problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Capnostream 35 is actively monitoring a patient, do not interrupt monitoring, power cycle the device, remove network accessories, or change communication settings unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. Move the patient to another approved monitor if monitoring must continue during troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient monitoring continues without interruption. Why it matters: Connectivity problems should not create a gap in EtCO2, respiration rate, SpO2, pulse rate, or alarm monitoring.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Connectivity Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm what issue was reported: Data is not exporting; Patient data is not reaching the EMR; Wireless connection is unavailable; Vital Sync is not receiving data; Data is delayed, intermittent, or associated with the wrong patient; USB export is failing; Network connection drops during use. Expected outcome: The exact failure mode is identified before changing settings. Why it matters: “Not connecting” can mean several different problems, including network access, configuration, patient association, server communication, or export media failure.

steps.title: Check Whether Bedside Monitoring Is Still Working
steps.instructions: Confirm the Capnostream 35 is still displaying patient parameters correctly, including EtCO2, respiration rate, waveform, SpO2, pulse rate, and alarms if in use. Expected outcome: The monitor itself is still functioning clinically. Why it matters: A communication issue is different from a monitoring failure. If monitoring values are missing or unreliable, troubleshoot that clinical function first.

steps.title: Check Power and Startup Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is powered on normally, not in a low battery condition, and not displaying system errors that could affect communication. Connect to AC power if battery level is low. Expected outcome: The monitor is fully powered and stable. Why it matters: Low battery, unstable power, or startup faults can interrupt communication or prevent export functions from completing.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Patient and Location Information
steps.instructions: Verify that the patient information, bed/location, department, or device association is correct according to the site workflow. If the Capnostream 35 is sending data through Vital Sync or an EMR interface, confirm the device is assigned to the correct patient or room before assuming a device failure. Expected outcome: Data is linked to the correct patient/location. Why it matters: EMR and middleware issues are often caused by patient association, location mapping, or workflow errors rather than hardware failure.

steps.title: Check Physical Connections
steps.instructions: If the connection uses a cable, inspect the network cable, communication cable, adapter, dock, or export cable for loose connections, damage, bent pins, or strain. Reseat the cable at both ends if safe to do so. Swap with a known-good cable if available. Expected outcome: The connection is secure and communication resumes. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the cable or connection problem.

steps.title: Check the Network Port or Wall Jack
steps.instructions: If using wired network communication, connect the cable to a known-good network port approved for medical device use. Confirm link lights if available on the port, adapter, or network device. Expected outcome: The monitor has a valid physical network connection. Why it matters: A bad wall jack, inactive port, or disconnected patch cable can look like a monitor failure.

steps.title: Check Wireless Availability
steps.instructions: If using wireless communication, confirm the wireless function is enabled according to site policy and that the monitor is in an area with expected wireless coverage. Compare with another known-working device in the same location if possible. Expected outcome: Wireless service is available in that area. Why it matters: Wireless dead zones, access point issues, or network changes can cause intermittent EMR or Vital Sync communication problems.

steps.title: Check for Recent Network or IT Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there were recent changes to: Wireless network settings; VLANs; IP addressing; Certificates; Firewall rules; Vital Sync server configuration; EMR interface settings; Device replacement or room reassignment. Expected outcome: Recent system changes are identified. Why it matters: Connectivity failures often occur after IT changes, server updates, certificate expiration, or interface changes.

steps.title: Compare With Another Capnostream 35
steps.instructions: If another Capnostream 35 is available, test whether it communicates correctly from the same location using the same workflow. Do not swap devices on an active patient unless clinical staff approve. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to one monitor, one location, or the network/interface system. Why it matters: A comparison test helps separate device failure from network, EMR, or Vital Sync issues.

steps.title: Check Data Export Media
steps.instructions: If the issue involves exporting data to USB or removable media, confirm the media is approved by site policy, properly formatted, not full, and not physically damaged. Try a known-good approved USB drive if permitted. Expected outcome: Data export completes successfully or the media is identified as the cause. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the failed export media.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the device is not actively monitoring a patient, power cycle the Capnostream 35 and allow it to fully restart. After restart, recheck network, export, EMR, or Vital Sync communication. Expected outcome: Temporary communication faults clear after restart. Why it matters: Some communication sessions can hang and recover after a controlled restart.

steps.title: Check Site Configuration Settings
steps.instructions: Review communication settings only within the limits allowed by your facility and service process. Confirm the device is configured for the correct connection method, network profile, server destination, or export workflow. Do not randomly change IP, wireless, EMR, or server settings without approved configuration information. Expected outcome: Settings match the approved site configuration. Why it matters: Incorrect settings can break communication or send data to the wrong destination.

steps.title: Escalate to IT, Interface Team, or Vendor Support When Needed
steps.instructions: If the monitor appears functional but data is not reaching Vital Sync or the EMR, involve the appropriate IT/network/interface team. Provide them with the device serial number, asset number, room/location, connection type, time of failure, and whether other devices are affected. Expected outcome: Network or interface-side causes are investigated. Why it matters: Clinical Engineering can verify the device and external setup, but EMR routing, server logs, wireless authentication, and interface engines often require IT support.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Capnostream 35 was monitoring at the bedside but patient data was not transferring to Vital Sync/EMR.
CCR.Cause: Verified bedside monitoring was functional; found the device connected to an inactive network port in the patient room.
CCR.Resolution: Moved the network cable to a known-good approved port, confirmed communication restored, and returned the monitor to service.

helpfulDetails: Exact reported issue: EMR, Vital Sync, wireless, wired network, or USB export
Wireless coverage compared with another device
Any error messages or connection indicators
IT/interface team ticket number if escalated

title: Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 Patient Warming System - Replace Filter alert / expired HEPA filter counter
description: Addressing a Replace Filter alert caused by an expired HEPA filter interval or a filter counter not reset after replacement.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
If the WarmTouch WT 6000 is currently supporting a patient, do not begin maintenance or remove the filter enclosure during use.

Coordinate with clinical staff to transfer warming to a known-working device when clinically appropriate before taking the unit out of service for filter replacement.

Expected outcome: The patient is safely supported before Clinical Engineering performs filter maintenance.

Why it matters: The unit must not be operated with the filter enclosure removed, and patient warming should not be interrupted without a safe alternative.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Alert
steps.instructions:
With the unit available for evaluation, plug it into a suitable grounded outlet and press the On/Standby key.

Confirm whether the Replace Filter screen appears at power-on. On this model, the alert appears at startup when either the filter operating-hours counter or calendar-days counter has expired. The HEPA filter replacement interval is 2,000 operating hours or 365 days, whichever comes first.

Expected outcome: The alert is confirmed as a scheduled filter-maintenance condition rather than an unrelated warming system failure.

If the alert does not reappear: Review the reported history, document that the issue could not be duplicated, inspect filter status through the device menu while in Ready mode, and continue evaluation as appropriate.

steps.title: 3. Inspect for Additional Safety Concerns
steps.instructions:
Before treating the issue as routine filter maintenance, inspect the warming unit for:

Damaged power cord or plug.
Damaged enclosure, hose, or mounting hardware.
Missing or damaged labels.
Evidence of liquid entry.
Unusual odor, heat, noise, airflow restriction, or another alarm condition.

Expected outcome: No additional safety concern is identified beyond the filter replacement alert.

If damage, burning odor, abnormal heat, airflow failure, or another alarm is present: Stop troubleshooting, remove the unit from service, and send it for bench evaluation or repair.

steps.title: 4. Review Filter Status Information
steps.instructions:
Place the unit in Ready mode. From the Main screen, access the menu and view the Filter Information screen to confirm the remaining filter days and operating hours.

The Filter Information screen is accessible in Ready mode and shows the days and operating hours remaining before filter replacement is required.

Expected outcome: The filter status indicates an expired interval or confirms that the alert is associated with the filter counter.

If the filter was recently replaced but the counters remain expired: Proceed to verify that replacement documentation exists and that the filter counter reset was completed by qualified personnel.

steps.title: 5. Verify Whether the HEPA Filter Has Actually Been Replaced
steps.instructions:
Check the asset history, PM record, service label, or prior work order to determine whether the HEPA filter has already been replaced.

Do not clear the Replace Filter alert solely to remove the message if there is no confirmation that a new filter was installed.

Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering confirms whether the unit requires a new HEPA filter or only a documented post-replacement counter reset.

If no filter replacement is documented: Replace the HEPA filter before resetting the counter.

steps.title: 6. Replace the HEPA Filter When Due
steps.instructions:
Remove the unit from clinical use and disconnect the power cord from AC power before performing filter replacement.

Using the correct WarmTouch HEPA filter replacement kit, access the external filter enclosure, remove the expired filter, inspect the enclosure gasket for damage, install the new filter, and securely reinstall the filter enclosure and power connection according to approved service practice.

Expected outcome: A new HEPA filter is installed and the filter enclosure is fully secured before power is reapplied.

Why it matters: The manufacturer indicates that failure to replace the filter as recommended can adversely affect device performance, and the unit must not be operated with the filter enclosure removed.

steps.title: 7. Reset the Filter Counters Only After Filter Replacement
steps.instructions:
After the new filter is installed and the enclosure is secure, power on the unit and follow the qualified-service filter reset process from the Replace Filter / Filter Information screens.

The service procedure resets the counters to 365 days and 2,000 operating hours after filter replacement.

Expected outcome: The counters reflect a newly installed filter.

If the counters cannot be reset, immediately revert to expired values, or the alert cannot be cleared after confirmed filter replacement: Remove the unit from service and send it for bench evaluation.

steps.title: 8. Verify Alert Clearance and Basic Operation
steps.instructions:
Return the unit to Standby, then power it back into Ready mode.

Confirm that:

The Main screen appears instead of the Replace Filter screen.
No new alarm or fault appears.
The unit passes its startup check.
The filter enclosure is seated properly.
The power cord and exterior remain undamaged.

The service documentation instructs verifying that the Main screen appears at power-on after resetting the filter counters.

Expected outcome: The Replace Filter alert is cleared and the unit is ready for applicable post-maintenance verification.

If the alert clears and the device meets required inspection/testing criteria: Document the completed maintenance and return the device to service.

steps.title: 9. Complete Required Post-Maintenance Checks
steps.instructions:
Perform the inspection and any verification required by facility procedure before returning the warmer to clinical use.

At minimum, verify physical condition, secure filter enclosure installation, filter status, normal startup behavior, and final device status. Follow applicable requirements for electrical safety or operational testing when required by the maintenance activity or local policy.

Expected outcome: The unit is documented as safe and ready for clinical use following filter replacement and counter reset.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 displays a Replace Filter message each time the unit is powered on.
CCR.Cause: HEPA filter interval was expired at the filter status screen, indicating the filter reached its required replacement limit of 365 days or 2,000 operating hours.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, replaced the HEPA filter, reset the filter counters, verified the Replace Filter alert cleared at startup, and returned the warmer to service after inspection.

helpfulDetails: Replace Filter message confirmed at startup.
Filter operating hours remaining before replacement.
HEPA filter replaced and replacement date.
Main screen displayed at power-on after reset.
Power cord and housing inspection findings.
Any additional alarms, unusual odor, heat, or airflow concerns.
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service.

title: Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 Patient Warming System - Unit does not reach or maintain selected temperature
description: Troubleshooting inadequate or unstable warmed-air output caused by setup, blanket, airflow, filter, power, alarm, or temperature-verification issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the WarmTouch WT 6000 from active patient use if warming is inadequate, unstable, or associated with an alarm. Move the patient to a known-working warming system or approved alternative warming method according to clinical procedure before troubleshooting. Do not operate the blower hose directly on a patient without an attached WarmTouch blanket. Expected outcome: The patient is no longer dependent on a warming device with questionable heat output. Why it matters: Inadequate or unstable warming can prevent effective temperature management, and the unit may shut down heat and airflow when output temperature is outside specification.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Symptom and Check for Alarms
steps.instructions: With the unit removed from clinical use, power it on and document: Selected temperature setting; Displayed temperature behavior; Whether the temperature rises and stabilizes; Any audible alarm or displayed alarm message; Whether the fan continues running or shuts down. The unit should complete power-on self-test, display the main screen, and show stable temperature response after a heated setting is selected. Expected outcome: The complaint is confirmed and any error or alarm information is captured before further evaluation. Stop if resolved: If the unit powers on normally, reaches stable temperature, and no alarm recurs during verification, document the findings and complete required safety checks before returning it to service.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Source and Power Cord
steps.instructions: Verify that the unit is connected to a hospital-grade grounded outlet and that the power cord is fully seated at the warming unit. Inspect the power cord and plug for damage, looseness, overheating, discoloration, or intermittent connection. Test using a known-good outlet and approved compatible power cord when available. Expected outcome: The status indicator and display remain stable during operation, with no intermittent power loss or reset. Why it matters: A secure grounded power connection is required for reliable unit operation and electrical safety.

steps.title: Verify Correct Operating Mode and Temperature Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm that the unit is in treatment mode rather than standby or ready mode. Standby mode: amber status LED, display off, heater off, fan off. Ready mode: green status LED, display on, heater off, fan off. Treatment mode: green status LED, display on, fan on, heater commanded as needed. Select a heated temperature setting and observe whether the displayed temperature begins rising and stabilizing. Expected outcome: The fan runs and the displayed temperature responds to the selected heated setting. Stop if resolved: If the issue was caused by the unit remaining in ready mode or an incorrect setting, verify proper function and document the correction.

steps.title: Inspect the Hose and Nozzle Externally
steps.instructions: Inspect the warming hose and nozzle for: Kinks, crushing, tears, punctures, or loose sections; Visible contamination or obstruction; Damaged nozzle or blanket-retaining clip; Air leakage at the hose or nozzle. With the unit operating in a heated mode and disconnected from patient use, verify that airflow is present at the nozzle and does not appear weak or intermittent. Expected outcome: The hose is intact, unobstructed, and delivering consistent airflow. Why it matters: Restricted or leaking airflow can reduce effective heat delivery even when the unit is producing warm air.

steps.title: Confirm Use of a Properly Attached WarmTouch Blanket
steps.instructions: Verify that a compatible WarmTouch blanket is being used and that the hose nozzle is properly inserted and secured to the blanket inlet. Inspect the blanket for: Incorrect connection or partial disconnection; Tears or large leaks; Compression under equipment or patient positioning; Blocked air distribution channels; Improper blanket type. Do not use non-WarmTouch blankets with this unit. Expected outcome: A compatible blanket is securely attached and able to distribute warmed airflow properly. Stop if resolved: If replacing or correctly connecting the blanket restores expected warming performance, document the blanket/setup issue and verify the unit operates normally. Why it matters: The WarmTouch WT 6000 is designed for use with WarmTouch blankets, and blanket condition or attachment can directly affect warming performance.

steps.title: Check the Air Intake and Filter Status
steps.instructions: Inspect the rear air intake area externally for blockage from linens, packaging, dust accumulation, wall placement, or nearby equipment. From the unit menu in ready mode, review the filter status information. If the unit displays a filter replacement message or indicates the HEPA filter is expired, remove the unit from use until the filter is replaced and the required verification is completed. Expected outcome: The air intake is unobstructed and the filter is within its service interval. Why it matters: The fan draws room air through the HEPA filter before heating and delivery. The service documentation specifies HEPA filter replacement every 2,000 operating hours or 365 days, whichever occurs first.

steps.title: Evaluate for Environmental or Electromagnetic Interference
steps.instructions: If the complaint is intermittent and occurs only in a particular clinical location, temporarily evaluate the unit away from adjacent powered equipment. Where appropriate, reposition nearby cabling or connect the warming unit to a separate approved outlet circuit branch for troubleshooting purposes. Expected outcome: The unit operates consistently without intermittent alarms or unstable temperature output. Stop if resolved: If performance becomes stable after separation from nearby equipment or relocation, document the environmental finding and coordinate corrective action before clinical reuse. Why it matters: The manufacturer identifies electromagnetic interference as a possible contributor to warming unit performance problems.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Temperature Accuracy Verification
steps.instructions: With the unit removed from patient use, perform temperature verification using an appropriate calibrated temperature probe and approved Clinical Engineering procedure. For the manufacturer-described verification approach: Confirm that the unit passes power-on self-test and enters ready mode; Fully compress the hose and position it vertically with the nozzle facing upward; Position the temperature probe at the metal screen near the base of the hose duct adapter; Test the 34°C setting and allow at least 3 minutes for stabilization; Confirm measured output is within 1°C of the selected temperature; Repeat at the 47°C setting, allowing at least 3 minutes for stabilization. Expected outcome: Measured output temperature stabilizes within 1°C of the selected temperature at both tested settings. Stop if resolved: If the unit passes temperature accuracy verification and no setup or blanket defect remains, document successful testing and complete required post-service safety checks before return to service. Why it matters: This separates a clinical warming-performance complaint from a confirmed blower temperature-control failure.

steps.title: Check for Airflow Failure During Bench Verification
steps.instructions: With the unit off the patient and using an approved test setup, select the 34°C setting and confirm that the fan ramps up, airflow is present at the nozzle, and no alarm occurs. Expected outcome: The unit delivers consistent airflow without alarm or shutdown. Stop if resolved: If low warming performance was caused by an external hose, blanket, intake, or filter-related airflow restriction that has been corrected, document and complete appropriate verification before return to service.

steps.title: Review Displayed Alarm or Error Information
steps.instructions: If the unit fails temperature verification, produces an alarm, shuts off the heater or fan, or cannot maintain stable output, record the displayed alarm message and available error information. Do not proceed into internal component troubleshooting as part of this guide. Expected outcome: The failure is documented clearly for bench evaluation or repair. Why it matters: The manufacturer’s troubleshooting path for heater failure or temperature accuracy failure moves from recorded error information to internal evaluation of the thermistor, heater circuitry, or power/control assemblies.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 produced airflow but would not reach or maintain the selected warming temperature during setup for patient use.
CCR.Cause: Found the unit failed controlled temperature verification at the heated settings after confirming proper power connection, clear air intake, serviceable filter status, intact hose, and correct WarmTouch blanket connection.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the WarmTouch WT 6000 from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented displayed behavior and test results, and routed the unit for bench evaluation of the temperature-control system.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested and power cord condition
Status LED color and operating mode observed
Selected temperature setting and displayed temperature behavior
Any alarm message or error information displayed
Hose, nozzle, and blanket connection condition
Confirmation that a WarmTouch blanket was used
Air intake condition and filter status
Airflow present, weak, intermittent, or absent
Measured output temperature at 34°C and 47°C settings
Whether the temperature stabilized within specification
Any unusual sounds, heat, or odor
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 Patient Warming System - Thermostat Open / Over-Temperature Alarm
description: Troubleshooting thermostat-open or over-temperature alarms involving airflow obstruction, blocked intake, hose restriction, filter condition, or internal temperature-control failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately discontinue use of the WarmTouch WT 6000 if a thermostat-open or over-temperature alarm occurs during patient warming. Move the patient to another approved warming system or alternate warming method according to clinical procedure before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient is no longer dependent on a warming unit with an unresolved heat-control alarm. Why it matters: The WT 6000 monitors delivered air temperature and shuts down the heater and fan when temperature exceeds its operating limit; the safety thermostat provides additional over-temperature protection.

steps.title: Verify and Document the Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: With the unit removed from clinical use, confirm the displayed alarm or error message. Document: Exact alarm text or error code; Temperature setting in use when the alarm occurred; Whether the unit was operating in Boost, High, Medium, Low, or Ambient mode; Whether the heater and fan stopped; Whether the alarm clears after the unit cools and is restarted. If the display reports Error Code 157, the service manual identifies this as Thermostat open. Expected outcome: The reported condition is accurately captured for troubleshooting and repair follow-up.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Excessive Heat or Damage
steps.instructions: Unplug the warming unit and allow it to cool before inspecting it. Check the exterior, power cord, hose, nozzle, and blanket connection area for: Unusual heat; Burnt odor; Melted, discolored, or damaged plastic; Cracked or crushed hose sections; Evidence of fluid intrusion. Expected outcome: No evidence of unsafe overheating or physical damage is present. If heat damage, burning odor, or melted components are found: Stop troubleshooting, label the unit Out of Service, and send it for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify the Power Source and Operating Environment
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is connected to a suitable hospital-grade grounded outlet. Verify that the unit was not being operated: In an unusually hot environment; Directly next to a heat source; With the housing obstructed by linens, drapes, equipment, or stored supplies; In a stacked or confined arrangement that could restrict airflow. Expected outcome: The unit has a proper power source and adequate clearance for normal air movement.

steps.title: Check the Air Intake and External Vent Areas
steps.instructions: Inspect the visible air intake and vent areas for lint, dust buildup, packaging material, linens, drapes, or other obstructions. Do not open the enclosure or bypass safety devices. Remove only external obstructions that can be safely cleared without disassembly. Expected outcome: External air intake and vent areas are unobstructed. Why it matters: Restricted airflow can contribute to abnormal temperature conditions in convective warming systems.

steps.title: Inspect the Hose and Nozzle
steps.instructions: Examine the warming hose from the unit to the nozzle. Confirm that the hose is: Fully extended enough for normal airflow; Not sharply kinked, crushed, collapsed, or pinched under equipment; Free of visible internal blockage at the nozzle; Properly attached and undamaged. Expected outcome: The hose and nozzle provide an unobstructed path for airflow. If a damaged or obstructed hose is found: Remove the affected accessory from use and retest only with an approved, intact component when permitted by facility procedure.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Blanket and Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm that an approved WarmTouch blanket was being used and that the nozzle was properly secured to the blanket inlet. Do not operate the warming unit with the hose blowing directly toward the patient or without the nozzle connected to the warming blanket. Expected outcome: Correct blanket use and proper nozzle attachment are confirmed. Why it matters: The manufacturer specifies use with WarmTouch blankets and warns against free-hosing because thermal injury may occur.

steps.title: Review Filter Status and Exterior Filter Condition
steps.instructions: Check for an onscreen filter expiration warning or documented overdue filter maintenance. Inspect only the externally accessible condition of the filter enclosure area for obvious blockage, heavy lint accumulation, or damage. Expected outcome: No external indication of overdue or restricted filter condition is present. If the filter is overdue, visibly restricted, or the unit indicates filter service is required: Remove the unit from service for maintenance according to the approved service process.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Functional Check Only If Safe
steps.instructions: Only after the unit has cooled, external obstructions have been ruled out, and no physical damage is present, perform a brief functional check off-patient according to department procedure. Use an approved blanket connection. Observe for: Normal startup; Normal airflow; Recurrence of the alarm; Heater or fan shutdown; Unusual odor, noise, or excessive heat. Expected outcome: The unit operates without recurrence of the thermostat-open or over-temperature alarm. If the alarm returns: Stop testing. The unit is not safe for clinical use and requires repair evaluation.

steps.title: Escalate Error Code 157 or Recurring Over-Temperature Conditions
steps.instructions: If Error Code 157 — Thermostat open is present, or the over-temperature alarm repeats after external airflow, hose, blanket, filter, and environmental checks are completed, do not continue clinical use. The WT 6000 service manual identifies Error Code 157 troubleshooting as evaluation of the internal thermistor position and, if required, replacement of the power PCBA. These are repair-level actions beyond basic external troubleshooting. Expected outcome: A suspected internal temperature-sensing or heater-control fault is appropriately escalated.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 alarmed for thermostat open/over-temperature during patient warming and stopped delivering warmed airflow.
CCR.Cause: Verified recurring thermostat-open alarm after confirming the hose, blanket connection, visible air intake, and operating environment were unobstructed, indicating a likely internal temperature-control fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the WarmTouch WT 6000 from service, applied an Out of Service label, documented the displayed alarm condition, and routed the unit for bench evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Exact displayed alarm text or error code, including Error Code 157 if present.
Temperature mode active when the alarm occurred.
Whether the unit had been operating in Boost mode.
Outlet tested and power cord condition.
Air intake or vent obstruction observed.
Unusual heat, odor, sound, or visible damage.
Whether the alarm recurred during off-patient functional check.

title: Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 Patient Warming System - Fan or Airflow Failure
description: Troubleshooting absent, weak, or abnormal airflow caused by hose, blanket, filter, power, obstruction, or fan-related faults.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
If the WarmTouch WT 6000 is being used for patient warming and airflow stops, becomes weak, or an alarm appears, discontinue use and provide alternate warming support according to clinical procedure.

Do not troubleshoot the unit while it is actively supporting a patient.

Expected outcome: The patient is supported by a known-working warming system before evaluation begins.

Why it matters: A sudden performance change requires discontinuation of use until the warming system can be evaluated by qualified personnel.

steps.title: 2. Confirm the Reported Airflow Problem
steps.instructions:
With the unit removed from clinical use, connect the hose nozzle to an appropriate WarmTouch blanket before operating the blower. Do not operate the unit by directing the hose alone toward a patient or test surface.

Power the unit on and select a treatment setting that should activate airflow. Observe whether:

The fan starts.
Air exits through the connected blanket.
Airflow is weak, intermittent, or absent.
The display shows an alarm, warning, or filter message.
The unit produces unusual noise, vibration, heat, or odor.

Expected outcome: The problem is confirmed under safe conditions and documented accurately.

If normal airflow is restored and no alarm or abnormal behavior is present, complete operational verification according to department procedure and return the unit to service only when safe. Stop troubleshooting.

Why it matters: In Standby and Ready modes, the fan is normally off; fan operation is expected in Treatment mode. The manufacturer also warns against free-hosing.

steps.title: 3. Verify Power and Operating Mode
steps.instructions:
Confirm that the unit is connected to a known-good hospital-grade grounded outlet and that the power cord is fully seated and undamaged.

Observe the status indication and display behavior:

In Standby mode, the display and fan may be off.
In Ready mode, the display is on, but the fan remains off.
In Treatment mode, the fan should operate.

Select the intended treatment mode and reassess airflow.

Expected outcome: The unit enters Treatment mode and produces airflow through the blanket.

If the fan begins operating normally after correcting the power source or mode selection, document the correction and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 4. Inspect the Hose and Nozzle Externally
steps.instructions:
Power the unit off and inspect the entire accessible hose and nozzle for:

Kinks, crushing, collapse, or severe twisting.
Tears, holes, separation, or obvious damage.
A loose or damaged nozzle.
Debris or blockage visible at the nozzle opening.

Reposition the hose so air can travel freely, reconnect it securely to the blanket, and retest in Treatment mode.

Expected outcome: The hose is unobstructed and airflow is restored or the symptom remains unchanged.

If correcting a kinked or obstructed hose resolves the problem, document the cause and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 5. Check the Connected Warming Blanket
steps.instructions:
Confirm that a compatible WarmTouch blanket is installed correctly and has not been compressed, folded excessively, blocked by equipment, or damaged in a way that could restrict airflow.

For troubleshooting off the patient, replace the suspected blanket with a new compatible WarmTouch blanket and repeat the airflow check.

Expected outcome: Airflow is normal through a known-good blanket.

If airflow returns with a replacement blanket, remove the defective or obstructed blanket from use, document the finding, and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 6. Inspect Accessible Air Intake and External Obstructions
steps.instructions:
With the unit powered off, inspect the exterior air-intake and filter enclosure area for visible lint, packaging material, drape material, dust accumulation, or other external obstruction.

Verify that the unit is not positioned where linens, walls, cart accessories, or other equipment are blocking air entry or discharge.

Do not operate the warming unit with the filter enclosure removed.

Expected outcome: The unit has unobstructed exterior airflow paths and produces normal airflow when retested.

If removing an external obstruction restores airflow, document the correction and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 7. Check for a Filter Warning or Overdue Filter Maintenance
steps.instructions:
Power the unit into Ready mode and check the display for a filter expiration or replacement warning.

The WarmTouch WT 6000 monitors filter usage, and the manufacturer specifies HEPA filter replacement every 2,000 operating hours or 365 days, whichever occurs first.

If the filter is expired or due for replacement, follow the approved Clinical Engineering maintenance procedure for filter replacement and required post-maintenance verification.

Expected outcome: The filter condition is verified, overdue maintenance is corrected when applicable, and airflow is reassessed.

If completing approved filter maintenance restores normal airflow and the unit passes required verification, document the work and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 8. Record Any Fan-Related Error Code
steps.instructions:
If the display indicates an alarm or fault, record the exact displayed code and behavior before removing power.

Fan-related fault descriptions identified in service documentation include:

Error 013: Fan motor drew too much current.
Error 014: Fan speed below 3000 rpm.
Error 015: Fan speed above 5000 rpm.
Error 016: Fan speed too low for current fan voltage.

Errors 013, 014, and 016 may be associated with fan blockage or fan failure; Error 015 may indicate a power-control issue. These findings require further bench evaluation rather than continued clinical use.

Expected outcome: The fault is identified well enough to support appropriate repair escalation.

steps.title: 9. Evaluate for Abnormal Fan Behavior
steps.instructions:
During a controlled off-patient test, listen for abnormal fan behavior such as:

Fan not starting in Treatment mode.
Intermittent airflow.
Grinding, scraping, rattling, or excessive vibration.
Repeated alarms during blower operation.
Burning smell or unusual heat.

Immediately discontinue testing if there is unusual heat, odor, smoke, electrical concern, or repeated fault behavior.

Expected outcome: The device either operates normally without abnormal signs or is clearly identified as requiring repair evaluation.

If the unit continues to demonstrate weak, absent, intermittent, or abnormal airflow, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 powered on but produced little to no airflow through the warming blanket.
CCR.Cause: Verified the unit entered Treatment mode, but airflow remained absent with a known-good hose position and replacement WarmTouch blanket; fan fault code 014 was displayed.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the warming unit from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the displayed fan-speed fault, and routed the unit for bench evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Exact alarm or error code displayed.
Unusual fan noise, vibration, heat, or odor.

title: Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 Patient Warming System - Keypad, display, or UI startup error
description: Troubleshooting an unresponsive keypad, blank or abnormal display, or startup/UI error preventing reliable warming unit operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately remove the WarmTouch WT 6000 from patient use if the keypad, display, or startup interface is not functioning normally. If warming is required, notify clinical staff and move the patient to a known-working warming device before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient is no longer dependent on a warming unit with unreliable controls or status information. Why it matters: A malfunctioning display or keypad may prevent confirmation of temperature settings, alarms, or safe operating status.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: With the unit removed from clinical use, inspect the front panel and document the symptom: Display remains blank after attempting to power on. Display illuminates but is distorted, incomplete, frozen, or shows abnormal colors. One or more keypad buttons do not respond. Unit displays a startup, POST, or UI-related error. Unit powers on but cannot be placed into a normal operating mode. Record any displayed alarm or error code exactly as shown. Expected outcome: The complaint is clearly identified as a keypad, display, or startup/UI problem rather than a general warming failure.

steps.title: Verify Normal Standby Versus a Blank-Display Failure
steps.instructions: Connect the unit to an appropriate grounded hospital-grade outlet and observe the status LED. On the WarmTouch WT 6000, Standby mode normally shows an amber status LED with the display blank. After the On/Standby key is pressed, Ready mode should show a green status LED with the display on. If the LED is amber and the display is blank, press the On/Standby key once. If the LED turns green and the display appears normally, the unit may have been in normal standby. Continue checking keypad response before returning it to use. If the LED is green but the display remains blank or abnormal, stop and proceed toward escalation. If no LED illuminates, evaluate the issue as a possible power-on failure rather than a display-only complaint. Expected outcome: Normal standby behavior is distinguished from a true display or startup failure.

steps.title: Check the Power Source and External Power Cord
steps.instructions: Inspect the power cord and external connections before suspecting an internal UI failure. Confirm the power cord is fully seated at the warming unit. Inspect for bent pins, damaged insulation, loose strain relief, fluid contamination, or signs of overheating. Test the outlet with another approved device or outlet tester according to department procedure. Try a known-good compatible power cord when available. Expected outcome: The unit powers into standby with an amber LED and then enters Ready mode with a functional display after pressing On/Standby. If resolved: Document the external power issue and complete operational verification before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Inspect the Keypad and Display Externally
steps.instructions: Examine the front panel without opening the unit. Check for: Cracked or peeling keypad membrane. Stuck, depressed, or damaged buttons. Dried fluid residue or cleaning solution around the keypad edges. Cracked display window or visible moisture behind the display. Signs of impact damage or excessive heat. Do not continue using the unit if the keypad appears physically stuck or the display cannot reliably communicate settings and alarms. Expected outcome: No visible external damage, contamination, or stuck key is present. If damage is found: Remove the unit from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Reattempt Startup Without Pressing Any Key During the Startup Sequence
steps.instructions: Power the unit off, wait briefly, then press the On/Standby key once and allow the startup sequence to complete without touching any additional buttons. A WarmTouch POST keypad error may occur when a key is pressed while the power-on self-test is running. The service documentation identifies error 133 as a keypad error during POST and indicates that repeated occurrence without a key being pressed suggests a keypad fault. Expected outcome: The unit completes startup normally, the display becomes readable, and all required keys respond appropriately. If resolved: Document that the startup error cleared after restarting without keypad input. Complete operational verification before return to use.

steps.title: Check for Electromagnetic Interference or Location-Related Behavior
steps.instructions: If the startup/UI issue is intermittent, evaluate whether it occurs only in one clinical location or near other powered equipment. Move the unit away from nearby devices with high electrical activity. Reposition external cabling so it is not bundled tightly with other powered equipment. Try a different approved outlet circuit in a safe evaluation area. The WarmTouch service documentation notes that certain communication-related faults may be associated with electromagnetic interference and recommends separating devices, repositioning cabling, or using separate outlet circuit branches. Expected outcome: The device starts and operates normally without display freezing, UI errors, or repeated startup alarms. If resolved: Document the location-related behavior and determine whether the unit requires further evaluation before clinical return.

steps.title: Verify Basic Control Response Only if the Unit Starts Normally
steps.instructions: If the display is readable and no error remains: Press each necessary front-panel control once to confirm response. Confirm the temperature setting displayed changes appropriately when commanded. Verify the unit reaches a normal ready state without repeating a keypad, display, or UI error. Observe for alarm tones, unexpected resets, or a frozen interface. Do not place the unit back into patient use solely because it powers on once after a reported UI failure. Repeated operation should be stable and controls must be dependable. Expected outcome: The display is clear, required keys respond consistently, and no startup/UI fault recurs. If resolved: Follow department policy for final functional verification and document the result.

steps.title: Capture Any Error Code Before Escalation
steps.instructions: If the unit shows a startup or UI-related error, document: Exact error code or alarm message. LED color at the time of failure. Whether the display was readable. Whether keys responded. Whether the error occurred during startup or after operating. Whether restarting without pressing keys changed the outcome. The WarmTouch service documentation associates multiple POST and UI faults with internal user-interface or communication failures that require further service evaluation rather than continued clinical use. Expected outcome: The repair technician or vendor receives meaningful failure information for bench diagnosis.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Medtronic WarmTouch WT 6000 powered on but the display remained blank and the keypad would not respond.
CCR.Cause: Verified appropriate outlet and known-good power cord; unit entered standby but failed to provide a functional display after power-on, indicating an unresolved display/UI failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed WarmTouch WT 6000 from service, labeled unit Out of Service, documented LED behavior and startup symptoms, and routed the device for bench evaluation.
